notes.Arrow of Acid: Acid surface gives a no-save -2 AC to whatever you hit, effectively free archery fighting style; damage is irrelevant but the to-hit boost is huge for Sharpshooter archers.
notes.Arrow of Fire: Fire surface deals a guaranteed 1d4 even on a miss and can stack in packed fights; a solid default but falls off as enemies gain fire resistance.
notes.Arrow of Ice: Ice surface forces a prone save that can burn whole enemy turns and break concentration, plus strong surface-combo synergies (steam, water, electrified).
notes.Arrow of Lightning: Identical to the other elemental arrows but creates no surface, so it's only a small chance at marginal damage - not worth stockpiling.
notes.Arrow of Beast Slaying: Doubles damage vs beasts, but only sold late in Act 1 after the hardest beast fights are over.
notes.Arrow of Construct Slaying: Great for the dangerous Act 3 construct fights, though piercing resistance and single-instance-damage mechanics blunt it somewhat.
notes.Arrow of Elemental Slaying: Sold from Act 2; misses the deadly early elementals but shines in a couple of dangerous late-game elemental encounters.
notes.Arrow of Fiend Slaying: Fiends are common and mostly filler, but the arrow is very useful in the few genuinely hard fiend fights.
notes.Arrow of Monstrosity Slaying: Monstrosities cluster in one Act 1 zone and are rarely threatening; usefulness depends entirely on play order.
notes.Arrow of Undead Slaying: Undead appear in huge numbers across all three acts including some of the hardest fights - grab as many as possible.
notes.Arrow of Aberration Slaying: Surprisingly few aberration fights, but they're high-priority targets in the most important encounters, so doubling damage matters.
notes.Arrow of Dragon Slaying: Only a handful of dragon fights, but they're among the deadliest, and you can stockpile enough to use exclusively against them.
notes.Arrow of Humanoid Slaying: About half the enemies in the game are humanoid, so this comes up constantly - stockpile for every party.
notes.Arrow of Darkness: Casts a 3-turn no-concentration Darkness as an attack action, breaking action economy; S+ for darkness/warlock builds, merely S otherwise.
notes.Arrow of Ilmater: Stops healing for a turn (good vs natural regenerators) plus a weak hit-only 1d4 necrotic; situational, occasionally backfires by denying the AI a wasted heal.
notes.Arrow of Roaring Thunder: No bonus damage, low strength-save knockback that enemies resist easily, and often forces you to forgo hitting an enemy to aim it.
notes.Arrow of Arcane Interference: Guaranteed no-save concentration break is decent (great for non-archers), but the intended silence barely functions due to BG3's duration timing.
notes.Arrow of Many Targets: Hits up to 4 targets with near-full damage each, multiplies crit chances and on-hit stacks (fastest Arcane Acuity stacking) - roughly 3.5x damage, wipes encounters.
notes.Arrow of Salving: Always-hit ally heal that removes poison/paralysis/blind as an attack - a rare but valuable panic button, especially for characters without lesser restoration.
notes.Arrow of Smokepowder: A free Fireball-on-an-arrow (8d6 split fire/force) plus knockback, no spell slot - a third-level spell as an attack action, massive damage.
notes.Arrow of Transposition: Free 60ft Misty Step as an attack; rarely your primary mobility (a bonus action is usually better) but great to keep one on every character.

notes.Firewine Barrel: The famous one: ignited it explodes for 4d6+18 fire (avg 32, more than Fireball) using your character's item save DC so it often lands full; broken into an alcohol surface it can instead be frozen into a huge crowd-control ice sheet — hugely versatile.
notes.Runepowder Barrel: Detonates for 10d8+40 force (avg 85) in a massive 59ft radius and auto-crits objects; only four exist and the radius makes in-combat use tricky, but AOE-spell/teleport/globe-of-invulnerability setups make the absurd damage well worth it.
notes.Runepowder Bomb: Functionally identical to the Runepowder Barrel (10d8+40 force, ~85 avg, 59ft radius); slightly shorter model can make line-of-sight to ignite it a touch harder.
notes.Smokepowder Barrel: Ignited gives a Fireball-equivalent 4d4+18 force (avg 28) PLUS a smoke powder surface that adds 2d6 fire/2d6 force and a no-save knockback — reposition enemies into pits or AOEs reliably; excellent damage and control.
notes.Water Barrel: A level-3 Create Water in a 20ft radius that makes everything Wet — double cold/lightning damage across a whole encounter, ice-surface freezes, and fire resistance for your own party; the broken Wet condition at huge scale.
notes.Smokepowder Satchel: Can be thrown intact then ignited (better positioning than barrels); 3d4+9 force (avg ~16) in a 13ft radius plus a smoke powder surface for extra damage and, crucially, a no-save knockback — marginal damage but powerful repositioning.
notes.Runepowder Vial: A unique satchel: throw-then-ignite for 5d4+18 force (avg ~30) in a 25ft radius (over 3x the deceptive on-screen indicator) plus a no-save knockback; bigger than the Smoke Powder Satchel but the larger radius makes knockback placement less precise.
notes.Oil Barrel: Throw/break for a flammable oil surface, or ignite for 6d6 fire (DC12 dex save for half) plus burning in a 20ft radius; decent damage but less and less versatile than the Firewine Barrel (no ice option), and finicky to set up.
notes.Nautiloid Tank: Functionally identical to the Oil Barrel (6d6 fire ignition, flammable surface) but weighs less, so it's a bit easier to lug around.
notes.Box of Fireworks: The tooltip promises an 8d6 ranged firework blast but that activation is bugged and does nothing; you can still drop and ignite it for a decent Fireball-sized explosion — keep it in case a patch makes it work as intended.
notes.Box of Oddfire Fireworks: Functionally identical to the Box of Fireworks (same bugged 8d6 firework, same drop-and-ignite explosion); very slightly taller model makes it marginally easier to hit over a ledge.
notes.Surprise-Based Teddy Bear: Like the Oil Barrel (6d6 fire) but with a tougher DC15 save and, unlike the barrels, it can be thrown then ignited from range — a bit less damage overall but easier to land and set up.
notes.Brine Barrel: Throw/break for a 20ft caustic brine surface dealing a guaranteed 1d4/turn; no-save damage that can finish low-HP groups and is non-embarrassing in the early game, but only worth it if you have the carry weight to spare.
notes.Popdrake: A firework that travels in a straight line for 3d6 fire (dex save for half, your item DC) in a small AOE; its color spray rider is bugged to zero duration, leaving essentially a scroll of Chromatic Orb without the surface — a fine pinch-hit, not exciting.
notes.Squidjins: Functionally identical to Popdrake — 3d6 fire in a small straight-line AOE with the same bugged, useless color spray rider.
notes.Wogglims: Functionally identical to Popdrake and Squidjins — 3d6 fire straight-line firework with the same broken color spray effect.
notes.Acid Barrel: Throw/break for a 20ft no-save -2 AC acid surface that can't be converted to other surfaces; spending an action just to make attacks slightly easier (like True Strike) is almost never worth it — only useful if caught in an AOE next to an enemy for free.

notes.Boots of Aid and Comfort: Healing a target also grants them 3 temp HP; weak alone but a core piece of the on-heal trio with Hellrider's Pride and Whispering Promise (Bless + Blade Ward + temp HP), and obtainable early — pre-buff the whole party by throwing a potion.
notes.Boots of Speed: Bonus-action double movement every turn — a ton of mobility for any character without a better bonus-action use, great for closing distance or escaping a losing fight; slightly undercut by bonus-action jump and the Night Walkers, but you get it first.
notes.Boots of Stormy Clamour: Inflicting any condition also applies 2 turns of Reverberation (-1 physical saves/stack, prone at 5 stacks); reverberation is a top-tier broken mechanic and these are the best way to stack it — radiating-orb clerics and tiger-heart barbarians abuse it, but anyone benefits.
notes.Disintegrating Night Walkers: Misty Step once per short rest (the best mobility tool) plus immunity to web/entangle/ensnare and no slipping on grease/ice — everything you want from boots in one package; arguably the single most-equipped item in the game.
notes.Vital Conduit Boots: 8 temp HP whenever you cast a concentration spell (even a cantrip); S-tier for Spore Druids, where a bug lets it fully restore the Spore shield for effectively infinite temp HP, and A-tier for other medium-armor casters.
notes.Evasive Shoes: +1 acrobatics and +1 AC — a rare flat passive stat boost from the boot slot, so many parties equip it on a character lacking a specific boot need; that +1 AC can lift a low-AC monk/light-armor character to stop dragging down party defense.
notes.Boots of Uninhibited Kushigo: Adds your Wisdom modifier to unarmed-attack damage — best-in-slot for every monk (doubling Wis-to-damage on an open-hand monk), a huge per-attack boost on a class that attacks many times a turn.
notes.Helldusk Boots: Force-movement immunity, ignore difficult terrain, a bonus-action short-rest Misty Step with a no-save 28 fire explosion, AND once/rest auto-succeed a failed save — an incredible all-in-one defensive package, best-in-slot endgame boots.
notes.Boots of Striding: One turn of momentum on casting a concentration spell, but the real draw is immunity to prone/push while concentrating — preventing the no-save concentration loss from being knocked over; great for Druid/cleric casters, though defense-only.
notes.Linebreaker Boots: 3 turns of Wrath (+1 melee damage/stack, up to +7) when you dash in combat; needs bonus-action dash and melee weapons, so it shines on dagger Rogues and Stallion-barbarian/Rogue builds that dash to close anyway.
notes.The Watersparkers: Standing in water electrifies it and grants 3 lightning charges/turn — combined with the Spark Swall ring (immunity to the shock) it's free, constant charge generation plus an area the AI avoids; the core item for any lightning-charge build.
notes.Bonespike Boots: +5ft jump, +1 AC/saves while unarmored/shieldless, and a bonus-action Brutal Leap that knocks enemies prone for two turns (item save DC); excellent for unarmored Barbarians and defensive monks who want to leap onto enemies.
notes.Boots of Psionic Movement: Fly as a bonus action once per long rest (works for any class, not just gith) — a strong, slot-free, flexible mobility option and an interesting alternative to the Night Walkers for parties that aren't going full-illithid.
notes.Boots of Elemental Momentum: Momentum (2 turns, +movement) when you deal elemental spell damage; good mobility, but restricted to medium-armor casters (Tempest cleric, Druid, gith) and it wants you to lead with a damage spell, which isn't always ideal.
notes.Hoarfrost Shoes: Can't fall prone on icy terrain — passive (unlike Boots of Striding) so it works for non-casters, but it doesn't stop enemy-forced prone; only worth it for an ice-surface party already running something like the Snowburst Ring.
notes.Springstep Boots: 3 turns of momentum (+15ft movement) when you dash; decent extra mobility, but characters who bonus-action dash usually prefer the Linebreaker Boots' Wrath, since you're already moving far on a dash turn.
notes.The Speedy Lightfeet: 3 lightning charges on dash (medium-armor); both halves are outclassed — Linebreaker Boots give better dash damage and the Watersparkers stack charges more easily — but useful for a ranged lightning-charge build (charges work on ranged attacks, Wrath doesn't).
notes.Boots of Brilliance: Regain one Bardic Inspiration charge once per long rest; a useful resource for Bards, but it takes fiddly swap-in/swap-out micro to maximize, so most won't bother.
notes.Boots of Persistence: Longstrider plus Freedom of Movement (difficult-terrain immunity and, crucially, immunity to paralysis and restrained); a fine peace-of-mind swap-in for hold-person fights, rolling several weak effects into one decent package.
notes.Boots of Very Fast Blinking: At-will Misty Step (once/turn) with a brutal catch: you drop your armor, helmet, gloves, and cloak where you blink from; reasonable for unarmored Barbarians/monks and for infinite out-of-combat traversal if you tolerate re-equipping micro.
notes.Spacehunt Boots: Dimension Door once per long rest; a relatively weak spell normally, but free it's a useful swap-in for late-game encounters where teleporting yourself (and allies) around matters.
notes.Cinder Shoes: 2 turns of Heat when you burn an enemy, discharged for +1 fire damage/stack in an AOE; only relevant for a build centered on burning enemies (Alchemist's Fire / fire arrows), and Heat is a weak mechanic — mostly ignorable.
notes.Mystra's Grace: Feather Fall at will; most parties already have it as a ritual spell, so it's only handy as a slot-free in-combat cast or for a party lacking the spell — easily duplicated by scrolls.
notes.Spiderstep Boots: Immunity to webbed and no slowing on web surfaces; entirely encounter-dependent, but you conveniently get them right before one of the few web-heavy fights — wear them there, then swap out.
notes.Swiresy Shoes: +1 acrobatics and +5ft jump distance; a passive mobility bump (tripled by Enhanced Leap) that helps low-strength characters cross gaps, but it doubles down on jumping, which most characters already use for mobility.
notes.Varsh Ko'kuu's Boots: Acid-surface immunity and acid resistance — a relatively rare resistance, so a fine swap-in for the few acid-themed encounters, but otherwise covered by elixirs/protection spells.
notes.Boots of Apparent Death: Feign Death once per short rest; a terrible spell in normal combat, but useful in a couple encounters to keep an allied NPC out of a fight, and a bug lets you knock out and rob merchants without triggering crime.
notes.Boots of Arcane Bolstering: 2 turns of Arcane Charge (+2 spell damage vs threatened enemies) on dash; useless for most casters (who won't dash), but works for a Magic Missile evocation-wizard/Rogue and features in theoretical max-damage Spike Growth combos.
notes.Tyrannical Jackboots: +1 to Charisma saves (almost never rolled) and Charisma checks; only useful as a swap-in to pass a specific late-game dialogue check from a slot that can't otherwise boost it — more micro than most will do.
notes.Leather Boots: Non-magical boots with no mechanical effect — safely ignored.
notes.Metallic Boots: Non-magical, +1 Athletics (medium-armor); a negligible bonus worth wearing only before you find any real boots — safe to ignore.
notes.Boots of Genial Striding: Immunity to being slowed by difficult terrain (not its damage); difficult terrain is already trivialized by bonus-action jump in BG3, so this rarely matters and better options exist if you want to walk through Plant Growth.
notes.Acrobat Shoes: +1 acrobatics and advantage on Dex saves; Dex saves are usually the least threatening (mostly half-damage effects) and you can get this advantage elsewhere — not worth a whole boot slot over mobility/offense.
notes.Dark Justiciar Boots: +1 Dex saves and an action-cost, once/rest 60ft teleport to an obscured area; strictly worse than the Night Walkers (bonus action, no restrictions), and spending an action to teleport is rarely worth it.
notes.Blackguard's Greaves: +1 Athletics and Longstrider (medium-armor); Longstrider is a ritual spell your party should already have and these don't stack with it, so they're just a worse mobility option — ignorable.
notes.Slinging Shoes: A joke returning-weapon: thrown, they deal +2d4 psychic but only 1 base damage (boots are light), so even a throwing build does far less than with a real thrown weapon — funny, not good.
notes.Unwanted Masterwork Greaves: Mechanically identical to the Metallic Boots (+1 Athletics) but flagged rare/magical — no reason to pick them up.

tier_definitions.S: Always take if you have access
tier_definitions.A: Strong, great use of a turn
tier_definitions.B: Useful, situationally excellent or solid overall

notes.Eldritch Blast: The gold-standard damage cantrip: 1d10 force (rarely resisted) that gains extra ATTACKS (not dice) as you level — more crit chance, tighter damage spread, and every rider/Agonizing Blast bonus applies per beam; the baseline every damage build is measured against.
notes.Guidance: +1d4 to any skill check, usable reactively — basically free expertise on every check, all game; arguably the single best cantrip (maybe best spell) in the game. Every character who can take it should.
notes.Mage Hand: An invisible hand that interacts with objects and THROWS items — pre-summon it, then have it lob potions/bombs at allies or enemies for free (no action), all day; also baits see-invisible enemies into wasting turns. Fiddly, so A rather than S.
notes.Minor Illusion: Lures enemies to look at/walk to a point — group them for a Fireball, break their line of sight for a stealth opener, or pull them next to a chasm/hazard; often worth 100 damage or a free surprise round. Every party wants one.
notes.Blade Ward: A turn for two turns of halved physical damage — break-even on its own, but it DOUBLES temp HP (Armour of Agathys) and stacks multiplicatively with other damage reduction, making it very strong in those builds plus a survival button.
notes.Bone Chill: 1d8 necrotic at 60ft that, with no save, stops the target healing for a round and gives undead disadvantage on ALL their attacks — excellent vs healers/regenerators (vampires); one of the two best damage cantrips.
notes.Ray of Frost: 1d8 cold (rarely resisted in BG3) at 60ft that, no save, slows by 10ft — lets you kite enemies forever and freeze water into prone-inducing, concentration-breaking ice surfaces; the other top damage cantrip alongside Bone Chill.
notes.Produce Flame: Rolls a (slightly weaker) Firebolt and a Light source into one cantrip; the versatility of two useful effects in a single limited slot makes it a strong pick, especially for Druids with otherwise weak cantrips.
notes.Firebolt: The highest raw-damage cantrip for non-warlocks (1d10, scaling) and it ignites surfaces, but fire is commonly resisted and it has no rider — so the 60ft cantrips with utility (Bone Chill/Ray of Frost) edge it out.
notes.Friends: Advantage on a Charisma check, but it turns the target hostile afterward (crime in town) — so save it for important persuasion checks against people you'll fight anyway; rare but high-impact.
notes.Light: Lights an ally/object for a whole long rest (no concentration); you won't cast it often and it's duplicable (torch, glowing item), but it's very handy in the dark Act 2 encounters — most parties want one somewhere.
notes.Resistance: Guidance's combat cousin: +1d4 to saves for 10 turns, but it takes concentration (contested in combat) and saves happen mid-fight — fine as a pre-buff or for dialogue saves, just less reliably useful than Guidance.
notes.Sacred Flame: The cleric's 1d8 radiant damage cantrip (dex save, no rider); radiant is a great type many key enemies are weak to, and having a save-based option alongside a crossbow is nice — solid but pure damage.
notes.Thaumaturgy: Advantage on Intimidation/Performance with NO downside (unlike Friends); those checks are rarer, but a one-level cleric dip to grab it makes a Bard/Sorcerer/Warlock land intimidation checks very reliably.
notes.Thorn Whip: 1d6 at 30ft that pulls an enemy 10ft with no save — situationally huge (yank a foe off a cliff or into an AOE), and bumped up because it's one of the better picks for Druids' otherwise weak cantrip list.
notes.Dancing Lights: A 30ft lit radius but it costs concentration and lasts only 10 turns — outclassed by Light (all day, no concentration) or just throwing a torch.
notes.Shillelagh: Makes a club/staff use your Wisdom for d8 attacks (great with a torch for d8+1d4 fire), but it's really only relevant at level 1 — past level 2 a melee Druid just wild-shapes instead, and it locks you into limited weapons.
notes.Shocking Grasp: Melee 1d8 lightning that removes the target's reaction (escape melee, stop a Counterspell); useful effect, but you rarely want your caster in melee, and its real value comes from the War Caster feat, not the cantrip pick.
notes.Vicious Mockery: The Bard's only damage cantrip — weakest die (1d4 psychic, often resisted/immune) but disadvantage on the target's next attack; pick your spots (esp. with Cutting Words to deny a hit), otherwise a crossbow does more.
notes.Acid Splash: The only AOE cantrip, but it targets the weak Dex save (negates all damage on success), does the least reliable damage, and has no utility — almost always beaten by another damage cantrip.
notes.Poison Spray: Highest die (1d12) but only 10ft range, targets the strong Constitution save, and poison is one of the most-resisted/immune damage types — a bad damage cantrip even for the Druids who get it.
notes.True Strike: Advantage on your next attack, costing concentration — strictly worse than just attacking twice (and you can usually get advantage by hiding); the lone niche is a pre-combat cast when you can't hide. Not useless, but close.

notes.Deathstalker Mantle: Dark Urge-only and the only magical cloak in Act 1: turn invisible for two turns on a kill (once/turn) with no action cost — incredible survivability for squishy melee/solo builds. (Won't trigger on indirect kills like barrels or Moonbeam.)
notes.Cloak of Protection: +1 AC and +1 to all saves, permanently — small numbers but universally valuable, and each point compounds; someone in your party wears this all game.
notes.Cloak of Displacement: Disadvantage on attacks against you at the start of every turn (no cooldown) until you take damage; makes high-AC characters near-untouchable and redirects enemy AI away from squishy ones — featured in ~100% of parties.
notes.Cloak of the Weave: +1 spell save DC and spell attack rolls (one of the most valuable caster bonuses) plus a once-per-rest half-Elemental-damage reaction — best-in-slot endgame cloak for casters.
notes.Cloak of Cunning Brume: Casts a free Fog Cloud whenever you disengage; with bonus-action disengage (Rogue) it's a free spell that self-triggers Sneak Attack via blind, plus huge out-of-combat crime utility — high ceiling for the builds that can exploit it.
notes.Vivacious Cloak: 8 temp HP whenever you cast a spell in melee (even a cantrip) — a solid survival cushion for the spellcaster who can't wear the single Cloak of Protection; also helps refresh a Spore Druid's shield.
notes.Mantle of the Holy Warrior: Crusader's Mantle once per short rest (+1d4 radiant on all attacks, party-wide) without spending a slot; great for attack-heavy or summon-heavy parties — far better than paying a level-3 slot for a mediocre spell.
notes.Shade-Slayer Cloak: Lowers your crit threshold by 1 while hiding — excellent for stealth archers (who stay hidden) and a strong opener for any Rogue/Paladin crit build, though situational since most attacks break hiding.
notes.Cloak of Elemental Absorption: Once-per-rest reaction to halve incoming elemental damage plus +1d6 elemental on your next weapon hit; a fine panic button for an unexpected big hit, but reactive and often replaceable with gear/elixir resistance.
notes.Fleshmelter Cloak: 1d4 acid to enemies who deal you melee hit-point damage; free retaliation value on builds that expect to be hit, though annoyingly it won't trigger if Arcane Ward absorbs the damage.
notes.Nymph Cloak: Dominate Person once per long rest — a powerful spell when it lands, but it uses your weak item save DC and competes with the Cloak of the Weave/Displacement, so it's a situational swap-in for humanoid-heavy fights.
notes.Derivation Cloak: Heal 1d4 when you apply Poisoned; nearly nothing on its own, and even the three-item poison combo (with Brood Mother's Revenge + Poisoner's Gloves) is only okay for the slots it costs — playable but not optimized.
notes.Reverse Rain Cloak: Permanently Wet: fire resistance/no burning, but vulnerability to cold and lightning (and vulnerability hurts more than resistance helps); mostly a niche combo with the Pyroquickness Hat to stop a fire sorcerer self-immolating.
notes.Cloak: The non-magical cloak has no effect and no associated story event — pure Gale food.
notes.Thunderskin Cloak: Enemies with reverberation that damage you must pass a DC13 con save (unboostable, a bad save to target) or be Dazed; far too many specific prerequisites for a minor, reactive effect that almost never fires.
notes.Cindermoth Cloak: Enemies who damage you within 7ft make a trivial DC12 dex save or catch fire (1d4/turn) — essentially a strictly worse Fleshmelter Cloak, which deals its damage immediately with no save.
notes.Wavemother's Cloak: Grants the Wet condition but only in combat — a strictly worse Reverse Rain Cloak (can't lead off combat fire-immune), and the game pointlessly has two permanent-wetness cloaks; only worth it for the joke.

subject: Robes, clothes, and cloth armor
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, armor part 1

notes.Graceful Cloth: +2 Dexterity (the best stat: AC, initiative, attack/damage) plus advantage on Dex saves and +5ft jump — the author calls it the single strongest item on the whole list, best-in-slot for many party members all game.
notes.Protecty Sparkswall: +1 spell save DC (the number-one caster stat) on the earliest robe that grants it, plus a minor lightning-charge AC/save bonus; an unassuming Act 1 item that stays best-in-slot for robe-wearing casters through Acts 1-2.
notes.Potent Robe: +1 AC, adds your Charisma modifier to cantrip damage (multiplied across Eldritch Blast beams), and grants Charisma-modifier temp HP each turn — best-in-slot for warlocks and great on any caster; every ability is excellent.
notes.Bonespike Garb: 15 temp HP on entering rage, -2 incoming damage, and Con-modifier piercing retaliation; superb on Barbarians and even better on an abjuration wizard (the damage reduction stacks nonlinearly with Arcane Ward) — the best default cloth pick.
notes.Robe of the Weave: +1 spell save DC and spell attack rolls plus +2 AC (the highest from a cloth set) — a strict upgrade over the Protecty Sparkswall and the best-in-slot Act 3 robe for wizards and sorcerers.
notes.Vest of Soul Rejuvenation: +2 AC (one of only two cloth sets that grant it), 1d4 heal on a spell save, and a reaction unarmed strike when missed — best-in-slot for monks, though somewhat S-by-default since late-game monk clothing is thin.
notes.Infernal Robe: +1 AC, fire resistance, and Fire Shield (warm, also granting cold resistance) once/rest — one of the earliest robes that raises AC with two relevant defensive abilities, but story-locked and defense-focused when casters usually want offense.
notes.Enraging Heart Garb: +2 Constitution (more HP and better concentration saves, especially valuable for casters); its Wrath-on-rage ability is bugged and does nothing, but the passive +2 Con alone makes it a solid defensive pick.
notes.Icebite Robe: Cold resistance plus Armour of Agathys at level 3 once/rest (15 temp HP + 15 cold retaliation) that you can cast then unequip — effectively a free 3rd-level Agathys scroll daily, great for abjuration wizards or anyone wanting temp HP.
notes.Moon Devotion Robe: +1 to all saves, advantage on Con saves, free Mage Armor, and at-will Produce Flame with radiant retaliation on saves — probably the best purely-defensive robe in Act 2 for casters maintaining concentration.
notes.Garb of the Land and Sky: +1 AC, Blade Ward while using Patient Defense, and (via a bug) advantage + 1d8 force on all attacks the turn you use Step of the Wind — a nice monk bonus on a move you'd make anyway, though it costs a Flurry of Blows.
notes.Bloodguzzler Garb: Two stacks of Wrath (+1 melee damage/turn, up to +7) whenever an enemy damages you — powerful for multi-attack melee, but you must get hit, it doesn't work on unarmed strikes, and better Wrath sources exist; a build-around item.
notes.Bided Time: Arcane Charge (+2 spell damage on threatened enemies) when struck by a melee attack; great for an abjuration wizard's Magic Missile/Scorching Ray, but unreliable to trigger for anyone who isn't deliberately eating hits.
notes.Mighty Cloth: +2 Strength (the Graceful Cloth's strength counterpart) plus advantage on Str checks and a push ability; a top item if not for the Giant Strength elixirs, which set strength higher and free the slot — so only for non-elixir strength builds.
notes.Robe of Exquisite Focus: +1 spell save DC, but it arrives much later than the strictly-better Protecty Sparkswall and you'll have other save-DC items by then — only used if you run two casters who both want save DC in the chest slot.
notes.Mutilated Carapace: +1 attack/damage while shapeshifted or disguised, +1 AC, and advantage on Deception/Persuasion; a fine multi-attack bonus (esp. Eldritch Blast) but you must stay disguised, and the abilities don't synergize into anything exciting.
notes.Robe of Supreme Defences: Adds your spellcasting modifier to ALL saves while concentrating (so you basically never fail) plus +1 AC — the best defensive robe in Act 3, but defense is the wrong direction by then, so it's rarely needed.
notes.Wavemother's Robe: Fire AND cold resistance, 1d4 heal/turn while standing in water, a 4th-level Create/Destroy Water once/rest, and +1 AC — a cohesive bundle of defensive options to swap in for elemental fights or to set up water synergies.
notes.Armour of Uninhibited Kushigo: Monk-only: an unarmed strike against attackers who miss you while in Patient Defense — but monks have low AC (few misses), rarely want to use Patient Defense, and it does nothing for other classes; a weak build-around at best.
notes.Obsidian Laced Robe: Fire resistance (the most common damage type, with many single-type fire encounters) plus minor fire retaliation on spell saves — a reasonable swap-in for a known fire fight, but it costs your whole chest slot.
notes.Cloth of Authority: +1 AC, advantage on Intimidation and the otherwise-unboostable Insight checks, and fear immunity; each ability is just okay and easily duplicated, so the bundle never makes a powerful item — mainly for the Insight advantage if you lack fear immunity.
notes.Drunken Cloth: While drunk: advantage on Con saves and Con-modifier temp HP each turn — useful for a wall-of-fire evocation wizard taking constant chip damage, but situational, defense-only, and it costs a bonus action/pre-combat drink.
notes.Shelter of Athkatla: Advantage on saves vs spells plus Mirror Image once/rest; the Robe of Supreme Defences far outclasses the save bonus, and Mirror Image is easy to get from scrolls, so the item-swapping hassle isn't worth it.
notes.Poisoner's Robe: +1d4 on poison-damage spells, but almost no spells deal poison damage (mostly just Ray of Sickness and Cloud Kill), so even a Cloud Kill build would rather have save DC or initiative — no scenario where it beats an alternative.
notes.Robe of Summer: Just cold resistance, an effect that's narrow (mostly enemy-caster damage), better gained from elixirs/spells, and costs a whole gear slot to swap in — it looks great, which is the only reason to wear it.
notes.Cloth Armour: An Easter-egg/cut-content broken Bloodguzzler Garb (same Wrath-on-damage effect) found in Act 3; that effect was mediocre in Act 1 and is far worse this late.
notes.Veil of the Morning: Turn Undead once/rest (always uses Wisdom, so weak for non-Wis casters) plus undead disadvantage on attacks against you and advantage on saves vs them; only ~1.5 better than +2 AC even in undead fights, and purely defensive/situational.
notes.Viconia's Priestess Robe: +1 AC, advantage on stealth while obscured, and a beefed-up Shield of Faith — but Shield of Faith is a garbage concentration pick this late, and the Graceful Cloth does everything here far better; weak and uncohesive.

notes.Battlemage's Power: Locks in three stacks of Arcane Acuity all day (+3 to hit and +3 spell save DC, like +6 casting stat) that can't drop below three; trivially stacks enemies out of ever saving against your crowd control, breaking bounded accuracy.
notes.Hill Giant Strength: Sets Strength to 21, so drinking it daily on an 8-STR character gives free 20 Strength from level 1 — effectively two extra feats and a pile of attribute points, an egregious balance error that powers most broken builds.
notes.Cloud Giant Strength: Sets Strength to 27 (+3 to attack and damage) and breaks the attribute cap so even dex/finesse builds gain from it; drunk daily it's the same free-stats abuse as Hill Giant, just acquired later.
notes.Vigilance: Free Alert feat: +5 initiative (worth ~5x a tabletop point since BG3 rolls initiative on a d4) plus surprise immunity, so you go first in nearly every fight and shut enemies down before they act.
notes.Bloodlust: Grants an extra action and 5 temp HP on every kill; even with honour mode denying extra-attack scaling, one bonus attack or full spell per kill is enormous action economy that almost any damage dealer can exploit.
notes.Supreme Arcane Cultivation: Free level-4 spell slot daily — strong day-long buffs (summons, upcast Aid for +15 party HP) and extra game-winning casts like 4th-level Spirit Guardians; buyable from any elixir merchant at level 9.
notes.Colossus: Enlarge in a bottle: +1d4 damage on every attack plus battlefield-control from the size increase and advantage on Athletics shoves; a generically strong, somewhat overlooked offensive option.
notes.Heroism: Permanent stacking Bless (works alongside the actual spell) for +1d4 to attacks and saves all day, plus 10 temp HP; great on dex characters, just edged out of S because most builds prefer Bloodlust.
notes.Viciousness: Lowers your crit threshold by 1 and stacks with every other crit source; a minor bonus generally, but builds that care about crits (brutal-critical Barbarian, Smite Paladin, sneak-attack Rogue, GOO warlock fear) get huge value.
notes.Superior Arcane Cultivation: Free level-3 spell slot — the level where the best spells live (haste, hypnotic pattern, hunger of Hadar), often granting a whole extra fight per day; better than the lower tiers but still a minor benefit overall.
notes.Guileful Movement: Day-long Freedom of Movement: immune to difficult terrain, paralysis and restraint (counters Hold Person/Monster), but lacks the spell's stun-break panic button; solid defensive value that rarely beats an offensive elixir.
notes.Peerless Focus: Advantage on concentration saves (a free War Caster feat) plus charm/sleep protection; genuinely useful for support casters maintaining haste, but you'll usually want an offensive elixir instead.
notes.Resistance: Halves one chosen damage type and grants immunity to its condition; fight-winning against a damage-themed enemy, but it's purely defensive, demands prior knowledge of the fight, and is often available cheaper from other sources.
notes.Arcane Cultivation: Free level-1 spell slot — best used for a free Mage Armor cast or a Paladin Smite, but an extra level-1 spell is never a meaningful boost.
notes.Greater Arcane Cultivation: Free level-2 spell slot (good for Aid or an extra Cloud of Daggers); more than twice as good as the level-1 version but still too minor to leave C tier.
notes.Barkskin: Sets AC to a minimum of 16 without needing concentration, but doesn't stack with other AC and 16 is low, so only low-AC wildshape Druids benefit — and even they have better elixirs.
notes.Darkvision: Day-long 40ft darkvision, which is nearly useless in BG3 since arenas are lit or easily lit and the lighting engine is inconsistent; not harmful so not F, but a waste of clicks — sell it.
notes.See Invisibility: Forces nearby invisible enemies to save (using the weak item save DC) or be revealed; a joke effect you can replicate for free with create water, so better sold than used.
notes.Tadpole Elixir: Advantage on all attacks after using a tadpole power, but at the cost of disadvantage on Con/Wis/Int saves all rest — crippling the game's most important saves; Heroism or the risky ring give similar upside without the downside.

subject: Finesse weapons (daggers, shortswords, scimitars)
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, rogue weapons part 2

notes.+1 Shortsword: A +1 shortsword (best base finesse weapon — d6 + the Piercing Strike action) buyable from the very start; +1 to hit/damage matters a lot for the many-attacks builds that dual-wield, so grab one (ideally two) ASAP.
notes.Steelforged Sword: Just a +1 shortsword with a guaranteed early spawn (Blighted Village) — same value as any +1 shortsword, with the bonus of not relying on a merchant roll.
notes.Knife of the Undermountain King: A +2 shortsword whose passives work even from the unequipped slot: -1 to your crit threshold on ALL attacks (incl. bows/spells), rerolls low damage dice, and advantage vs obscured targets — best-in-slot stat stick for crit builds and archers all game.
notes.Shortsword of First Blood: A no-enhancement shortsword that adds +1d8 vs full-HP enemies — far more than a +1; attack first (or as an Assassin for a guaranteed crit) to double it. Best-in-slot damage for dual-wielders alongside the Knife.
notes.Justiciar's Scimitar: A +2 scimitar with Back Breaker (prone), a once/rest Shadow-Soaked Blow (+prof +1d6 psychic that doesn't break stealth), and a passive blind-on-advantage — a great damage + free-blind package for scimitar users.
notes.Render of Body and Mind: A +1 shortsword dealing +1d8 psychic whenever you attack with advantage — with the Risky Ring / Gauntlets of the Automaton you trigger it constantly, making it the best-in-slot damage weapon (vs non-psychic-immune enemies) alongside the Knife.
notes.Sword of Life Stealing: A +2 shortsword that deals +10 necrotic and grants 10 temp HP on a crit — and these builds crit a lot (esp. with the Knife); the temp HP suits squishy dual-wielders, a huge early-Act-2 upgrade.
notes.Ambusher: A +1 shortsword stat stick: passive +1 initiative (works from any slot), advantage on perception, and +1d6 necrotic vs enemies that haven't acted — free value for archers/casters, and big burst for Assassins (doubled on the guaranteed opening crit).
notes.Bloodthirst: A legendary +2 dagger: improved crit in either hand (stacks with the Knife), and in the main hand applies no-save piercing VULNERABILITY (doubles all your piercing) — broken whether wielded or used as an archer's crit/AC stat stick; pairs with Crimson Mischief.
notes.Crimson Mischief: A legendary +2 shortsword: +1d4 necrotic, +1d4 vs sub-50% enemies, and +7 on advantage attacks (main hand) or +ability-mod (off hand) — one of the highest damage outputs in the game, the intended partner to Bloodthirst.
notes.Rhapsody: A +1 dagger granting a permanent stacking +1/+2/+3 to attack, damage, AND spell save DC on kills (consumable for a guaranteed crit/heal) — a game-warping free stat stick, best on an archer or caster who isn't even using a dagger.
notes.Shortsword: The non-magical d6 with Piercing Strike (better than the scimitar's Lacerate); worth picking up two in the very early game to arm a dual-wielder before magical weapons appear.
notes.+1 Scimitar: Identical to a +1 shortsword but with the weaker Lacerate action instead of Piercing Strike — buy whichever the merchant rolls first; +1 to hit/damage is great for many-attack builds.
notes.Hunter's Dagger: A +1 dagger that (via a bug) always applies Ruptured on hit regardless of the save — an easy condition to feed condition-synergy items, plus chip damage when foes move; a cool alternative to a plain +1, though the Knife/render out-damage it.
notes.Sussur Dagger: A +1 dagger that silences on a con save in melee — devastating to enemy casters and to bosses whose legendary actions need speech; the best of the three Sussur weapons since you keep a strong second weapon. Swap in for the fights it matters.
notes.The Baneful: Always a +1 shortsword, and a +2 with no-save Bane on hit when bound as a pact/Eldritch Knight weapon — potentially the best weapon for a Dexterity Eldritch Knight or pact-of-the-blade warlock, with a debuff that also helps land their spells.
notes.Ritual Dagger of Shar: A +1 dagger with +1d4 necrotic on every attack — extra damage dice are always strong (scale with crits/bonus attacks), and it doubles as a solid backup throwing weapon; a touch below the top damage weapons but broadly useful.
notes.Thorn Blade: A +1 scimitar that, while you concentrate on a spell, adds +1d4 poison to ALL your melee attacks (incl. your main hand); acquirable in multiples that buff each other, so a concentrating character can stack a lot of extra damage.
notes.Dolor Amarus: A +2 dagger adding a flat +7 on any crit (works from the unequipped slot, even with bows/unarmed) — excellent on builds that GUARANTEE crits (Assassins, hold-person setups), though most prefer raw crit-chance items like the Knife.
notes.Dagger: The lowest-damage weapon (d4), so rarely equipped — but the non-magical dagger is a valuable thrown-weapon stockpile for throwers (full weapon damage when thrown, and a backup if your returning weapon bugs out).
notes.Speedy Reply: A no-enhancement scimitar granting momentum (extra movement) on hit; mobility is good but less useful after you've already closed, and it's lower damage than the +1s — a fine early find for a momentum/kiting build.
notes.Worgfang: A d4 dagger giving goblins disadvantage on attacks against the wielder (a passive, works from the off-hand) — underrated for the dangerous early goblin fights: put it on your squishiest character to make them far harder to focus down.
notes.Slicing Shortsword: A +1 shortsword applying no-save bleed on advantage attacks — great for bleed-synergy builds and setting up con-save effects, but narrow (many Act 2+ enemies are bleed-immune, and you often want a higher-damage weapon).
notes.Sylvan Scimitar: A +1 scimitar that attacks with your casting modifier — lets a caster (paladin, sword bard) make weapon attacks without investing in physical stats, but it's a weak base weapon and the effect is better gotten from Gloves of Dexterity / Strength elixirs.
notes.Belm: A +2 scimitar with Whirlwind and a once/rest bonus-action main-hand attack; a famous BG2 weapon, but the extra bonus-action attack is worse than what sword-and-board users or true dual-wielders already have — it doesn't live up to its reputation.
notes.Coldsnap: A +1 cold dagger: enemies who miss you become Chilled (cold-vulnerable), then a thrown water becomes a no-save Frozen stun; a cool off-hand for a high-AC build and cold synergy, but situational since enemies must attack you to trigger it.
notes.+1 Dagger: Only useful if you've been unlucky and rolled no +1 shortswords/scimitars; a guaranteed one exists near the goblin camp, and it's marginally better for throwers than a plain dagger.
notes.Assassin's Touch: A +1 dagger with +1d4 necrotic vs knocked-out/sleeping enemies; the rider rarely matters (sleeping enemies die easily anyway), so it's mostly just an early guaranteed +1 dagger to fill an off-hand.
notes.Ritual Dagger: A no-enhancement dagger granting one turn of Bless on hit / bonus action; the upkeep is fiddly, it conflicts with other Bless sources, and you sacrifice a +1 — Bless is good but better obtained elsewhere.
notes.Sword of Clutching Umbra: A +1 shortsword with the Shadow-Soaked Blow action, but it's available at the same spot as the far better Sword of Life Stealing — only interesting for a dedicated hiding build relying on the (buggy) no-break-stealth attack.
notes.Fleshrender: A +2 shortsword with a once/rest action that stops a non-undead enemy healing for three turns; situationally useful in the rare heal-heavy fight, but otherwise outclassed by better weapons.
notes.Kurwin's Cauteriser: A +1 scimitar with +1d4 fire that ignites enemies (con save) on every hit — only worth it for burning-synergy or condition-on-every-hit builds (e.g. Hat of Fire Acuity); a plain +1 in Act 3 is otherwise weak.
notes.Stillmaker: A +2 dagger casting level-3 Hold Person once/rest (two targets, sets up auto-crits); a fine effect, but you're using a worse weapon to save a slot you can easily restore otherwise — niche for no-scroll/long-day runs.
notes.Adamantine Scimitar: Effectively a +1 scimitar that ignores slashing resistance and auto-crits objects; the adamantine forge is far better spent on armor/shields, and it isn't even the best Act 1 weapon — never worth the mould.
notes.Scimitar: Identical to a shortsword but with the weaker Lacerate action instead of Piercing Strike — so the non-magical shortsword is simply the better pick.
notes.Poo-Scraper: An Act 3 joke item: just a non-magical dagger with no effects — added 'mostly to generate engagement', genuinely useless as a weapon.
notes.Artificial Leech: A non-magical/+1 dagger whose listed weapon action is only usable by the NPCs it spawns on, not the player — by Act 2 you have all the daggers you need, so no reason to pick it up.
notes.+1 Syringe: Same as the Artificial Leech group (the Syringe): its special action is NPC-only, leaving just a +1 dagger you don't need — D tier.
notes.Trepan: The third of the NPC-weapon trio: its weapon action is player-unusable, so it's just a dagger you'll never bother picking up.
notes.+2 Dagger: A generic +2 dagger found only late; you'll already have guaranteed stronger +2 weapons (Knife, Bloodthirst), so there's no reason to buy it.
notes.+2 Scimitar: A generic +2 scimitar; magical effects far outweigh a plain +2 by the time it appears, so it's skippable.
notes.+2 Shortsword: A generic +2 shortsword; only conceivably useful for an all-finesse party that has exhausted every better option — otherwise ignore it.
notes.Dread Iron Dagger: A +1 dagger with +1d6 necrotic only while hidden (first attack); barely better than the Ritual Dagger of Shar and badly outclassed by your Act 3 weapons.
notes.Gleamdance Dagger: A +2 dagger with a light radius and +1 AC in the off-hand; a shield does the AC better, and for shieldless characters Coldsnap is earlier and strictly better — no reason to use it.
notes.Murderous Cut: A +1 dagger with +1d4 vs sub-50% enemies — the Ritual Dagger of Shar already does +1d4 on every attack better, so this is outclassed by an already-outclassed item.
notes.Salty Scimitar (RRR): A +2 scimitar with Command once/rest — a generic +2 is weak in Act 3; its real value is the joke of announcing 'I'm attacking with my Salty Scimitar' in multiplayer.

notes.Bracers of Defence: +2 AC while unarmored and shieldless — effectively a shield for monks (and shield-less casters), letting them reach competitive AC; best-in-slot defensively for several strong builds and a top-2 glove for a wide array of characters early.
notes.Daredevil Gloves: +1 to spell attack rolls and lets you fire ranged spells in melee without disadvantage — best-in-slot for warlocks (Eldritch Blast) and to-hit cantrip casters until save-DC gloves arrive.
notes.Gloves of Archery: +2 flat damage on every ranged weapon attack (plus bow proficiency) — best-in-slot for any archer for much of Act 1, and the +2 stays relevant deep into the game; great on dual-hand-crossbow Thief Rogues.
notes.Gloves of Cinder and Sizzle: +1d4 fire on every unarmed strike (and a free Scorching Ray) — the first glove that boosts unarmed damage, so best-in-slot for monks, who attack many times and crit-double the die.
notes.Gloves of Dexterity: Set Dexterity to 18 — at worst a free +2 initiative/+1 attack, at best (drop Dex to 8) a free feat and a pile of stat points; the best item in the game, enabling and supercharging hundreds of builds.
notes.Gloves of Uninhibited Kushigo: +1d4 damage on thrown and improvised weapons — the first glove to boost a thrower's damage, so best-in-slot for the very strong throwing-weapon builds in Act 1.
notes.Hellrider's Pride: Casts Blade Ward (halve physical damage) on anyone you heal — effectively doubles a healed ally's HP for free; an Ancients paladin or AOE healer can keep the whole party warded, and you can even throw healing potions to apply it.
notes.Wondrous Gloves: +1 AC for any character (stacks with armor/shield) plus an extra Bardic Inspiration use (four more per day once it refreshes on short rest) — best-in-slot for many bards and a solid AC bump for everyone else.
notes.Dark Justiciar Gauntlets: +1d4 necrotic on every weapon attack — exactly what the offensive glove slot is for; the only downsides are the often-resisted necrotic type and arriving late in Act 2, but most parties happily run two damage-rider gloves.
notes.(Rare) Dark Justiciar Gauntlets: The upgraded version: the same +1d4 necrotic plus a free, cooldown-less bonus-action Beckoning Darkness (2d8 necrotic, DC14 Cha) — an S-tier glove with a strong bonus-action damage option on top.
notes.Flawed Helldusk Gloves: +1d4 fire on weapon attacks OR +1d4 necrotic (plus bleed) on unarmed — a flexible damage-rider almost any party wants; thrown attacks count as both, and it enables several broken prone/maim builds.
notes.Gloves of the Automaton: Bonus-action self-transform granting advantage on ALL weapon attacks for 10 turns once per short rest, no condition — permanent free advantage for any non-advantage build; even better via the bug that keeps it when swapped off.
notes.Luminous Gloves: Apply two stacks of Radiating Orb on any radiant damage — with one other radiant source you hit 8-10 stacks fast, shutting off an enemy's attacks entirely; core to the broken radiating-orb strategy.
notes.Gauntlet of the Tyrant: +1 spell save DC, +1d4 force on unarmed, and Command at level 3 once/rest — the best endgame spellcaster gloves for landing control effects (save DC plus a free cast of one of the most-used spells).
notes.Gloves of Soul Catching: The only legendary gloves: +2 Con, +1d10 force on every unarmed strike, and per-turn either heal 10 or gain near-permanent advantage on attacks/saves — absurd numbers, endgame best-in-slot for every monk.
notes.Helldusk Gloves: +1d6 fire (weapon) or +1d6 necrotic/bleed (unarmed), +1 to ALL attack rolls AND +1 spell save DC — best-in-slot endgame gloves for nearly everyone (caster or martial) who doesn't need Gloves of Dexterity or Soul Catching; there's only one.
notes.Legacy of the Masters: +2 to attack and damage with weapons (melee and ranged) — a massive flat boost any weapon user loves; comparable to the Helldusk Gloves and acquired a bit earlier, so it lands in most parties.
notes.The Reviving Hands: Like Hellrider's Pride (Blade Ward on heal) but NOT nerfed — works on self-healing too, so any character with a healing aura/Ring of Regeneration gets near-permanent Blade Ward; plus a free Revivify.
notes.Gloves of Baneful Striking: Currently bugged to apply no-save Bane (-1d4 to all saves) on any weapon hit — helps land your (and allies') control spells and reverberation/snowburst follow-ups; great on tiger barbarians/sword bards who hit many enemies.
notes.Gloves of Belligerent Skies: Two stacks of Reverberation on thunder/lightning/radiant damage (works in AOE) — the key glove for reverberation builds, getting much better in later acts when those damage types are easy to apply.
notes.Gloves of the Growling Underdog: Advantage on melee attacks while two or more enemies are in melee range — a strong, if positioning-dependent, early advantage source; bumped to A because the author kept recommending it over other fighter gloves.
notes.The Sparkle Hands: Two lightning charges per unarmed hit (+1 to hit, +1d8 every five) — a fine alternative to Cinder and Sizzle for non-Tavern-Brawler monks who value the to-hit bonus, especially if found first.
notes.Gloves of the Balanced Hands: Grants the Two-Weapon Fighting style (add your modifier to off-hand damage) — best-in-slot damage for a mono-class Thief Rogue dual-wielding, often beating even the +damage gloves; redundant if you already get the style from a class.
notes.Hra'cknir Bracers: Quickened Mage Hand plus Telekinesis once per short rest — a free, repeatable level-5 spell with tons of creative uses (shove enemies off cliffs, reposition, out-of-combat traversal); great to swap in for fights where it shines.
notes.Craterflesh Gloves: +1d6 (so 2d6 on the crit) force damage on critical hits, and it works on SPELL attacks — best-in-slot for a crit-focused Eldritch Blast warlock; lower average than flat-damage gloves for non-crit builds.
notes.Dark Displacement Gloves: Throwing a weapon swaps your position with the hit enemy (and +1 thrown attack) — a powerful, wild repositioning tool best on a non-dedicated thrower (e.g. a dagger-tossing monk); risky for a main thrower since you don't always want enemies pulled to you.
notes.Gauntlets of Hill Giant Strength: Set Strength to 23 from the glove slot; great on a no-elixir run or to free your elixir slot, but a Cloud Giant elixir (27) is simply better for most, so it's the elixir-free version of that effect.
notes.Martial Exertion Gloves: A free action once/short rest for an extra attack (at the cost of ~21 self-damage, easily healed) — superb for low-attack/high-damage characters and especially Hunter Rangers, since the extra attack can be Volley or a tiger cleave.
notes.Nimblefinger Gloves: +2 Dexterity for gnomes (uncapped, so up to 24 with the mirror) — a huge Dex boost usable by anyone via Disguise Self, though many prefer Gloves of Dexterity (frees feats/points) and you must stay disguised as a gnome.
notes.Servitor of the Black Hand Gloves: +1d4 force on unarmed strikes (plus a free Fear) — Force is the best generic damage type, so these are the best of the '+1d4 unarmed' monk gloves to bridge the gap until Helldusk/Soul Catching.
notes.Abyss Beckoners: A 30ft aura giving your summons resistance to all damage (but it drives them mad on a DC13 Wis save) — doubles the durability of mindless summons immune to madness (spiritual weapon, flaming sphere, flying ghouls); a strong but risky Summoner pick.
notes.Gloves of Heroism: Grant Heroism (5 temp HP/turn + fear immunity) when you use a Channel Oath ability — decent sustain and fear protection for a paladin who channels often (e.g. Vengeance), but most paladins have better glove options.
notes.Gloves of Power: +1 Sleight of Hand and a no-save (currently) Bane on a Charisma save; the Bane is better on the Baneful Striking gloves, but the +1 Sleight of Hand is a real, swap-in bonus when you already have advantage from elsewhere.
notes.Gloves of Thievery: Advantage on Sleight of Hand — the go-to lockpick/pickpocket glove to swap in when you lack another advantage source (otherwise prefer Gloves of Power); functionally paired with them.
notes.Braindrain Gloves: Psychic damage applies stacking Mental Fatigue (-1/turn to mental saves) — a poor man's Arcane Acuity that, with an illithid psychic-damage power, reliably lands Hold Person; useful when better save-stacking isn't available.
notes.Gauntlets of Surging Accuracy: +1d4 to hit on all attacks the turn you Action Surge — a nice bonus on your most important fighter turns, but it's only once/short rest and you usually don't need help on a surge turn, so flat-damage gloves often win.
notes.Gloves of the Duelist: +2 to melee attack rolls with a one-handed weapon (works with a shield) — solid hit chance for a sword-and-board character, but outclassed by the Automaton's advantage and the Growling Underdog for those builds.
notes.Bonespike Gloves: Your attacks ignore bludgeoning/piercing/slashing resistance — effectively doubles your damage against the many physically-resistant Act 3 enemies; a great swap-in for those fights (unneeded if you have the Bhaalist Armour).
notes.Gauntlets of the Warmaster: +1 to hit and enemies have disadvantage on saves vs your weapon maneuvers/actions — strong for a Battle Master or a Soulbreaker-Greatsword stun user, but many fighters prefer raw damage/advantage gloves.
notes.Seraphic Pugilist Gloves: +1d4 radiant on unarmed strikes (plus a free Guiding Bolt) — mainly for an open-hand monk radiating-orb build (stack radiant with the Callous Glow ring); otherwise a minor upgrade over other +1d4 monk gloves.
notes.Snow-Dusted Monastery Gloves: +1d4 cold on unarmed strikes (plus a free Ice Knife) — cold synergizes (Snowburst ring, Wet doubling), so a niche pick for a cold-focused monk, though endgame gloves overtake it.
notes.Spellmight Gloves: A spell-version of Great Weapon Master: -5 to a spell attack for +1d8 damage (once per spell on honour mode) — best on a crit-focused Eldritch Blast warlock who ignores the to-hit penalty, though Craterflesh usually beats it. (S-tier below honour mode.)
notes.Stalker Gloves: +1 initiative and +1d4 force on sneak attacks; the sneak-attack damage is small (once/round) versus per-attack gloves, so it's mainly for a non-rogue who desperately needs initiative and can spare the slot.
notes.Unlucky Thief's Gloves: +2 Sleight of Hand (the largest such glove bonus) — a joke item (you accumulate charcoal and eventually catch fire from stealing) but a genuinely useful swap-in for the toughest locks.
notes.Fleetfingers: Jump for free whenever you dash — extra mobility for a bonus-action-dashing Rogue, but Rogues rarely have mobility problems and the slot is better spent on stats/damage.
notes.Gloves of Hail of Thorns: Hail of Thorns once/short rest as just an action (free 1d10 AOE on a hit) — fine if a weapon attacker is starved for AOE, but otherwise unexciting.
notes.Gloves of Missile Snaring: Reaction to reduce one ranged attack's damage by 1d10+Dex — a defensive band-aid for a low-AC caster getting pincushioned (e.g. the goblin camp), but the Bracers of Defense or Shield spell usually do it better.
notes.Cindersnap Gloves: Protection from Missiles once/short rest (near-immunity to archers for 10 turns) — a purely defensive swap-in for archer-heavy fights or the Mirror fight, but you'd rather buff offense from this slot.
notes.Gloves of Crushing: +1 to hit and +2 damage on unarmed strikes — a fine effect, but slightly worse than the Sparkle Hands and badly outnumbered by better monk gloves by the time you find them.
notes.Jhannyl's Gloves: +1 saves and auto-cast Lesser Restoration on yourself once/rest when blinded/poisoned/paralyzed — niche insurance for an otherwise-invincible build (Dodge tank, abjuration wizard) against the conditions that can still drop them.
notes.Poisoner's Gloves: Apply Poisoned on poison damage — the core of the Derivation Cloak / Brood Mother's Revenge poison-loop combo, but it costs several gear slots for a weak payoff and many Act 2 enemies are poison-immune.
notes.Swordmaster Gloves: Sword proficiencies and +1 to melee attacks; most characters already have the proficiencies and prefer damage or the better hit-chance gloves, so it rarely sees use (niche: a non-martial wanting greatsword proficiency).
notes.Thermoarcanic Gloves: Build Heat on fire damage to dump as bonus fire damage later — a solution in search of a problem (boosts AOE damage late in a fight, when you don't need it), and the per-turn self-damage threatens concentration.
notes.Bhaalist Gloves: +1 to hit and a once/rest Garrotte (silence + immobilize, DC14 str) that also roots YOU and costs concentration — the effect is decent but the DC is too low and the action cost too high for Act 3, so only a desperate caster-lockdown swap-in.
notes.Gemini Gloves: Once/short rest your cantrip hits an extra target (stacks with Twin Spell) — a nice doubling for a dedicated cantrip build (e.g. Ray of Frost), but most casters want gloves that improve their leveled-spell turns too.
notes.Knock Knuckle: Knock once per long rest — only saves a scroll/slot for a party with no Sleight of Hand character; safe to ignore otherwise.
notes.Quickspell Gloves: Cast an action cantrip as a bonus action once/short rest — best for non-targeted cantrips (Blade Ward, True Strike), so a niche pick for an abjuration wizard keeping Blade Ward up in long fights.
notes.Thunderpalm Strikers: +1d4 thunder on unarmed strikes (plus a free Thunderwave) — the obvious reverberation-monk glove, except the Gloves of Belligerent Skies (which apply reverb) need the same slot, so the synergy can't happen.
notes.Leather Gloves: Non-magical gloves with no effect — purely cosmetic.
notes.Metallic Gloves: Non-magical, +1 strength saves (medium armor) — strength saves are extremely rare, so barely an upgrade over nothing.
notes.Herbalist's Gloves: Cure Poisoned on a creature you heal — very few enemies poison you and poison is easily removed otherwise, so this is a near-useless effect to discard early.
notes.Reason's Grasp: 15 (very) temporary HP when a barbarian manually ENDS rage — barbarians almost never want to end rage, and the HP vanish at end of combat, so there's no reason to use it.
notes.Winter's Clutches: Stacking Encrusted with Frost on cold damage (freeze-stun at 7 stacks) — but reliable cold damage is hard in Act 1, the DC12 con save is easily passed, and enemies die before you stack it; rarely effective.
notes.Gloves of Battlemage's Power: Supposed to grant Arcane Acuity on condition-applying weapon hits, but currently bugged and does nothing — D until fixed (would be A-tier working).
notes.Ichorous Gloves: Chance to apply a weak Noxious Fumes (1d4 acid AOE) on acid damage — acid is hard to deal regularly, the DC13 dex save is easy, and the effect is tiny; not worth the setup.
notes.Raven Gloves: Find Familiar (Quill the raven) once/short rest — a level-1 spell effect in a premium slot, and it can't combine with better familiars; never worth it.
notes.Blackguard's Gauntlets: +1 to hit and a once/rest level-4 Inflict Wounds (medium armor) — the spell needs a high save DC but the to-hit bonus suits martials, so it fights itself, and ~32 necrotic is worse than just attacking by Act 3.
notes.Cerebral Citadel Gloves: Bless for 3 turns when you charm/frighten an enemy — by Act 3 you have Bless elsewhere, and crit-focused fear builds don't need the accuracy; a situational, weak effect.
notes.Spell-Seeking Gloves: Spend sorcery points for advantage on a spell attack (1 per spell level) — far too expensive (you'd rather cast another spell with those points), and advantage is cheaper from hiding/invisibility; avoid.
notes.Unwanted Masterwork Gloves: +1 to attack and +1 strength saves (medium armor) — the +1 to hit is duplicated and far exceeded by many other Act 3 gloves, so it'll essentially never be worn.
notes.Winkling Gloves: +1 Sleight of Hand and +1 to attack — both effects are duplicated far better by other gloves you'll already have, so like the near-identical Unwanted Masterwork gloves it almost never sees play.

notes.Smokepowder Bomb: 3d4+9 force (avg ~16, very reliable) in a fireball-sized 13ft AOE, buyable early — better than any level-1/2 AOE spell at that stage; force damage is rarely resisted, and it can be cheesed to multi-detonate off damage riders.
notes.Water: Cheap, humble, and broken: applies Wet (doubles lightning/cold damage, grants fire resistance) and a water surface you can turn to ice (prone) or steam; throw water then zap for instant kills — every character should carry a stack.
notes.Haste Spore Grenade: AOE Haste for your whole party with NO lethargy downside; action-neutral if you drop it at your feet, and the three turns of extra actions are flatly broken — only doesn't stack with other haste sources (but stacks with Terazul).
notes.Haste Spore Flask: A quest-reward Haste Spore grenade with a larger 10ft radius and a cloud that lasts forever instead of three turns — even more of the best grenade in the game, worth hanging onto.
notes.Sanguine Explosive: Story-locked but worth a level-4 slot: 4d8 force + 4d8 fire (avg 36, more than Fireball) in a huge 26ft AOE plus burning, with a high DC17 save; buy up to two a day for nearly free Nova damage anyone can throw.
notes.Flashblinder: Blind on a beatable DC16 con save is mediocre, but its unlisted no-save stun on Steel Watchers trivializes some of Act 3's most dangerous encounters — situational, but it instantly wins those fights.
notes.Alchemist's Fire: No-save burning plus a 10ft fire surface for ~3d4 fire, common and craftable; reliably finishes low-HP goblins and replaces a spell like Burning Hands — wins you a fight or two in the early game.
notes.Grease Bottle: A Grease spell in a bottle: chance to knock enemies prone (trading your action for theirs), creates difficult terrain, and is flammable for guaranteed finishing damage; low fixed DC12 but high ceiling against grouped enemies.
notes.Void Bulb: One trivial force damage, but a 20ft AOE pull on a genuinely high DC18 strength save — reliably groups enemies for AOE spells/attacks or yanks them off high ground; repositioning is powerful and stays relevant late.
notes.Web Grenade: Plays almost identically to Grease Bottle: low DC12 but the debilitating Webbed condition (can't move, attacked with advantage), difficult terrain, and a flammable surface for extra damage — usually good for one solid effect.
notes.Acid Vial: 2d6 acid that rarely lands (DC13 dex save, a bad save to target), but the no-save acid surface gives -2 AC in a 7ft area — handy to set up a Sharpshooter/GWM ally's attacks, though often beaten by just attacking.
notes.Scrap and Shrapnel Grenade: Fireball-sized AOE dealing a guaranteed ~3 damage plus no-save bleed (disadvantage on con saves) — great for setting up a con-save spellcaster or bleed synergy; easy to forget, and Alchemist's Fire is better for raw damage.
notes.Spiked Bulb: A functional reprint of the Scrap and Shrapnel Grenade (same AOE, ~3 guaranteed damage, no-save bleed/con-save disadvantage), obtainable from the very start of the game.
notes.Caustic Bulb: Creates a unique caustic brine surface for 1d4/turn no-save damage, but it's strictly worse than Alchemist's Fire (no initial burst, no lingering burn) — only relevant on the nautiloid before you can craft Fire.
notes.Holy Water: 3d6 radiant (dex save for half) only to fiends/undead, but those enemies show up late enough that Spirit Guardians and cantrips outdamage it; the water surface is its lone niche — keep one to finish a low-HP target.
notes.Fungal Bamboozler: A 3-turn befuddle cloud (essentially a stun), but the DC10 con save is so low enemies almost never fail it; only worth it with heavy setup (bleed for disadvantage + lockdown), so it's more a for-fun grenade.
notes.Noxious Spore Grenade: 1d4/turn poison plus 2d4 on a save (oddly using your item save DC), so slightly more reliable damage than Caustic Bulb, but a small 7ft AOE and self-destructs if ignited — marginal trapping tool.
notes.Hearthlight Bomb: Just Dancing Lights without concentration; lighting is a marginal effect you cover better with the Light cantrip or a thrown torch, so it's not worth the weight or crafting ingredients.
notes.Oil Flask: An Act 3-only item that makes an inert oil surface whose only use is catching fire — i.e. a strictly worse Alchemist's Fire (which is available from the start), so completely redundant.
notes.Sleeping Potion: Actually a grenade: a tiny 3ft AOE forcing a DC11 con save to sleep, but the DC is too low, allies wake each other, and one-shot use means no re-rolls — a ~30% stun on one enemy for an action; just sell it.

notes.Cap of Curing: Lets you spend Bardic Inspiration for a Healing Word (1d6) — a flexible, modal use of inspiration available very early, and it enables on-heal synergy builds; great for most bards.
notes.Diadem of Arcane Synergy: Inflicting any condition grants Arcane Synergy: add your spellcasting modifier to ALL weapon attacks — trivial to trigger (e.g. an illithid ability-drain power) for huge bonus damage; best-in-slot for pact-of-the-blade warlocks.
notes.Grymskull Helm: Critical-hit immunity (removes the damage spikes that lose runs) plus fire resistance — the main reason to take heavy-armor proficiency; lets you build a crit-immune party, with a free Hunter's Mark on top.
notes.Haste Helm: Three turns of momentum (+15ft move) at combat start — a huge early mobility boost (no more dash-only turns) on any character; once-bugged but now fixed, and stays relevant for much of the game.
notes.Holy Lance Helm: +1 Con saves and 1d4 radiant to enemies who miss you (no cooldown) — chains incredibly with Adamantine/radiating-orb/reverberation gear (radiant applies those), passing out many saves and stacks per turn; underrated.
notes.The Shadespell Circlet: +1 spell save DC while obscured — one of the earliest save-DC boosts (the single most valuable caster stat), and you can reliably stay obscured with darkness/fog; a powerful early swing.
notes.Circlet of Hunting: +1d4 to hit against Hunter's-Marked (or faerie-fire'd/guiding-bolt'd) enemies — turns the bonus action into +1d4 hit and +1d6 damage on every attack for Hunter's Mark Rangers; hit chance is king, especially for Sharpshooter.
notes.Fistbreaker Helm: +1 spell save DC AND +1 initiative — the two things every caster wants, passively and unconditionally; one of the author's most-equipped items, best-in-slot for any caster not stacking Arcane Acuity.
notes.Hat of Fire Acuity: Stacks of Arcane Acuity on fire damage (Scorching Ray maxes it instantly): +save DC and +spell attack per stack — a game-breaking mechanic that pushes save DCs beyond the curve so enemies can't pass; the easiest Acuity item to trigger.
notes.Helmet of Arcane Acuity: Two stacks of Arcane Acuity on any WEAPON hit — trivial to trigger (any attacker), then dump four stacks into a guaranteed Command/Hypnotic Pattern via the Band of the Mystic Scoundrel; the staple of the most broken builds.
notes.Helm of Balduran: Immunity to BOTH crits and stun (the two things that wreck honour runs) plus +1 AC/saves and 2 HP/turn regen (triggers on-heal items) — the premier defensive helmet; medium armor required.
notes.Hood of the Weave: +2 spell save DC and spell attack rolls — basically an upgraded Fistbreaker for the late game (when you have initiative elsewhere); best-in-slot for casters not using Arcane Acuity.
notes.Mask of Soul Perception: +2 initiative and +2 to attack rolls (works on spell attacks too) — two excellent, synergistic stats (go first, hit more) that almost every attacking character wants; a near-universal Act 3 pick.
notes.Cap of Wrath: Two stacks of Wrath each turn you start combat at half HP (+melee damage, up to +7) — a lot of free first-turn damage for a multi-attacker, but you must deliberately sit at half HP and have a way to not heal above it.
notes.Dark Justiciar Helm: +1 to all saves while obscured (medium armor) — easy to maintain and a solid all-round upgrade over the basic helmet; not exciting, but you're always happy to have it on.
notes.Helmet of Autonomy: Grants Wisdom-save proficiency (if you lack it) — the most dangerous save to fail; nearly solves a build's hold-person weakness, making a physically-tanky character almost unkillable. Defensive, so not universal.
notes.Mask of the Shapeshifter: At-will Disguise Self (a DLC item) — disguising your race gives better merchant prices, dialogue/quest options, and access to race-locked gear; very useful to carry even if not worn full-time.
notes.Wapira's Crown: Heal 1d6 yourself whenever you heal another creature — strong with the on-heal buff set and AOE/aura healing (Ancients paladin); a lot of cumulative healing, though it lost some value when Hellrider's Pride stopped triggering off self-heals.
notes.Warped Headband of Intellect: Sets Intelligence to 17 — frees a wizard-dip or Arcane Trickster from investing in Int, and it's one of the best dialogue items (with Jack of All Trades it passes most Int checks); niche but enabling.
notes.Covert Cowl: -1 to your crit threshold while obscured (easy to maintain, e.g. a GOO warlock in its own darkness) — basically passive +1 crit chance, great for crit-stacking Rogues/paladins/warlocks; light armor.
notes.Dark Justiciar Helmet: +1 Con saves, +1 saves vs spells, and -1 crit threshold while obscured (medium armor) — the same crit boost as the Covert Cowl plus useful defensive saves; great for Rangers shoring up their one weakness (saves).
notes.Hat of Storm Scion's Power: Arcane Acuity on THUNDER damage — just as broken as the fire/weapon versions, but harder to trigger (no thunder Scorching Ray); lets you stack Acuity on a second character (e.g. with the Drakethroat Glaive or a Tempest cleric).
notes.Assassin of Bhaal Cowl: +2 initiative (plus negligible see-invisibility/10ft darkvision) — a big bonus, but in Act 3 you likely have initiative sorted elsewhere, so its value is freeing another initiative item/elixir for another character.
notes.Birthright: +2 Charisma (effectively uncapped, up to 26) — strong for any Charisma caster (Eldritch Blast damage, save DC, hit chance), but in the contested Act 3 slot it's often edged out by Arcane Acuity or the Fistbreaker's initiative.
notes.Helldusk Helmet: Crit immunity with NO armor requirement (unique), +2 saves vs spells, and blind immunity (great in a darkness party) plus a fire spell — a huge pile of defensive stats, just outclassed for some by the Helm of Balduran or Mask of Soul Perception.
notes.Helmet of Grit: An extra bonus action while below 50% HP — an extra attack is among the best damage boosts in the game (people take 3 Thief levels for it), usable by ranged builds too; you must manage sitting at half HP.
notes.Circlet of Blasting: Scorching Ray at level 2 once/long rest — a handy extra damage cast to swap in when your fire-build's spell slots are strained early; duplicable by scrolls, so just a convenience.
notes.Circlet of Psionic Revenge: 1d4 psychic to whatever made you succeed on a save (any save, reactive) plus +1 mental saves for gith — small damage, but the mental-save boost is a genuine defensive help for save-vulnerable martials.
notes.Devilfoil Mask: Worn by 2+ nearby characters, each gets +1 Strength (and -1 to mental stats) per extra wearer — a real party-wide Strength boost for a themed all-strength party (e.g. four barbarians get +3), though Strength elixirs usually do it better.
notes.Shadow of Menzoberranzan: Invisibility (2 turns) once per short rest — enough for many out-of-combat and repositioning uses, conserving your precious invisibility potions; an action, so worse than a potion, but free.
notes.Circle of Bones: Free Animate Dead once/rest plus a physical-resistance aura for nearby undead — a durable zombie pet for any character (no madness downside, unlike Abyss Beckoners), and it snowballs for necromancers; arrives a bit late.
notes.Flawed Helldusk Helmet: +1 Con saves and +2 saves vs spells (medium armor) — a genuinely good defensive item, but it's outcompeted in its niche by the Helmet of Autonomy (Wis saves) and the Dark Justiciar Helmet (saves + crit); fine on anyone.
notes.Marksmanship Hat: +1 to ranged/thrown attack rolls — a real hit-chance bonus, but Rangers prefer the Circlet of Hunting and Rogues prefer crit-chance, so most archers who want it have a better option; never a bad pick though.
notes.Bonespike Helmet: +2 intimidation and a once/turn free Menacing Attack (frighten + ~+4 damage) for barbarians — a strong lockdown/damage option that would be top-tier elsewhere, but Act 3 helmet competition is fierce.
notes.Hat of the Sharp Caster: Reroll 1s/2s on spell-attack damage dice — minor for Eldritch Blast/Scorching Ray, but (via a bug) it works on Magic Missile, making it a key piece of max-damage Magic Missile builds.
notes.Horns of the Berserker: +2 to hit and +2 necrotic vs non-full-HP enemies while you're hurt — a solid, near-unconditional damage/accuracy boost, but most builds prefer a defensive helmet or the Helmet of Grit's extra bonus action.
notes.Pyroquickness Hat: An extra bonus action when you deal fire damage with a leveled spell (but you set yourself on fire) — great for a Quicken-spell fire sorcerer who's immune to burning (e.g. Wet), though the Hat of Fire Acuity is better for that character.
notes.Helmet: The non-magical metallic helmet's +1 Con saves is actually useful very early (concentration for a cleric/Druid on Bless), so you may wear it a while before finding a magical helmet — quickly outclassed.
notes.Key of the Ancients: +1 Nature (perhaps the least-rolled skill) plus an alternate solution to an Act 1 quest — wear it only for the rare nature check or the quest access.
notes.The Lifebringer: 3 temp HP when you gain lightning charges — redundant with the Boots of Aid and Comfort on turn one, so only marginal in-combat regen for a charge build with no other temp-HP source.
notes.Whispering Mask: See through Auntie Ethel's illusions for better loot/skips, but every turn risks turning hostile (Wis save) — usable if you pre-cast Protection from Good and Evil; only relevant for that one encounter.
notes.Browbeaten Circlet: +1 spell save DC while threatened — a good effect, but it forces you into melee and is outclassed by the Shadespell Circlet, Fistbreaker, and (especially) the Arcane Acuity items available at the same time.
notes.Coldbrim Hat: Two turns of Encrusted with Frost (disadvantage on Dex saves) when a spell applies a condition — useful for stacking conditions to trigger radiating orb/reverberation, but it arrives late when you have better save-DC tools.
notes.Gibus of the Worshipful Servant: Charisma-modifier momentum at combat start (up to +25ft) plus advantage on concentration saves — a genuinely nice package, but spellcasters in Act 3 want save DC/initiative and already have mobility options.
notes.Sarevok's Horned Helmet: Permanent -1 crit threshold and fear immunity (plus darkvision) — but the Dark Justiciar Helmet gives the same crit boost (only mildly conditional) plus saves, so this is mainly for those who'd rather not micro obscurement.
notes.Shapeshifter Hat: +1 wild-shape charge (long rest) — modest extra longevity for a Druid, but most Druids want save DC/initiative to cast-then-shift; a swap-in if you run dry, not a mainstay.
notes.Steel Watcher Helmet: Advantage on Con saves and blind immunity (medium armor) — lets a non-warlock fight in their own Darkness clouds, but it's the fourth-best source of that effect, only for a dedicated multi-character darkness party.
notes.The Pointy Hat: Adds your Intelligence modifier to persuasion — niche, since most party faces dump Int and you've made the big checks by Act 3; mainly lets a Gale/wizard act as party face. Glorious, though.
notes.Hat: A non-magical hat with no mechanical effect — pure cosmetic/superstition.
notes.Leather Helmet: +1 Dex saves (light armor) — you'll have something better long before you face an enemy that makes you roll Dex saves, so it rarely matters.
notes.Dark Justiciar Mask: +1 intimidation (light armor) — a tiny bonus to the least-used Charisma skill, easily gotten elsewhere; safe to forget.
notes.Helmet of Smiting: Charisma-modifier temp HP when a Smite spell applies a condition (medium armor, paladin) — too hard to trigger for ~3 temp HP, which the Boots of Aid and Comfort give for free; no reason to use it.
notes.Circlet of Mental Anguish: Heal 1d4 when an enemy fails a mental save vs your spell — a defensive effect that only triggers when you've basically already won the fight (hypnotic pattern, banish); too minor and self-defeating.
notes.Hat of Uninhibited Kushigo: +1 spell save DC (one turn) after an unarmed hit — needs you to attack and cast the same turn, doesn't stack, and four-elements monks who'd want it lack the stats; too many hoops for a duplicable bonus.
notes.Jannath's Hat: Advantage on persuasion/deception — but you've made the key checks by Act 3, and the Friends cantrip / Enhance Ability duplicate it cheaply and without risk; little impact.
