notes.Scabby Pugilist Circlet: +2 weapon/unarmed damage when surrounded by 2+ enemies — strictly worse than the Horns of the Berserker (near-unconditional +2 damage and +2 hit, no proficiency needed), which is itself often beaten by the Mask of Soul Perception.

subject: Light armor
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, armor part 2

notes.Spidersilk Armour: 12 AC, +1 stealth, and advantage on Constitution saves; unremarkable for the Rogues it seems built for, but best-in-slot for defensive light-armor casters (warlock/bard) who need to hold concentration. Guaranteed in Act 1 regardless of story choices.
notes.+2 Studded Leather: The first 14 AC light armor (finally beating Mage Armor) plus +1 initiative — and d4-rolled initiative makes that bonus hugely valuable; the first light armor you're actively happy to get, and merchant-available to every party from level 9.
notes.Armour of Moonbasking: 13 AC and advantage on spell saves that both persist into wild shape, +2 AC to all wild-shape forms, and 22 temp HP with -1 damage taken on each transform — exactly what Moon/wild-shaping Druids want, since few items work while shifted.
notes.Armour of the Sporekeeper: 13 AC, +1 save DC, +1 necrotic damage (a rider that can multiply on stacked builds), and grants once-per-rest Spore Sac abilities — including a bonus-action AOE Haste for your whole party, which alone is worth swapping it in for.
notes.Bhaalist Armour: 14 AC, +2 initiative, and the Aura of Murder: every enemy within 7ft becomes vulnerable to piercing (doubling all piercing damage, even against resistant foes) — game-breaking for daggers/archers and free value for any party with incidental piercing; arguably S+.
notes.Elegant Studded Leather: 14 AC, +2 initiative, advantage on stealth, and Shield once per short rest — the first light armor that genuinely keeps you alive, an elegant all-in-one package; not story-locked, so accessible to most parties (unlike Bhaalist).
notes.Studded Leather: 12 AC and drops off many early enemies — your first upgrade from the base 11 AC armors; perfectly fine early, but you'll replace it as soon as something better appears.
notes.Drow Studded Leather: 12 AC and 'stealth +1' coded as a proficiency bump, so characters with stealth expertise actually get +2; comes from a box (not a merchant), making it a meaningful early upgrade over plain studded leather.
notes.Armour of Landfall: 13 AC, +1 save DC, advantage on Con saves, and Plant Growth once per short rest — a solid spellcaster package usable by any human/half-elf caster, but the low 13 base AC means robes+Mage Armor or medium armor often serve better.
notes.+1 Padded Armour: 12 AC with 1 bludgeoning resistance (a damage type that's rare early), available levels 2-4; only worth it if you can't field enough Studded Leather for the whole party — a marginal optimization, not worth seeking out.
notes.+2 Padded Armour: Your first 13 AC light armor (levels 5-8) with 2 bludgeoning resistance, but 13 AC just matches Mage Armor, so most characters do better in a good clothing set + Mage Armor; useful only when you lack Mage Armor or want armor-slot boots/gloves.
notes.Shadeclinger Armour: 12 AC, stealth +1, and advantage on ALL saving throws while obscured (easy to maintain in dark Act 2); a powerful but purely defensive effect undercut by the low base AC, so it's a situational swap-in for fear/hold-person fights on caster types.
notes.Padded Armour: Stand-in for all 11 AC non-magical armors; you'd never equip it on purpose, but it's the one use case where a robe-wearing wizard/sorcerer with light armor proficiency can bump AC from 10 to 11 in the very early game.
notes.+1 Leather Armour: 12 AC with +1 stealth (levels 4-6); a marginal step up from +1 Padded, but by the time you can buy it you likely have better options like the Graceful Cloth — not worth the mental energy.
notes.+2 Leather Armour: 13 AC with +2 stealth and +1 to Dex saves/checks (Act 2); a niche pick for characters who must wear light armor and can't use Mage Armor + Graceful Cloth, but otherwise outclassed by that combo.
notes.Penumbral Armour: 12 AC with +3 stealth while obscured; the big stealth bonus suits stealth archers, but the low AC and the fact that Graceful Cloth + Mage Armor does it better relegate it to all-stealth parties or backup.
notes.Blazer of Benevolence: 11 AC; grants 4 temp HP when you Bardic-Inspire an ally, but you give up at least 1 AC versus studded leather and 4 unhealable temp HP is worth less than that AC at the level you get it — even in its best case it's worse than plain studded leather. Looks cool, though.
notes.+1 Studded Leather: 13 AC with 1 bludgeoning resistance (levels 6-8) — strictly worse than +2 Padded Armour, which has the same AC, more resistance, and is always available a level earlier; no reason to ever use it.
notes.Scarlet Leather: An Act-3-only reprint of +1 Leather Armour (12 AC, +1 stealth) — a weak Act 1 armor handed to you in Act 3, so it's terrible by the time you see it.

subject: Medium armor
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, armor part 3

notes.Githyanki Half Plate: 15 base AC and Lae'zel's starting armor, so with +2 Dex and a shield you hit 19 AC from level 1 — near-unhittable early, and it stays best-in-slot for medium/heavy users through a big chunk of Act 1.
notes.+1 Breastplate: 15 AC and 1 piercing resistance (the most relevant physical type, vs early goblin archers); one is stealable in the Druid Grove, giving you a crucial second high-AC set early — a top early-game priority for most parties.
notes.Luminous Armour: 15 AC plus Radiating Shockwave: dealing radiant damage stacks Radiating Orb (up to -10 to enemy attack rolls) in a 10ft radius — arguably a top-3 item in the game, instantly winning boss fights, and strong even with only incidental radiant damage.
notes.Adamantine Scale Mail: 16 AC, -1 to all incoming damage, critical-hit immunity, and Reeling on attackers who hit you — a cohesive defensive package that removes the random crit spikes that lose fights; best-in-slot tank armor for most of the game.
notes.Yuan-Ti Scale Mail: 15 AC that uses your FULL Dex bonus (uncapped) plus +1 initiative and no stealth disadvantage — the highest-AC setup in the game for Dex characters and the gold-standard Act 2 armor for them.
notes.(Rare) Dark Justiciar Half-Plate: The very rare DJ plate: 17 AC (highest non-uncapped in Act 2), advantage on Con saves, advantage on stealth while obscured, Shield of Faith once/rest, plus -2 damage and necrotic reflection while it's active — strictly better than the base version.
notes.Elven Chain: 14 AC, +2 initiative, advantage on Dex saves, and grants proficiency — implemented as 'counts as cloth', so non-proficient casters/Rogues wear it freely AND it stacks with Bracers of Defense, monk speed, etc.; a superb high-AC + initiative option for those classes.
notes.Armour of Agility: 17 base AC, fully uncapped Dex, and +2 saves — the highest AC values in the game (30+ is easy), making high-Dex characters near-invincible to physical attacks, while the +2 saves cover the fear/dominate effects that are their only real weakness.
notes.Flame Enamelled Armour: 16 AC, +2 initiative, +2 saves, fire (and on-cast cold) resistance, and Fire Shield once/rest — a fantastic all-rounder that lets a high-AC character also go first; the safe default if no other S-tier armor calls to you.
notes.Scale Mail: 14 AC non-magical, the Fighter's starting armor that also drops off the nautiloid devils — a solid +1 AC upgrade over Shadowheart's chain shirt for the very early game, quickly superseded.
notes.+1 Half Plate: 16 AC with 1 piercing resistance, buyable from level 6 — likely your first 16 AC medium armor and a meaningful party-wide upgrade, though less critical than others since heavy-armor users start converting around now.
notes.+2 Hide: 14 AC, +1 to Dex saves/checks, and +1 initiative from level 5 — likely your first medium-armor initiative source, and initiative is good enough in BG3 that it's worth the 1 AC drop versus Githyanki Half Plate.
notes.+2 Scale Mail: 16 AC, 1 slashing resistance, and +1 initiative — high AC plus an initiative bonus makes it a strict upgrade over the +1 Half Plate or +2 Hide for many characters, though defensive/Dex builds prefer Adamantine/Yuan-Ti.
notes.Dark Justiciar Half Plate: 16 AC, advantage on Con saves, advantage on stealth while obscured, and Shield of Faith once/rest — a great spellcaster package (high AC + concentration help), just edged out by Adamantine Scale Mail and Luminous Armour for the classes that want it.
notes.+1 Chain Shirt: 14 AC and 1 slashing resistance from the first merchant; only useful for a third medium-armor user since you'll already have Githyanki Half Plate and a stealable +1 Breastplate — a small, quickly-outgrown upgrade.
notes.+2 Chain Shirt: 15 AC, 1 slashing resistance, and +1 to Dex saves/checks (level 5); marginally useful for a stealthy medium-armor character making actual stealth checks, but a minor upgrade not worth seeking out.
notes.Sharpened Snare Cuirass: 14 AC but uncapped Dex (so high AC for high-Dex builds) plus disadvantage on enemy saves vs your Ensnaring effects — enables a fun entangling build with Cruel/Woad Shield/gloves, though Yuan-Ti Scale Mail's higher AC keeps it in B.
notes.+2 Half Plate: 17 AC with 2 piercing resistance (level 9); possibly your first 17 AC armor, but by then high-Dex/caster/defensive characters are committed to better sets — a fine second armor for an unspecialized character.
notes.Unwanted Masterwork Scalemail: 16 AC, uncapped Dex, and fire resistance — a powerful uncapped armor, but Yuan-Ti Scale Mail (+1 initiative) and Armour of Agility (+2 saves, 17 AC) are available around the same time and better, leaving it without a niche.
notes.Chain Shirt: 13 AC non-magical (Shadowheart's start); normally ignorable, but a Ranger (11 AC leather) or low-Con Barbarian can use an early-dropping chain shirt to bump their AC.
notes.Dark Justiciar Mail: 13 AC, no stealth disadvantage, and 1d4 caustic retaliation while obscured; too low AC to wear normally, but theoretically usable in an armor-of-agathys/fire-shield retaliation build for the extra necrotic-style damage.
notes.Hedge Wanderer Armour: 13 AC with +1 Nature (proficiency-coded, so +2 with expertise), +1 Dex saves/checks, and no stealth disadvantage; the AC drop isn't worth it full-time, but you can swap it in to pass a specific Nature or Sleight of Hand check.
notes.+1 Scale Mail: 15 AC with 1 slashing resistance (levels 4-6); by the time it appears you have better 15 AC options (Githyanki, +1 Breastplate, maybe Luminous), so it's only a tiny upgrade worth chasing for hardcore optimizers.
notes.The Jolty Vest: A +1 Chain Shirt (14 AC, slashing resistance) that, while you hold lightning charges, makes attackers save (DC14) or be Shocked; the DC is easy to pass and reactive Shocked is a weak effect, so it's a marginal Lightning-Charge pick.
notes.Barkskin Armour: Locks your AC at exactly 16 (no Dex or shield bonuses) with advantage on Con saves; you sacrifice a lot of AC for those Con saves, which is counterproductive for concentration — only for a forced-low-Dex challenge build.
notes.Psionic Ward Armour: 15 AC, and for gith (or anyone using Disguise Self) psychic resistance plus 1d4 heal on a successful spell save; psychic resistance is genuinely rare, making it a niche swap-in for psychic-heavy fights only.
notes.Damaged Scale Mail: 12 base AC non-magical; by the time it drops in Act 1 you have far better armor and it barely sells — no reason to ever pick it up.
notes.Breastplate: 14 AC, identical to Scale Mail but with no stealth disadvantage (which barely matters) and only available near the end of Act 1, when 14 AC is worthless — never worth picking up.
notes.Half Plate: Functionally identical to Githyanki Half Plate (15 AC) but only sold near the end of Act 1, where 15 AC non-magical armor is no longer valuable — never worth buying.
notes.+1 Hide Armour: 13 AC, no stealth disadvantage, +1 Dex saves/checks — a strictly worse Hedge Wanderer Armour (no Nature bonus), available at the same time and place, so there's no reason to ever buy it.
notes.Slippery Chain Shirt: 13 AC and a free disengage when you heal, but medium-armor characters rarely want to leave melee and the low AC isn't worth it; even the hoped-for Cloak of Cunning Brume combo doesn't trigger — a cool idea that's too weak.
notes.The Oak Father's Embrace: 13 AC with 1d6 radiant retaliation against undead (but you take extra radiant from beasts) — only a marginal swap-in over the Dark Justiciar Mail for undead retaliation fights; otherwise just a 13 AC armor with no real ability.
notes.+2 Breastplate: 16 AC, 1 piercing resistance, +1 Dex saves/checks (level 9); by Act 3 it's outclassed — the +2 Scale Mail gives the same AC plus +2 initiative, so there's no reason to use this.

subject: One-handed weapons (rapiers, flails, morningstars, war picks)

notes.Infernal Rapier: A +2 finesse rapier granting +1 spell save DC AND letting you attack with your spellcasting modifier — enabling spell/weapon hybrids (e.g. a Charisma paladin) to land attacks and Smites while maxing save DC; also a once/rest level-6 Planar Ally summon.
notes.Duellist's Prerogative: A legendary +3 rapier with +1d4 necrotic, a bonus-action attack and +1 crit while your off-hand is empty, and — crucially — a SECOND reaction every turn; the extra reaction (Counterspell, opportunity attacks, etc.) makes it a build-defining weapon.
notes.Flail of the Vortex: An exploit-only +3 flail with +1d8 lightning and an at-will action for +d10 and a DC13 stun; not intended to be obtainable, but if you grab it, it's a very powerful (if not wildly broken) stun weapon.
notes.The Sacred Star: A +2 morningstar (Heart Stopper) with +1d4 radiant that applies Radiating Orb on every hit, turns undead, and has a once/rest AOE blind — probably the best debuffing weapon in the game, piling attack-roll penalties and stuns on enemies; underrated because it isn't a damage king.
notes.+1 Rapier: The highest-damage finesse weapon early on, with no great competition in Act 1 — the natural pick for a Dexterity character using a one-handed weapon and shield, or a Bard/Rogue.
notes.Defender Flail: A +1 flail granting +1 AC and -1 to all physical damage taken — its passives work from the unequipped slot, so an archer or tank stacks it for very high AC/damage reduction (which scales nonlinearly); enables several tanky builds.
notes.Sword of Screams: A no-enhancement rapier with +1d4 psychic on every hit; one of the few finesse options in early Act 1, so a solid damage pick for shield/dual-wielder finesse characters (the psychic type is a mild downside until the Resonance Stone).
notes.Flail of Ages: A +1 flail with a once/rest Elemental Rage (+1 enhancement, +1d4 of a chosen type, and an on-hit condition); choosing cold applies Chilled (→ freeze-stun with Wet), and stacking damage types inflicts multiple conditions per attack — great for condition/reverb builds.
notes.Morningstar: Non-magical d8 carrying Heart Stopper — a two-turn stun (Chest Trauma) on a short-rest cooldown, the most powerful default weapon action; even the plain version is worth swapping to for one stun-critical encounter a day.
notes.+1 Morningstar: A +1 morningstar; worth buying specifically for reliable access to Heart Stopper's two-turn stun in the early game, since the +1 makes the attack more likely to land.
notes.Rupturing Blade: A +1 rapier with a once/rest +d6+proficiency fire and double bleed/burn saving throws (you take a little self-damage); a fine finesse option for a Rogue/Bard who lacks longsword proficiency, though Phalar Aluve usually outclasses it.
notes.Twist of Fortune: A +1 morningstar (Heart Stopper stun) that rerolls 1s/2s on its own damage die and has Blood Money (damage scaling with the target's gold) — the reroll is negligible and Blood Money is mostly flavor/exploit, but the stun keeps it useful.
notes.Pelorsun Blade: A +1 rapier with +1d4 radiant and passive advantage vs undead (works from the unequipped slot); arrives in Act 3 where undead are scarcer, but radiant damage on a finesse weapon suits a finesse radiating-orb paladin.
notes.Deep Delver: A no-enhancement war pick applying Shattered (+1d4 from this weapon, no save) on hit; by now Elemental Weapon gives +1d4 on any weapon, so it's weak — but the guaranteed no-save condition can cheaply apply Radiating Orb/reverberation.
notes.Harmonic Dueller: A +1 rapier with a once/rest performance check granting +Charisma to weapon damage for 10 turns; you can't pre-buff and swap away (the buff drops when unequipped), so only a niche dual-wield build wants it, and better options exist.
notes.Ravengard's Scourger: A +2 morningstar (Heart Stopper) with a once/rest Commander's Strike; even the strong stun action can't save an otherwise generic +2 weapon with a weak, niche bonus-action ability in Act 3.
notes.Flail: Non-magical d8 with a solid action set (Tenacity, Concussive Smash, Weakening Strike), but no character starts with one, so by the time you find it you already have magical weapons.
notes.Rapier: Non-magical d8 finesse with good weapon actions (Piercing Strike, Weakening Strike, Flourish); a great chassis, but you can't start with one, so it's superseded by magical weapons immediately.
notes.War Pick: Non-magical d8 with Piercing Strike, Weakening Strike, and a Maiming Strike (immobilize); a fine action set, but the rarest weapon type and never available before magical alternatives.
notes.+1 Flail: A generic +1 flail; outclassed by other weapons available from the start of the game, so there's no reason to buy it.
notes.+1 War Pick: A generic +1 war pick; like the +1 flail, it's outcompeted by better early options.
notes.Corrosive Flail: A +1 flail with a once/rest acid-surface action (-2 enemy AC); only once/rest, you usually stand in your own acid (taking the penalty), and it requires hitting first — defeating the point of an accuracy debuff.
notes.Hoppy: A +1 war pick with a once/rest +proficiency necrotic and 1d6 self-heal; far too minor an effect to justify the weapon slot, and other weapons heal you better.
notes.Myrkulite Scourge: A +1 flail with +1d6 necrotic; its only advantage is bonus damage, but as a late Act 2 weapon it simply deals less than the alternatives you already have.
notes.+2 Flail: A generic +2 flail only guaranteed at the very end; worse than the magical weapons you have by then.
notes.+2 Rapier: A generic +2 rapier, same story as the +2 flail — outclassed by your existing weapons, so not worth it.

notes.Diluted Oil of Sharpness: +1 to attack and damage for 10 turns that stacks with weapon enchantments and makes the weapon magical; pure upfront damage with no downside, scaling with every attack — just use it constantly.
notes.Oil of Accuracy: +2 to attack (better than the +1/+1 of Sharpness since to-hit is king); raises a damage dealer's average output by roughly 10% and makes them far more reliable.
notes.Crawler Mucus: DC13 con save or poisoned AND paralyzed for a turn — the most debilitating condition in the game (no actions plus guaranteed crits within 10ft); a free ~25% chance to instantly win an encounter on the opening hit.
notes.Karabasan's Poison: A unique, non-craftable version of Crawler Mucus that does exactly the same thing — DC13 con save or paralyzed, an encounter-ending effect.
notes.Oil of Combustion: No-save: hit an enemy then deal any fire damage and they explode for 3d6 fire (no save) in a 10ft radius; trivial to self-trigger with a fire damage source, and the explosions chain across grouped enemies for huge AOE burst.
notes.Arsonist's Oil: No-save strip of fire resistance (the tooltip lies — it doesn't grant vulnerability outright), mandatory for fire-damage builds; a buggy combo with the Wet condition can even flip enemies to fire-vulnerable for double damage.
notes.Drow Poison: DC13 con save or fall asleep — losing a turn often wins the fight outright; low DC and one-shot inoculation limit reliability, but the asleep effect is powerful enough to use all game, especially thrown early as a grenade.
notes.Wizardsbane Oil: No-save -3 to enemy spell attacks/save DCs plus disadvantage on concentration saves for two turns; reliably breaks enemy caster concentration, a nice-to-have for specific wizard encounters.
notes.Malice: DC15 con save (on a weapon) or poisoned and blinded; blind is a solid condition enabling advantage on follow-up attacks, but less debilitating than Crawler/Drow's effects — use it when the better poisons are unavailable. (Thrown DC is a near-useless 10.)
notes.Oil of Bane: DC11 Charisma save or Bane (-1d4 to attacks and saves); the low DC targets Charisma, the best save to target, and there's no inoculation so you keep retrying — a reliable workhorse debuff when you lack a better oil.
notes.Oil of Freezing: No-save stacking Encrusted with Frost; at 7 turns built up it forces a DC12 con save or Frozen (incapacitated, vulnerable to bludgeoning/thunder/force). No inoculation but needs ~4 hits to trigger — B by default, A/S for multi-hit builds.
notes.Basic Poison: DC11 con save or poisoned (disadvantage on attacks); the DC is too low to land reliably and it's purely defensive, but it's the earliest poison you'll have, so it can help in dangerous early multi-turn fights.
notes.Wyvern Toxin: A toxin dealing 1d8 at end of the enemy's next turn on a DC15 con save; weak in a vacuum, but you can get it extremely early, where the relevant damage and beatable DC speed up some of the hardest opening fights.
notes.Thisobald's Brewed-Up Bellyglummer: Weapon-only DC17 con save (no inoculation, so retries land it reliably) for disadvantage on attacks plus 1d6 poison/turn; similar to Oil of Bane but its disadvantage is easily duplicated by no-save effects like Fog Cloud.
notes.Simple Toxin: DC11 con save or 1d4 at end of the enemy's next turn — the DC is far too low and delayed end-of-turn damage barely matters; only worth it if you have no other coating and a spare bonus action.
notes.Serpent Fang Toxin: Same delayed-damage toxin as Simple Toxin with DC13 and 1d6; the DC is just reaching a breakpoint but 1d6/turn is still negligible damage for a coating slot.
notes.Purple Worm Toxin: DC19 (reliable) but only 1d10 delayed damage that ignores crits/riders, obtained in Act 2 where you have better coatings and many enemies are poison-immune — usually better sold.
notes.Oil of Diminution: DC11 con save or Reduced (-1d4 melee damage, disadvantage on strength); the save is pitiful and the effect marginal, so you can never build a strategy around it — sell it.

notes.Lightning Jabber: A +1 spear that applies shocking grasp (no reactions) and +1d4 lightning when thrown; on honour mode it's the best Act 1 throwing weapon for an Eldritch Knight (who can recall it), beating the Returning Pike on damage. (Insanely buggy/broken below honour mode.)
notes.Returning Pike: A +1 pike that returns when thrown — best-in-slot for any non-Eldritch-Knight throwing character (probably the best strategy in the game), and a fine early melee pike with great weapon actions besides.
notes.Sorrow: A +1 glaive granting at-will bonus-action Sorrowful Lash (a Thorn Whip that adds your spellcasting modifier and pulls enemies 10ft) — pickpocketable at the very start, so an early +1 glaive with a powerful repositioning bonus-action is S-tier.
notes.The Skinburster: A +1 halberd granting 2 stacks of Force Conduit on damage (up to -7 physical damage taken, with AOE force retaliation at 5+); its Cleave stacks it fast (6 in one action), making the wielder nearly unkillable — superb on tiger-heart barbarians.
notes.Unseen Menace: An invisible +1 pike granting advantage on every attack (until you miss) plus crits on 19-20 — the first source of permanent passive advantage; best-in-slot for early paladins (more crit smites) and Sentinel reach users.
notes.Drakethroat Glaive: A +2 glaive whose Draconic Elemental Weapon can be cast on ANY weapon (a free Elemental Weapon: +1 enhancement and +1d4 elemental, all day) — it passively upgrades your whole party's best weapons from the inventory; arguably the best weapon in the game.
notes.Halberd of Vigilance: A +2 halberd with +1d4 force, +1 initiative, advantage on perception, and advantage on reaction attacks — best-in-slot two-handed weapon when you get it, and a Drakethroat enchant turns it into a +3 with 2d4 bonus damage.
notes.Shar's Spear of Evening: A +3 spear granting advantage on all saves while obscured, +1d6 vs obscured enemies, blind immunity (devil-sight-like darkness abuse), at-will Darkness, and an AOE darkness weapon action — possibly the single best weapon in the game.
notes.Monster Slayer Glaive: A +1 glaive with +1d4 attack/damage vs monstrosities and +5ft jump; the bonuses are passive, so an archer can wield it in the unequipped slot for a huge boost (esp. dual hand crossbows) during monstrosity fights while keeping their real bow.
notes.Vision of the Absolute: A no-enhancement spear: hits force a DC11 dex save (a weak save) or Blind, plus +2d6 vs multi-eyed enemies (spiders/spectators); the free on-hit blind is decent value and it's excellent for the early spider fight, though the low DC limits it.
notes.Selune's Spear of Night: A legendary +3 spear, but mostly carried for its once/day Moonmote (60ft aura: +1d4 radiant to allied melee, difficult terrain for enemies, and free reverb/radiating-orb application); great for one encounter a day, unexciting otherwise — swap it out.
notes.Hellbeard Halberd: A +2 halberd dealing a flat +6 poison and applying Poisoned (no save) on every hit — disadvantage on enemy attacks all fight, great for long boss fights vs non-poison-immune enemies and the poisoner item set; low end of A.
notes.The Dancing Breeze: A +2 glaive that's also a finesse weapon with a once/rest Whirlwind AOE — lets a Dexterity character use Polearm Master/Sentinel reach builds with the best stat in the game, enabling many builds (e.g. Sentinel paladin) that otherwise wouldn't work.
notes.+1 Spear: A +1 thrown weapon — a better emergency/initiating throw than the non-magical version (+1 damage thrown), worth stockpiling for a throwing-weapon character, though hand axes/javelins are usually preferred for weight.
notes.The Watcher's Guide: A no-enhancement spear granting True Strike (advantage) on your next attack after a miss; often the first magical weapon you find, so worth picking up and using for an encounter or two before swapping.
notes.Moonlight Glaive: A +2 glaive with +1d4 radiant and a once/rest Moonlight Butterflies attack (≈+8 damage, advantage aura); a fine radiant weapon, but outclassed by the Halberd of Vigilance and Drakethroat — mainly for a radiating-orb paladin.
notes.Pike: The non-magical pike has arguably the best weapon-action combo in the game (Rush Attack + Brace + Piercing Strike), so it's one of the best base weapons to use while you're still on non-magical gear.
notes.Spear: A simple, versatile, thrown weapon (highest-damage simple weapon two-handed, fire-dippable); has some niche use as a thrown weapon, but no one starts with one and hand axes/javelins are better throws — mostly overshadowed.
notes.Jagged Spear: A no-enhancement spear applying Tortured (disadvantage on con saves) on a DC10 Charisma save; the condition is weak and unreliable, but it can debuff bleed-immune enemies — niche for a reverb + con-save build.
notes.Light of Creation: A +1 halberd with +1d6 lightning, but you must pass a DC10 con save each attack or be stunned (unless you're a construct); only usable by a character that reliably passes the save, and it's outclassed before the construct combo comes online.
notes.Harmonium Halberd: A +1 halberd granting +2 Strength (max 23) but -1 Int/Wis (hurting Wisdom saves); competes with Strength elixirs (which go higher, free, no weapon slot), so only a natural-strength character wants it, and even then it's unexciting.
notes.Glaive: Non-magical two-handed reach d10 with Rush Attack, Lacerate, and Brace — fine, but overshadowed by the better weapon actions on the non-magical pike, so it sits in D.
notes.Halberd: Identical to the glaive but trades Brace for Cleave, which only beats a normal attack when hitting 3 enemies — so as a non-magical weapon it's slightly worse than the glaive.
notes.+1 Glaive: A generic +1 glaive; the strictly-superior Returning Pike is sold by the same early merchants, so there's no reason to buy it.
notes.+1 Halberd: A generic +1 halberd; like the +1 glaive, it's outclassed by the Returning Pike available at the same early merchants.
notes.+1 Pike: A generic +1 pike; the Returning Pike is a strictly-better +1 pike available from the same early merchant, so the plain version is pointless.
notes.Merregon Halberd: Just a +1 halberd with a cool icon and no special effect — identical to a weapon you could already have, so there's no reason to use it.
notes.Spear of Night: A story-event +1 spear with no other magical effect — only rated for its weapon use, which at that point is bottom-tier.
notes.+2 Spear: A generic +2 spear found only at the very end; you'll have far better spears from Act 2 story events, so don't bother.
notes.Breaching Pikestaff: A +2 pike with +1d4 force and no other effect; in Act 3 it's worse than the Halberd of Vigilance or a Drakethroat-enchanted weapon — nothing exciting.
notes.Infernal Spear: A +1 spear with a flat 3 poison that (unlike the Hellbeard) doesn't poison on every hit; by Act 3 you have far better spears, and even poison builds have better options.
notes.Magical Pike: Just a renamed +1 pike — a +1 weapon has no use in Act 3.

notes.Supreme Healing: Heals a reliable 10d4+20 (avg 45) for a bonus action — roughly half a health bar, enough to undo a major enemy attack or revive an ally to fighting shape; uses unchanged tabletop numbers while BG3 makes drinking a bonus action, so it breaks the HP economy.
notes.Speed: Three turns of Haste from a potion, so no concentration risk and no chance of stunning yourself/allies; one extra action per turn breaks action economy, and even an unexpected lethargy stun still nets you two extra turns.
notes.Terazul: Two turns of Haste that costs no action to consume AND stacks with other haste sources — drink it alongside a Speed potion for two extra actions a turn; criminally under-discussed but flatly busted.
notes.Healing: 2d4+2 (avg 7) for a bonus action: a self-cast Healing Word that pushes you past one-hit thresholds, revives a downed ally, and turns gold into HP out of combat — indispensable on every character.
notes.Superior Healing: 8d4+8 (avg 28) is more than most healing spells and actually lets you out-heal incoming damage in combat; buyable/craftable regularly so you can stockpile a deep reserve.
notes.Angelic Slumber: Two turns of sleep grants a full long rest — all spell slots, HP, and long-rest abilities back; extends adventuring days, has real in-combat stall combos (banishment/globe), and bypasses story events tied to camping.
notes.Flying: Ten turns of flight — effectively a free Misty Step every turn with no spell slot or concentration cost; the king of mobility, letting you reach archers, cross chasms, and exploit verticality at will.
notes.Universal Resistance: Resistance to ALL damage (including hard-to-get physical) for 10 turns — like a Superior Healing potion every turn pre-cast; fight-winning for AOE-heavy fights or tanking a boss, only held back by rarity/cost.
notes.Gaseous Form: Bonus-action, slot-free Gaseous Form: resistance to all damage, hover over surfaces, slip through grates/gateways, and bait enemies into hazards; the spell's huge resource cost is what normally holds it back, and the potion removes that.
notes.Glorious Vaulting: Enhanced Leap (triple jump distance) for 10 turns — immense pre-combat repositioning power plus dungeon/trap bypass; can't combo with a same-turn jump because both cost a bonus action, keeping it just under S.
notes.Greater Healing: Strictly double Healing (4d4+4, avg 14), but by the time you use it enemies hit hard enough that 14 vs 7 rarely changes how many hits you survive — use the ones you find, but not worth chasing (and oddly costs more to craft than buy).
notes.Remedial: Cures blinded, silenced, paralyzed, and poisoned — the widest-coverage cleanse potion and the best panic button of the antidote family, especially for un-paralyzing an ally before they eat guaranteed crits.
notes.Angelic Reprieve: Two turns of sleep grants a short rest (half HP, first/second-level slots, short-rest abilities); great longevity/quality-of-life tool, especially the Sorlock sorcery-point cheese, though skipping it just means resting more often.
notes.Invisibility: Level-2 Invisibility with no slot or concentration cost: escape a bad spot, bait enemies out of position, set up before combat, or spend a bonus action for an advantage attack where hiding isn't available.
notes.Animal Speaking: Day-long Speak with Animals; mostly roleplay but bypasses some skill checks and saves a spell known/prepared (e.g. frees a Bard's level-1 pick) — worth keeping, not worth chasing.
notes.Antidote: Removes Poisoned and other poison-tagged conditions, including some day-long ones; a fine panic button to carry one or two of, though preempting poison is usually the better play and forgetting it rarely matters.
notes.Feather Fall: Ten turns of Feather Fall; powerful out of combat for bypassing dungeon sections and traps, but the bonus-action-to-drink + bonus-action-to-jump conflict kills its in-combat use, and a wizard usually covers it for free.
notes.Mind Reading: Detect Thoughts for bypassing dialogue/skill checks without spending concentration; useful on a party face that can't otherwise cast it, but a minor, occasional benefit.
notes.Vitality: A strictly-upgraded Antidote that also cures diseases, but BG3 has few diseases so it plays exactly like Antidote in practice — carry whichever you find, don't seek it out.
notes.Basilisk Oil: Reverts petrification, which essentially never happens in combat; purely a completionist tool for a few rare dialogue/loot story beats, so it's safe to sell.
notes.Dream Mist: A drug that forces a Con save or knocks you out for three rounds — no upside, avoid.
notes.Essence of Ether: Puts you to sleep for two rounds with no save and no positive effect (an Angelic Reprieve without the reward) — avoid.
notes.Silkroot: A drug giving three rounds of disadvantage on Dexterity and Charisma checks with no benefit; only a roleplay reason to ever want it — avoid.

notes.+1 Hand Crossbow: Only because there are so few hand crossbows: it's the best one in Act 1, and two of them is often the highest-damage archer setup (dual offhand attacks + doubled Sharpshooter value); the +1 to hit matters enormously for Sharpshooter builds.
notes.Bow of Awareness: A plain shortbow whose passive +1 initiative works even in the unequipped slot — so it goes on a melee character for free initiative; the first initiative gear in the game, and on a d4-rolled initiative that +1 is huge in the brutal early game.
notes.Titanstring Bow: +1 longbow that adds your Strength modifier to damage — and Strength is effectively free via Giant Strength elixirs, so it reads as +1 longbow with +5 damage per shot; the best two-handed damage bow in Act 1 and a long-term mainstay.
notes.Darkfire Shortbow: The first +2 ranged weapon, plus passive fire AND cold resistance and Haste once/day; archers want more damage, but on a melee character it grants free elemental resistance from an otherwise-empty ranged slot — an incredible value pickup.
notes.Hellfire Hand Crossbow: A +2 hand crossbow (upgrade over the +1s) with Scorching Ray and a fire-on-hit-from-stealth rider; the unique effects are mediocre, but hand crossbows get no further upgrades, so this is best-in-slot endgame by default.
notes.Ne'er Misser: A +1 hand crossbow dealing force damage (rarely resisted, converts all rider damage to force) plus Magic Missile; basically a force-damage upgrade preferred over a generic +2 hand crossbow unless you're doubling piercing with the Bhaalist Armour.
notes.Hellrider Longbow: A +1 longbow whose stats don't matter — the passive +3 initiative (and perception advantage) is the point; best-in-slot in the bow slot for any non-archer, an enormous free bonus (would be S+ if the list had one).
notes.The Dead Shot: A +2 longbow with improved crit and your proficiency bonus added twice (≈+4 to hit) on every attack; the author's favored endgame bow — slightly less per-hit damage than Titanstring+Cloud Giant, but far more reliable, so more average damage.
notes.Vicious Shortbow: A +2 shortbow whose +7-damage-on-crit is a passive (works from the unequipped slot), making it a best-in-slot off-hand 'stat stick' for any melee crit build that doesn't need the Hellrider's initiative.
notes.Bow of the Banshee: A +1 shortbow: hits force a DC12 Wisdom save or Frightened (stops movement — great for an archer), plus a passive +1d4 to attack/damage vs frightened enemies that benefits melee fear builds (e.g. GOO warlock) from the off-hand slot.
notes.Harold: A +1 heavy crossbow applying Bane on hit (DC13 Charisma, a weak enemy save, with many tries); spreading a save-lowering debuff sets up your control spells, and it even applies before its own Piercing Shot weapon action.
notes.Least Expected: A +2 shortbow granting +1d4 to hit while obscured (stacks with Bless) plus a Blinding Shot action; lower damage than the top setups, but the best fix at this point for an archer who does big per-hit damage yet struggles to land shots.
notes.+2 Hand Crossbow: Generic, but the +1 to hit/damage over the Ne'er Misser makes it the pick specifically for a Bhaalist-Armour build doubling all piercing damage; otherwise force damage (Ne'er Misser) is usually preferred.
notes.Blightbringer: A +1 shortbow (light on stats) but its crits apply Slow with NO save — and with crit gear an archer crits often, letting you lock down bosses with a powerful debuff they can't resist; great for a crit setup.
notes.Gontr Mael: The only legendary bow: +3 longbow with guiding-bolt-on-hit and at-will Celestial Haste (haste with no lethargy downside); only the third-best two-handed damage bow, but the free permanent haste is valuable on archers and non-archers alike.
notes.Light Crossbow: A simple d8 ranged weapon (strictly better than shortbows) with the solid Piercing Shot action; the early-game ranged option for simple-weapon casters/fighters, equipped widely for the first few levels and a fine off-hand.
notes.Hand Crossbow: The non-magical hand crossbow; since there are so few hand crossbows, your dual-wield users ride this a while, and it's a decent ranged option for a Drow/githyanki who isn't a primary archer.
notes.Gandrel's Aspiration: A +1 heavy crossbow granting advantage vs monstrosities (e.g. bulettes) and Turn Undead once/rest; a strong swap-in for the specific dangerous encounters it's built against, while still doing decent d10 damage.
notes.Giantbreaker: A +1 heavy crossbow applying Reeling on hit (attack-roll penalty); its reeling resets when enemies miss (worse than the Adamantine Shield's version), but it's good against single-attack high-HP bosses where you can re-apply it each turn.
notes.Hunting Shortbow: A +1 shortbow with Hunter's Mark once/rest; often the first magical bow you find and a decent early damage bump if your build lacks Hunter's Mark — replaced once you find a real damage bow.
notes.Hellfire Engine Crossbow: A +2 heavy crossbow with a level-4 Lightning Arrow (real damage) and a no-cooldown DC15 dex save-or-take-half-and-pulled-30ft action; cool for pulling enemies through surfaces, but the fixed low DC limits reliability.
notes.Longbow: Non-magical d8 with Brace (the best default weapon action — reroll damage, take the higher); worth using very early if you have proficiency, but replaced as soon as a magical bow appears.
notes.Crossbow of Arcane Force: A +1 heavy crossbow with Brace, Piercing Shot, and a once/rest +1d4 force damage action; just worse than other Act 1 damage bows, only relevant as a third bow for an unusual all-archer party.
notes.Firestoker: A no-enhancement hand crossbow with +1d4 (slashing) vs burning targets; loses the +1 to hit/damage that archers value most (for Sharpshooter), so it's worse than the +1 hand crossbow except in a non-Sharpshooter build.
notes.Fabricated Arbalest: A +2 heavy crossbow with a clunky mini-Sunbeam action and a bonus-action 1d4 attack that applies one turn of Radiating Orb; only interesting as extra radiating-orb stacking in a themed build.
notes.Shortbow: The weakest bow — d6 damage and the weak Hamstring Shot action; light crossbows are also simple weapons and strictly better, so there's no reason to use a shortbow.
notes.Heavy Crossbow: The best non-magical weapon (d10 + Brace + Piercing Shot), but it only shows up late in Act 1, by which point you'll have better magical ranged options — so it never gets used.
notes.+1 Shortbow: A generic +1 bow; early merchants sell strictly-superior magical versions at the same place, so there's no reason to use the plain +1.
notes.+1 Longbow: Generic +1 with no effect; strictly-superior magical longbows are available from the same early merchants.
notes.+1 Light Crossbow: Generic +1 with no effect; better magical alternatives sell at the same merchants, so it's never needed.
notes.+1 Heavy Crossbow: Generic +1 with no effect; the merchants selling it also stock strictly-better magical heavy crossbows.
notes.Spellthief: A no-enhancement longbow that restores a level-1 slot on a crit once/rest; too situational (needs long days, luck, a bow-firing caster) and low-impact to ever be worth using.
notes.The Joltshooter: A no-enhancement longbow generating lightning charges; effectively a +1 longbow with ~+1-and-a-bit damage, outclassed on both damage and hit chance by bows available earlier and at the same time (and it competes with the better Spellsparkler).
notes.+2 Longbow: Generic +2; if you want damage you take Titanstring, if you want hit chance you take Least Expected/Dead Shot — no reason to use the plain +2.
notes.The Long Arm of the Gur: A +2 heavy crossbow with +1d4 vs undead; even in undead fights it does less damage than the Titanstring or Dead Shot you already have — just worse, so ignore it.

wnames.The Long Arm of the Gur: The Long Arm of the Gur

notes.Caustic Band: +2 acid damage on every weapon attack (melee or ranged) — acid is almost never resisted, so it's near-universal bonus damage available early; goes on whoever makes the most attacks. Flat (not a die), so it isn't doubled on crits.
notes.Crusher's Ring: +10ft movement, stacking with Longstrider — mobility is one of the best stats in honour mode (reach enemies, kite, escape losing fights), and every character benefits, so it's a near-universal early pick.
notes.Ring of Evasion: Reaction to auto-succeed a failed Dexterity save (Gale-origin only) — halve every Fireball and dodge restraint effects at will; a powerful, very early defensive panic button that sticks around a long time.
notes.Ring of Flinging: +1d4 damage on thrown attacks — huge for a throwing-weapon build (applied many times a round) and useful on any melee character carrying backup throwables; boosts one of the game's best strategies.
notes.Ring of Protection: +1 AC and +1 to all saves from a half-slot — premium defensive stats every character wants; put it on your lowest-AC party member to raise the party's floor.
notes.Shapeshifter's Boon Ring: +1d4 to ALL ability checks while disguised/shapeshifted — basically a second, permanent Guidance (stacks with it) if you walk around in Disguise Self; a massive boost to everything you do out of combat.
notes.Strange Conduit Ring: +1d4 psychic on every weapon attack while concentrating on a spell (trivially maintained with a day-long concentration ritual) — even better than the Caustic Band since the die doubles on crits; a top damage ring all game.
notes.The Whispering Promise: Bless (the spell) on anyone you heal — including yourself, and triggerable by thrown potions; near-permanent +1d4 to attacks and saves across the party, the core of healing builds and great even without one.
notes.Callous Glow Ring: +2 radiant damage to any illuminated enemy, per damage instance — easily +10/+20 a round on multi-hit attackers, and being radiant it stacks Radiating Orb; one of the most universally contested items in the game.
notes.Coruscation Ring: Two stacks of Radiating Orb on spell damage (bugged to ignore the light requirement) — the engine of radiating-orb builds (especially with Spirit Guardians), and a free attack-roll debuff for any damage caster.
notes.Risky Ring: Advantage on ALL attacks (and disadvantage on all saves) — permanent free advantage is enormous (more hits, more crits); the save downside is real but offset by going first, killing fast, or save-advantage gear.
notes.Shadow Cloaked Ring: +1d4 damage to obscured enemies on weapon AND unarmed attacks — a die (so it crit-doubles) of bonus damage that's easy to maintain in darkness; can't combine with the Callous Glow Ring (lit vs obscured).
notes.Snowburst Ring: Creates a 15ft ice surface on any cold damage — free prone-inducing, turn-ending ice under enemies (often better than a stun); best-in-slot for any cold-damage character, with prone-immunity needed for melee use.
notes.Band of the Mystic Scoundrel: Cast an illusion/enchantment spell as a bonus action after a weapon hit — effectively permanent Haste for a hybrid attacker/caster (attack, then Command/Hypnotic Pattern), and it pairs perfectly with Arcane Acuity.
notes.Crypt Lord Ring: Create Undead (a level-6 summon, a mummy) once per long rest — a free 6th-level spell slot and a durable extra body; the only non-wizard way to get the spell, great in summon/necromancer parties.
notes.Ring of Feywild Sparks: Guarantees wild-magic procs under Tides of Chaos for a wild sorcerer, but the real draw is an UNLISTED +1 spell save DC for any caster — a save-DC boost from the ring slot, which is otherwise rare; best-in-slot for offensive casters.
notes.Ring of Regeneration: 1d4 healing at the start of each combat turn — minor alone, but it triggers every on-heal effect each turn (permanent Bless via Whispering Promise, permanent Blade Ward via Reviving Hands); a powerful synergy engine.
notes.Bracing Band: +1 AC for a turn whenever you displace an enemy — excellent on a warlock with Repelling Blast (near-constant AC) and decent for any character whose best bonus action is a shove; passive +1 AC rings are stronger, though.
notes.Ring of Absolute Force: +1 thunder damage whenever you deal thunder damage (plus Thunderwave once/rest) — the extra instance can re-trigger reverberation/radiating orb, so it's strong for thunder-based reverberation builds and Tempest clerics.
notes.Ring of Arcane Synergy: Arcane Synergy (add your spellcasting modifier to weapon attacks) on dealing cantrip damage — a big damage boost for pact-of-the-blade warlocks, but the Diadem of Arcane Synergy triggers more easily, so this is the backup/second copy.
notes.Ring of Salving: +2 HP to every heal you give another creature — strong for a dedicated healer (life cleric) and it boosts thrown-potion efficiency; not mandatory, since many healing builds care about the on-heal buffs more than the numbers.
notes.Smuggler's Ring: +2 stealth and +2 Sleight of Hand (-1 Charisma) — a large out-of-combat boost to open almost any lock and to greater-invisibility stealth; swap it off a Bard/warlock before dialogue, but worth carrying all game.
notes.The Sparkswall: Lightning resistance and immunity to electrocution — pairs with the Watersparkers boots (stand in electrified water for free lightning charges each turn without taking the damage), the core of lightning-charge builds; also a swap-in resistance.
notes.Eversight Ring: Blind immunity — lets a non-warlock fight in their own Darkness/Fog Cloud (immune to ranged attacks, melee enemies blinded) and even shoot INTO darkness clouds; mandatory for darkness parties, situational otherwise.
notes.Killer's Sweetheart: Your next weapon attack after a kill is a guaranteed crit (once/rest) — you control it by finishing a low-HP enemy, then landing a guaranteed-crit Smite/sneak attack; very strong for one encounter a day on a big-dice build.
notes.Ring of Spiteful Thunder: Thunder damage to a reverberating enemy forces a con save or Dazed (no reactions) — basically free value for a thunder reverberation build (which already deals thunder), and it applies another condition to stack.
notes.Fetish of Callarduran Smoothhands: Invisibility once per long rest — saves a slot/potion for the strong out-of-combat and pre-combat-positioning uses of invisibility; only one cast a day, so a swap-in utility.
notes.Guiding Light: Light at will — saves a caster's cantrip slot (or carrying torches) on a universally-wanted utility effect; minor but genuinely handy, especially early.
notes.Mage's Friend: +1 Arcana and +1 Religion — unlike Nature/Survival these are heavily-used dialogue skills with some big mechanical checks (esp. an Act 3 religion check), so it's a valuable swap-in for conversations.
notes.Ring of Poison Resistance: Poison resistance — a relatively rare damage type, but resistances are always powerful, so a worthwhile swap-in for the few poison-heavy fights (e.g. Bhaal-adjacent); slightly generous B for the principle.
notes.Ring of Geniality: +1 persuasion and +1 deception — persuasion is a high-value skill and there are important Act 2 persuasion checks (skipping bosses), so a useful dialogue swap-in; a small but real bonus.
notes.Poisoner's Ring: Make up to three enemies poison-vulnerable (DC14 con, no combat start needed) — niche, but with Cloud Kill it doubles a huge poison-damage tick (effectively ~30d8 across three foes); a real build-around for poison casters.
notes.Ring of Mental Inhibition: Mental Fatigue (-mental saves) when an enemy fails a save vs your spell/action — a poor-man's Arcane Acuity for landing Hold Person; hot-fixed to not work with walls/clouds/weapon riders, so it needs a specific stun-then-cast build.
notes.Ring of Shadows: Pass Without Trace (+10 stealth) once/rest — basically mandatory for a greater-invisibility stealth-archer (higher stealth = more attacks before enemies react), niche otherwise.
notes.Shadow Blade Ring: Bonus-action summon a 2d8 psychic shortsword — high base damage but no enhancement (lower hit chance), concentration-dependent, and its advantage-vs-obscured is bugged off; good built around psychic vulnerability (Resonance Stone).
notes.Shifting Corpus Ring: Invisibility and Blur, each once/long rest — saves a level-2 slot on two useful utility/defensive spells; handy to carry, not game-changing.
notes.True Love's Caress: Half of the Warding Bond pair (worn by the protected character) — lets a non-cleric cast Warding Bond, great on a high-HP barbarian or damage-reducing wizard, but the pair costs two ring slots in your active party.
notes.True Love's Embrace: The other half of the Warding Bond pair (the caster) — same verdict: a strong effect (stacks toward quarter damage) but spending two precious ring slots on a level-3 spell is a real cost (better via a camp caster).
notes.Burnished Ring: Paralyzing Ray once/rest — like a no-concentration Hold Person that targets Constitution and works on non-humanoids/undead; set it up with reverberation/bleed to land it on enemies you otherwise couldn't paralyze.
notes.Djinni Ring: Mage Hand once per short rest — Mage Hand is itself once/rest, so this genuinely extends its use across more fights (throw water/potions for free actions); strong for solo/Mage-Hand-abuse builds, low-end B by Act 3.
notes.Ring of Truthfulness: Advantage on Insight checks — valuable because Insight is a hidden roll you can't boost reactively, but you get it in Act 3 when few important Insight checks remain; a dialogue swap-in.
notes.Explorer's Ring: +1 Nature and +1 Survival — two of the least-useful skills (failed Survival digs still work), so only a niche swap-in for a known check; nice for the click-saving of high Survival.
notes.Ring of Elemental Infusion: +1d4 elemental damage on your next weapon attack after dealing elemental spell damage — needs the rare caster-who-also-weapon-attacks, and only fires ~every other turn; extra damage, but hard to leverage.
notes.Ring of Jumping: Enhanced Leap once per short rest — duplicates a ritual spell most parties already have for free; only useful for an oddly-composed party that lacks the spell.
notes.Spurred Band: One turn of momentum (+5ft) each turn you're at/below 50% HP — too small to justify sitting at half HP; its only real use is maintaining max momentum alongside another momentum source on a low-HP build.
notes.Firzu's Ring of Trading: +1 deception (proficiency-coded, so +2 with expertise) — strictly worse than the Ring of Geniality (which also gives persuasion), so rarely worth carrying.
notes.Ring of Free Action: Permanent Freedom of Movement (no difficult-terrain slow, immune to paralysis/restraint) — but it lacks the spell's stun-break panic use and doesn't stop Spike Growth damage; a situational swap-in for hold-person/plant-growth fights.
notes.Ring of Self Immolation: Bonus action to gain Heat (set yourself on fire), dumped as bonus fire damage on a later Fireball — only worthwhile with other Heat sources; otherwise the Callous Glow Ring's constant +2 dominates it.
notes.Ring of Twilight: +1 AC while obscured — strictly worse than the Ring of Protection, and conditional +1 AC isn't enough for the precious, competitive ring slot in Act 2.
notes.After Death Do Us Part: Revive at half HP once/rest, then become uncontrollable (currently bugged to skip the downside, so just a free extra life) — a purely defensive panic button; useful for solo play (prevents game over), but better to not go down at all.
notes.Ring of Blink: Blink once per long rest — a 50% chance to be untargetable each turn; too unreliable to plan around in a party (enemies just hit someone else), but genuinely good in solo play (removes ~half of enemy turns).
notes.Till Death Do Us Part: Beacon of Hope once/rest (advantage on Wisdom saves, maximized healing) — useful in wisdom-save-heavy fights, but Heroes' Feast (which you usually have by Act 3) gives party-wide Wisdom-save advantage; niche for parties without it.
notes.Ring: Non-magical rings have no effect — light and worth selling to merchants, but never worth equipping.
notes.Mattis's Rings: Joke 'rings of resistance to ants' etc. from the Tiefling kid — Easter eggs with no mechanical effect; the author played whole runs wearing them and found nothing.
notes.Ring of Colour Spray: Color Spray once/rest — a one-turn no-save blind, but you want longer blinds, and no-save Fog Cloud (also a free at-will item) does it far better; possibly the most useless magic item in the game.
notes.Ring of Mind Shielding: Advantage on saves vs Charmed — a very narrow effect (and you get it after the harpies, the main early charm threat); Protection from Evil and Good just makes you immune, so no reason to use it.
notes.Tarnished Ring: A little crafting mini-quest produces a ring with no actual mechanical effect (cut content or a data error) — D for its (non-existent) effect.
notes.Family Ring: +2 to death saving throws — you should rarely be rolling death saves, and revivify/resurrection is cheap, so there's no reason to equip it; a flavor item.
notes.Ring of Exalted Marrow: A melee paralysis (unboostable DC13 con) and an undead-only Command, each once/rest — the DC is too low to rely on and the spells are weak for Act 2; great name, mediocre effect.
notes.Guild Ring: A quest ring with no mechanical effect — only 'useful' as Gale food if console-spawned in Act 1.
notes.Keepsake Ring: Dominate Beast once/rest — a poor spell with few valid targets, concentration-dependent and breakable; there are almost no beast fights left by the time you get it in Act 3.
notes.Orphic Ring: Confusion (upcast to 5) once/rest — only ~50% reliable and unpredictable, so a situational backup at best; not worth keeping equipped in advance when scrolls of Confusion are plentiful.
notes.Seemingly Gleaming Ring: +1 Charisma saves but -1 Wisdom saves — you almost never make Charisma saves and Wisdom saves are the most dangerous, so it actively makes your character worse; an F-tier effect if the list had one.

notes.Shield: The plain non-magical shield's +2 AC is deceptively huge — every miss is effectively a stunned enemy turn, low-AC party members are your weak link, and AC compounds the value of HP/healing/resistance; trivially makes the early game near-invincible.
notes.Adamantine Shield: Shield Bash (knock attackers prone, using your spell save DC), Reeling on enemies who miss, and full critical-hit immunity — the best pure-defensive shield in the game all game, removing the random crit spikes that snowball lost fights.
notes.Justiciar's Greatshield: +2 AC, Shield Bash, advantage on perception, and a bonus-action Darkness + auto-hide — casting Darkness as a bonus action is wildly powerful: a panic button granting a turn of ranged immunity and protected concentration.
notes.Ketheric's Shield: +2 AC, Shield Bash, advantage on Dex saves, and +1 spell save DC / spell attack rolls — a top-tier caster bonus (which even improves its own Shield Bash) in a slot that normally can't grant it; the author's second-most-used shield.
notes.Sentinel Shield: +3 initiative, advantage on perception, and Shield Bash — initiative (rolled on a d4) is one of the strongest stats, so this patches a build's initiative entirely, near-replacing Alert; the only downside is there's just one.
notes.Viconia's Walking Fortress: The only legendary shield: +3 AC, improved Shield Bash (prone + 2d4 force), advantage on spell saves with disadvantage on spell attacks against you, and bonus-action Reflective Shell that bounces projectiles back — one of the best tank items, and it wrecks the AI.
notes.Absolute's Warboard: +2 AC and (with the Brand of the Absolute) +1 to all saves plus Heroism once/rest; a solid early upgrade over the basic shield, great for concentration, but not run-defining if you skip it.
notes.Safeguard Shield: +2 AC and +1 to all saving throws with no story requirement — usually your first upgrade over a non-magical shield and a strong, free concentration aid for casters.
notes.Wood Woad Shield: +2 AC and a bonus-action Ensnaring Strike (melee, uses spell save DC) — locks down an enemy for 3 turns (no move, disadvantage on their attacks, advantage on yours); superb on a melee caster hunting enemy archers/casters.
notes.Shield of Scorching Reprisal: Essentially a better +1 Shield: +2 AC, Shield Bash, fire resistance (the most common elemental damage), a bonus-action +1 AC (making it +3), and minor fire retaliation — the go-to for any character that just wants high AC.
notes.Swires' Sledboard: +2 AC, Shield Bash, and 2 stacks/turn of Force Conduit (stacking damage reduction that ticks UP over a fight, with AOE force retaliation at 5+ stacks) — excellent for damage-reduction/abjuration builds that want long fights.
notes.Glowing Shield: +2 AC and 8 temp HP once/rest when hit below 50% HP (self-triggerable); a fine free bonus early but the small temp HP gets obsoleted quickly by Act 1/2 shields.
notes.+1 Shield: +3 AC instead of +2 — a solid, simple upgrade in Act 2, but you'll soon want shields whose effects are worth more than one AC, and the Adamantine Shield is usually the better defensive pick.
notes.Absolute's Protector: Shield Bash and (with the Brand) -1 damage from all spells plus Fire Shield (cold) once/rest; a fine but uncompelling package — mostly for a retaliation/tank build, which often already has Fire Shield.
notes.Shield of Devotion: +2 AC, Shield Bash, an extra level-1 spell slot (good for Paladin Smites), and Aid once/rest (+10 max HP); fine but a weak package — most characters prefer initiative, defenses, or save DC from the S-tier shields.
notes.Shield of the Undevout: +2 AC, Shield Bash, an extra level-1 slot, and disadvantage on enemy saves vs your fear effects — best-in-slot for a Great Old One warlock, but held back because most important Act 3 enemies (where you get it) are fear-immune.
notes.The Real Sparky Sparkswall: +2 AC and a 3-lightning-charge Aura: AOE save-or-1d6, then 3 turns of no-save 1d4/turn that blocks enemy reactions; niche for lightning-charge or 'walking' damage-rider builds, but charges are usually better spent attacking.
notes.Gloomstrand Shield: +2 AC and 'stealth +1' coded as a proficiency bump (so +2 with stealth expertise) — the only shield that boosts stealth, useful solely for a stealth-archer maximizing stealth checks who can spare the slot.
notes.Shield of Shielding: +2 AC, Shield Bash, and the Shield spell once per LONG rest — too infrequent to be exciting in Act 3, and it gives up better passive effects; only worth micro-swapping until you take a hit.
notes.Broken Shield: A training/set-dressing shield giving only +1 AC instead of +2, with no special interactions — safely ignored.
notes.Ironvine Shield: Deals your Wisdom modifier to melee attackers, but only while wielding a Shillelagh'd weapon — an absurdly narrow build that also wants to be hit; even its intended user is better served elsewhere.
notes.Watcher's Shield: +2 AC and advantage on perception and nothing else — and that perception bonus is already bundled onto the far better Justiciar's Greatshield and Sentinel Shield.
notes.Abdel's Trusted Shield: +2 AC, Shield Bash, and Protection from Missiles once/rest, but it costs an action and concentration for only ~50% ranged protection — the bonus-action Justiciar's Greatshield Darkness does it better (100%).

subject: Simple weapons (clubs, maces, sickles, javelins, handaxes, light hammers, torches)

notes.Loviatar's Scourge: A no-enhancement mace dealing 2d6 (the highest-damage simple weapon for a long while) plus +1d6 necrotic AOE to everything in melee on each hit, with necrotic resistance offsetting the self-damage; an early-game power spike, barterable/pickpocketable in the goblin camp.
notes.Shattered Flail: A +2 mace (very strong this early) that heals 1d6 on every hit — incredible free sustain, especially with cleave/multi-target attacks and on-heal synergies; the catch is a madness debuff if you go a turn without hitting, easily managed with Wisdom-save gear.
notes.The Blood of Lathander: A legendary +3 mace that passively blinds nearby undead/fiends (a condition, so it stacks radiating orb), revives you once/rest, and casts level-6 Sunbeam once/rest — a level-6 spell in Act 1; any one of its effects would justify S.
notes.Deva Mace: Glitch-only, but the highest-damage weapon in the game: d6 + 4d8 radiant on every hit, one-handed (dual-wieldable); not intended to be obtainable, but if you exploit it in you get absurd, build-warping damage output.
notes.Devotee's Mace: A legendary +3 mace with +1d8 radiant (great for radiating-orb clerics) and a once/rest Healing Incense aura (party-wide 1d4/turn for 10 turns that triggers on-heal items) — pairs with Blood of Lathander (cast Sunbeam, then swap to this).
notes.+1 Javelin: A magical thrown weapon with one of the highest thrown damage dice; throwing-weapon characters want as many magical throwables as possible (their main early damage before a returning weapon), so stockpile these.
notes.+1 Handaxe: Same value as the +1 Javelin — a high-damage-die magical thrown weapon to stockpile for throwers (and any melee character wanting a ranged option); the +N adds to thrown damage even though not to the thrown attack roll.
notes.Club of Hill Giant Strength: A plain club that sets Strength to 19 — competes with the (stronger) Giant Strength elixirs, but it's great as an off-hand stat stick for a Tavern Brawler monk or on a Titanstring archer, freeing the elixir slot for Bloodlust/Vigilance/etc.
notes.Ritual Axe: A no-enhancement handaxe that applies Bane (-1d4 to enemy attacks AND saves) with NO save on every hit — a powerful, condition-counting debuff that sets up control spells; you take 1d6 on hit above 50% HP, mitigable with resistance.
notes.Shining Staver-of-Skulls: A +1 light hammer with +1d4 radiant and a light radius; a great throwable to keep on hand (extra damage die, applies radiating orb) and a bound-weapon option for an Eldritch Knight — most parties should carry it.
notes.Xyanyde: A +1 mace whose once/rest effect, on a MISS, applies Faerie Fire with no save — granting your whole party advantage against that enemy for two turns; a uniquely reliable (miss-triggered, no-save) effect, great swapped in for single-boss fights.
notes.Shadow Lantern: A story club (poor for attacking) that conjures a Shadow once/rest — a strong summon (high damage, strength-drain) for the cost of holding the weapon; great on characters who don't need their weapon slot, swap away if it dies.
notes.Handaxe: A light, thrown d6 — the highest light-weapon damage die, so dual-wieldable, but mainly valued (with daggers/javelins) as a thrown-weapon stockpile, including to open combat without losing your returning weapon to the recall bug.
notes.Javelin: A thrown d6 with Piercing Strike; same stockpile value as the handaxe for throwers and as a backup ranged option for any party member (you rarely activate its melee weapon action since you're throwing it).
notes.+1 Mace: A real upgrade over the base mace for a shield-using simple-weapon character (e.g. cleric) who actually makes weapon attacks; worth it only if they swing often, otherwise skippable.
notes.Dragon's Grasp: A no-enhancement handaxe with +1d4 vs burning enemies; the situational extra die is nice in a fire-themed party that burns enemies regularly, but most parties get more from a plain +1 handaxe's accuracy.
notes.Torch: Lit, it deals d4 + d4 fire — the best one-handed damage from the very start — plus it lights areas and ignites surfaces, and works with Shillelagh; all duplicable (fire-dip any weapon, Light cantrip), but a handy all-in-one to carry if you don't micro-dip.
notes.Ironwood Club: A +1 club that adds +1d4 when Shillelagh'd (d8 + 1d4 + fire-dip) and grants Back Breaker (prone); a solid damage option for a Shillelagh nature cleric/Druid or dual-wielding Spore Druid, though many prefer a magical staff.
notes.Wavemother's Sickle: A +2 sickle with +1d4 cold and advantage vs Wet enemies; decent damage that doubles via cold vulnerability, a reasonable dual-wield pick for a Spore Druid without shortsword proficiency.
notes.Mace: The non-magical d6 mace with Concussive Smash; the best simple one-hander for an early shield-user with only simple proficiency, but you'll never equip a plain mace later — and may prefer a spear/quarterstaff anyway.
notes.Adamantine Mace: A +1 mace that auto-crits objects and makes ALL your bludgeoning damage ignore resistance — useless as a weapon, but a niche off-hand stat stick for a monk's unarmed strikes; costs an adamantine forge (better spent on armor).
notes.Redcap Sickle: A no-enhancement sickle dealing 2d4 (fire-dippable); a touch more base damage than other early weapons, but most characters prefer the accuracy/enhancement of a +1 weapon (especially Spore Druids landing their passive damage).
notes.Salami: Mechanically just a club, BUT it's both a weapon and a Camp Supply — so a Chimpanzee-aspect Barbarian throwing it blinds enemies, and an Eldritch Knight can bind it for a returning blind-thrower ('Salami Slinger'); a real, if goofy, niche build.
notes.Sickle of Boooal: A 2d4 sickle with advantage vs bleeding enemies; setting up bleed usually costs an action and the base weapon is weak by the time you get it, so it's mainly relevant as the source of its permanent story buff.
notes.Comeback Handaxe: A joke +1 returning handaxe that hits YOU on return and goes to inventory (not re-equipping) — uniquely, that lets you throw it while keeping two passive stat-stick weapons equipped (e.g. Club of Hill Giant Strength); niche but real.
notes.Handmaiden's Mace: A +2 mace with +1d6 poison that sets Strength to 18; redundant with the Club of Hill Giant Strength / elixirs, and arrives late when +2 + poison is unexciting (many Act 3 enemies are poison-immune) — only for no-elixir or poison-synergy builds.
notes.Club: A light d4 with Concussive Smash, but strictly outclassed — dual-wield handaxes (d6) or use a mace instead; the base club never sees play.
notes.Light Hammer: A light, thrown d4 — strictly better than a club (can throw it), but its low die and arcing throw make handaxes/javelins/daggers better throwables, so no reason to use it.
notes.Sickle: A light d4 with the weak Lacerate action — effectively a worse club/light hammer (already D), so unsurprisingly D too.
notes.+1 Club: A generic +1 club; still outclassed by handaxes/maces, and the +1 to hit isn't worth the money over carrying a thrown weapon — safely ignored.
notes.+1 Light Hammer: Since the enhancement doesn't apply to thrown attack rolls, it throws like a plain handaxe/javelin while being a worse melee weapon — same D tier as the +1 club.
notes.Ceremonial Mace: A +1 mace used in a story event; by the time you get it you have far better options and could've bought a +1 mace long ago — D for weapon use.
notes.Skybreaker: A +1 light hammer with Searing Smite once/rest; you give up too much damage for one fire-Smite a day, and a scroll/grenade does burst better — leave it home.
notes.Sussur Sickle: A +1 sickle that silences on a DC12 con save — fine, but it's the worst of the three Sussur weapons: the dagger is strictly better (finesse, better action) and the greatsword hits harder, so there's no reason to pick the sickle.
notes.Moonlantern: A story-event club with no magical effect; useful only for the story, not as a weapon.
notes.Wulbren's Hammer: A +1 light hammer with +2d4 force vs objects only; breaking doors faster isn't a meaningful combat effect, so it's low-damage and skippable.
notes.+2 Mace: A generic +2 mace found only late; you'll already have a +3 legendary mace, so there's no reason to use it.
notes.Infernal Mace: A +2 mace with a flat 3 poison on hit — duplicated and bettered by the Handmaiden's Mace (1d6 + strength set), and far too little damage for Act 3.
notes.Torch of Revocation: A +1 torch adding necrotic + fire (3d4+1 lit) — a fun two-element idea, but you can replicate it by fire-dipping a better weapon, and the damage is unexciting for most Act 3 characters.

notes.Stealth: By itself can win the game: with a high enough score (pass without trace, invisibility, racial advantage) you make undetected attacks and end encounters before they start by picking off enemies — the backbone of stealth-archer solo runs.
notes.Athletics: Powers the bonus-action shove (reposition or one-shot enemies into chasms/ice) AND resists being shoved, so it secures both your bonus action and your attacks of opportunity — useful on any character, even low-strength ones.
notes.Sleight of Hand: Picks locks, disarms traps, and picks pockets (near-infinite money from restocking merchants); disarming traps on the first try is critical on honour mode where there's no reason to eat avoidable trap damage.
notes.Perception: Detects traps (and the rare stealthed enemy) — finding and safely disarming traps, some of which can end a run, is critically important on honour mode; comes up constantly and is the best generically useful skill.
notes.Persuasion: By far the most common dialogue skill (293 checks in Act 1) and usually offered wherever Deception/Intimidation are, so it covers nearly all social checks and gates the game's most important ones — important if you want any dialogue, but not strictly critical.
notes.Arcana: The most frequent of the five intelligence skills (~50 Act-1 checks) and the game often offers it as an alternative to History/Religion checks, so it tends to supersede the other knowledge skills.
notes.Medicine: Mostly scripted (including an early advantage to save Us on the nautiloid), but a level-6 transmutation wizard uses it to brew TWO potions instead of one — doubling output of Cloud Giant Strength, Bloodlust, Speed, etc.; A only on that Alchemist, otherwise ~C.
notes.Acrobatics: Helps resist being shoved (but never shove), with essentially no dialogue checks; useful when it comes up, but the ranged characters who get it rarely mind being pushed — much weaker than its counterpart Athletics.
notes.Religion: A knowledge skill that'd otherwise be C, bumped up because several early Religion checks grant Inspirations, which are especially valuable in Act 1 for re-rolling critical checks like disarming a trap.
notes.Intimidation: A common dialogue skill (209 Act-1 checks), but most of its checks are also covered by Persuasion, so it's useful rather than essential.
notes.Deception: Comes up less often than Intimidation (147 checks) but tends to gate more important checks, and along with Persuasion it's the usual way to talk your way out of being arrested (an unscripted use).
notes.Performance: A few good scripted dialogue uses plus an unscripted draw — performing pulls NPCs over to watch (a Minor Illusion stand-in for distracting marks) and earns a little money; borderline B/C.
notes.Insight: A scripted-only skill that comes up far less than the Charisma dialogue skills, rarely grants Inspiration, and usually just provides an edge or info you could get from a Persuasion/Deception check anyway.
notes.History: A scripted-only knowledge skill (~30-40 Act-1 checks) that's frequently superseded by an alternative Arcana check; fine to skip if you have one intelligence skill covered.
notes.Investigation: Another scripted-only intelligence skill of similar frequency, generally redundant with Arcana and skippable.
notes.Nature: A scripted-only knowledge skill, again often offered as an alternative to Arcana, so it's marginal once you have one intelligence skill.
notes.Animal Handling: Effectively 100% bypassable — Speak with Animals (a free potion or party spell) covers essentially every animal-handling check in the game, so there's no reason to invest.
notes.Survival: Only used to locate buried loot chests, and you can just grab a shovel and dig at the failed-check spot anyway; the random loot is unimportant and money is a non-issue, so never invest points here.

notes.Corellon's Grace: A magical quarterstaff (no enhancement) granting +1 unarmed attack/damage and, while unarmored, +2 to all saves — excellent early for monks who haven't taken (or won't take) Tavern Brawler, offering both offense and strong early-game defense.
notes.Melf's First Staff: +1 quarterstaff with +1 spell save DC and spell attack rolls (the single most important caster stat) plus a free Melf's Acid Arrow; best-in-slot for most spellcasters across all of Acts 1-2.
notes.Mourning Frost: +1d4+1 cold damage on hit, makes enemies Chilled (cold-vulnerable, enabling freeze-stuns with Wet), boosts all your cold damage, and grants Ray of Frost at will — the premier cold-damage staff, best-in-slot for cold builds and a common off-hand all game.
notes.Markoheshkir: The only legendary staff: +1 save DC/attack, a free 6th-level spell once/day (Arcane Battery), AND elemental attunement (lightning gives Chain Lightning once/short rest) — best-in-slot for essentially every spellcaster; builds are designed around maximizing it.
notes.Staff of Cherished Necromancy: +2 staff, +1d4 necrotic, enemies have disadvantage vs your necromancy, and killing with a spell grants a free necromancy spell — a self-sustaining engine of free 6th-level casts, the only staff competitive with Markoheshkir (great dual-wielded with it).
notes.Staff of Spellpower: A strictly-worse Markoheshkir (no attunement) — but still busted: +1 save DC/attack and a free spell once/day; an extra 6th-level cast daily for any spellcaster, used to Nova or to bank a day-long buff.
notes.Staff of Arcane Blessing: Free Bless once/rest plus, when you Bless, an extra +1d4 to spell attack rolls (the tooltip lies that the whole bonus is additive); excellent for any party making frequent spell attacks (Eldritch Blast, Scorching Ray, cantrips).
notes.The Spellsparkler: Grants 2 lightning charges whenever you deal spell/weapon damage (+1 to hit, +1 lightning/instance, 1d8 at 5 stacks); great on multi-attack casters like warlocks and fire sorcerers, and scales up as they fire more beams late-game.
notes.Caitiff Staff: +2 staff with +1 save DC/attack and restores an expended warlock spell slot once/rest; a strict upgrade over Melf's for warlocks (and Sorlock sorcery-point fuel), plus a solid +save-DC off-hand for dual-wield staff builds.
notes.Staff of Interruption: Counterspell at level 5 once/rest — lets a non-caster (e.g. an archer) carry the game's best defensive reaction; a great safety swap-in against enemy casters and a fine off-hand for any spellcaster. Underrated.
notes.Staff of the Emperor: +2 staff, +1 save DC/attack, and whenever you succeed on a save the caster must pass an Intelligence save (a weak save) or be stunned — free, action-less stuns; superb on a high-AC/high-save character enemies can only target with spells.
notes.Quarterstaff: The base non-magical staff: two-handed d8 is the highest-damage simple weapon (fire-dippable), and Topple knocks prone — a surprisingly good melee pick for simple-weapon characters in the very early game, swapped out ASAP.
notes.Cacophony: +1 staff with +1d4 thunder and Thunderous Smite once/rest; a reasonable weapon for an attacking monk (though Smite competes with Flurry of Blows) and an early thunder source for reverberation builds — situational.
notes.Gold Wyrmling Staff: +1 staff, +1d4 fire (doesn't stack with fire-dipping, so no real damage gain), but Firebolt at will is a genuine cantrip upgrade for Druids/clerics/bards lacking good damage cantrips — a fine early pick for a melee-capable caster like a Spore Druid.
notes.Incandescent Staff: +1 ranged spell attack, fire resistance, Firebolt at will, and Fireball once/rest; the spell-attack bonus is beaten by Melf's/Spellsparkler, but the free Fireball is a worthwhile swap-in for one Act 2 encounter, or a decent off-hand staff.
notes.Hollow's Staff: +1 staff, +1d4 necrotic, and disadvantage on enemy saves vs your necromancy; mostly just lands more damage spells, but pairs well with a debuff like Eyebite — generously B for an Eyebite-focused build.
notes.Woe: +2 staff, +1 save DC/attack, free Blight once/rest, and 1d4 heal whenever an enemy fails a save vs your spells; a fine all-rounder, but essentially the fifth-best of the +save-DC staffs, outclassed by Markoheshkir/Cherished Necromancy/Emperor.
notes.Nature's Snare: Hitting an enemy forces a DC12 strength save or Ensnared (a strong lockdown condition); used to apply on any damage you dealt (a great bug, now fixed), so as a no-enhancement staff it's hard to land — only for a themed ensnaring build.
notes.Pale Oak: No enhancement; immunity to druidic Vines and Entangle once/rest — both weak effects, since few enemies entangle you; a generous C for a niche build that stands inside its own Entangle.
notes.Despair of Athkatla: +2 staff with +1 save DC/attack — fine, but it's the worst and hardest-to-get of the many staves granting that effect, so only relevant if you run multiple casters who all want it (e.g. an all-wizard party).
notes.Harper Sacredstriker: +1 staff with a level-6 Spiritual Weapon once/rest; the upcast (3d8/turn, bonus action) is okay damage but Act 3 enemies are too mobile and fights too short for it to do much — a marginal swap-in or second weapon.
