notes.Staff of the Ram: +2 staff that once/turn forces a DC8 con save (surely bugged-low) or knockback + stun; at this point enemies auto-pass DC8 even with disadvantage, so you'd need heavy reverberation stacking, and it costs you a real weapon's damage.
notes.+1 Quarterstaff: A generic +1 staff; staff-wielding casters rarely attack with it, and the few who do have far better weapon options even from the start — no reason to buy it.
notes.Creation's Echo: No enhancement; grants resistance to the elemental damage type you just dealt (not cold), but you must trigger it, it's purely defensive, and it eats your Elixir slot — just stockpile resistance elixirs instead.
notes.Rain Dancer: No enhancement unless you cast Create Water once/rest — an effect a bottle of water duplicates, so spending a weapon slot (that you must buy) on it isn't worth it; mainly for camp showers.
notes.Staff of Crones: No enhancement unless you cast Ray of Sickness once/rest — a weak level-1 spell that's usually worse than whatever else you'd do, and the staff cripples your stats otherwise; a grenade or scroll beats it.
notes.Bigby's Chew Toy: +1 staff with Enlarge once/rest; a level-2 spell is unexciting in Act 2, and a +1 quarterstaff isn't a weapon the enlarge-wanting attacker actually wants — no real use case.
notes.+2 Quarterstaff: A generic +2 staff (Act 3); you aren't attacking with staves by then, and the characters who do are better off with Mourning Frost — same verdict as the +1 generic.
notes.Staff of a Mumbling Wizard: A joke item: 'Firebolt?' at will (always 1d10, 1-in-20 chance to be a Fireball, and counts as an enemy failed-save 19/20 times for odd triggers); the weak base spell makes none of the quirky interactions worth your action.

notes.Blooded Greataxe: A +1 greataxe with +1d4 when at/below half HP, buyable from a merchant with no fights — usually the first real two-handed upgrade you get; works either as a plain +1 axe or a damage-boosted weapon if you start combat below half.
notes.Everburn Blade: A no-enhancement greatsword with +1d4 fire on every hit, looted on the nautiloid — the first magical weapon and a strict upgrade over a starting greataxe; a big damage bump unless you meticulously fire-dip every weapon (then it's merely redundant).
notes.Githyanki Greatsword: The looted (githyanki-patrol) version: a +1 greatsword that deals +1d4 psychic when wielded by a gith (or via Disguise Self), making it a strict upgrade over the Blooded Greataxe and Everburn Blade — the best weapon you have at that point.
notes.Soulbreaker Greatsword: A +1 greatsword with gith psychic bonus, +2 initiative, and a once/rest action that stuns for two turns — best two-handed weapon in Act 1 and for many characters most of the game; the initiative patches a paladin's weakness and helps land the stun first.
notes.Sword of Justice: A +1 greatsword granting Shield of Faith (+2 AC) as a bonus action once/rest — and since two-handed users rarely need concentration, it's effectively a permanent +2 AC; the defensive counterpart to the Blooded Greataxe, available very early.
notes.Balduran's Giantslayer: A legendary +3 greatsword that DOUBLES your strength modifier to damage (≈+16 with a Cloud Giant elixir), advantage vs large+ enemies, a free Enlarge, and a prone action — the best, highest-damage two-handed weapon in the game for single targets.
notes.Hellfire Greataxe: A +2 greataxe with +1d6 fire, Heat stacking, and a once/rest Hellflame Cleave that leaves a 6d6/turn Hellfire field — the best AOE-damage weapon in the game (and, being coded as a cantrip, a sorcerer can even Quicken it).
notes.Hamarhraft: A no-enhancement maul dealing 1d4 thunder AOE whenever you jump, with no once-per-turn limit — a monk (Step of the Wind) or anyone spamming the Fly action can trigger dozens of shockwaves a turn for record-setting damage; even casually, it turns a Flying potion into a bomb.
notes.Jorgoral's Greatsword: A +1 greatsword with a once/rest Colossal Onslaught — a fireball-sized line attack (weapon damage, half on a miss) that gives single-target melee characters a strong AOE option; worth swapping in for crowds.
notes.Sussur Greatsword: A +1 greatsword forcing a DC12 con save or Silenced for two turns on hit; great for locking down enemy spellcasters and bosses with non-spell legendary actions — the two-handed pick of the Sussur flower weapons (take the dagger if you can't use this).
notes.Svartlebee's Woundseeker: A +1 greatsword granting +1d4 to hit against enemies that have already taken damage (easy to set up) — accuracy is hugely valuable for Great Weapon Master users; great for high-AC, high-HP boss fights, available in Act 1.
notes.Punch-Drunk Bastard: A +1 greatclub (with Cleave) that, while drunk, grants advantage on all attacks and a 1d4 thunder AOE shockwave on every hit — combined with Cleave it instantly stacks max reverberation, one of the best ways to apply it; enables a drunk-reverb build.
notes.Silver Sword of the Astral Plane: A legendary +3 greatsword with gith psychic bonus, advantage on mental saves (for gith), and the Soulbreaker two-turn stun action; excellent and accurate, but more of a defensive endgame pick than the higher-damage Giantslayer/Hellfire Greataxe.
notes.Sword of Chaos: A +2 greatsword with +1d4 necrotic that heals 1d6 on every hit — strong sustain, especially on AOE/cleave attacks and damage-halving barbarians, and it triggers on-heal item synergies; a solid default and enabler of healing builds.
notes.Greataxe: Non-magical d12 with a great chassis (Cleave, plus Prepare to double your strength mod to damage), and a barbarian's starting weapon — the highest-damage weapon you can begin the game with, very useful on the nautiloid before upgrades.
notes.Exterminator's Axe: A no-enhancement greataxe with +1d6 fire vs plants, myconids, and small enemies (which includes many Act 1 goblins/underdark foes), and the bonus even stacks with fire-dipping — a worthwhile swap-in for those common encounters.
notes.Corpsegrinder: A +2 maul with +1d4 thunder and a once/rest 10ft AOE attack that shoves enemies (by your shove distance) — a solid weapon with a relevant damage type and a mini-fireball, though most prefer the Soulbreaker when it's available.
notes.Greatsword: Non-magical 2d6 (highest, most consistent base damage) with Cleave, Lacerate, and a bonus-action Pummel Strike (daze) — a great chassis, but you can't start with one, so you'll just upgrade to a magical weapon ASAP.
notes.Maul: Non-magical 2d6 with one of the best weapon-action sets (Tenacity, Concussive Smash, and Back Breaker prone); an excellent chassis you'll only use briefly since you don't start with it.
notes.Doom Hammer: A no-enhancement maul applying Bone Chill (no healing; disadvantage for undead) on hit; situationally good against healers/undead, but it hits less and does less than other great weapons, and an Arrow of Ilmater or the cantrip applies the effect with less opportunity cost.
notes.Argument Solver: A +1 greatclub (with the maul's full weapon actions) dealing +proficiency poison and creating poison clouds (DC11 con save) on hit; the low damage and low DC make it weak now, but it's a cute base for a poison-cloud themed build.
notes.Rat Bat: A +1 greatclub with advantage vs beasts and an undocumented +1d6 piercing; decent damage for a simple-weapon-only two-handed user, but mostly notable for being absurdly buggy (record damage) below honour mode — niche on honour.
notes.Sethan: A +2 greataxe granting a level-6 Spiritual Weapon (greataxe) once/rest plus Reduce; a moderately useful bonus-action spell you might swap in when desperate for long-rest resources, but usually a more powerful weapon is better.
notes.Greatclub: Non-magical d8 simple weapon with okay actions (Tenacity, Concussive Smash), but simple-weapon users do better with a quarterstaff and martial users with a higher-damage weapon — lowest damage in the category.
notes.+1 Greataxe: A generic +1 greataxe; early merchants and encounters provide strictly-better or equal magical greataxes, so there's no reason to buy it.
notes.+1 Greatclub: A generic +1 greatclub; outclassed by better options available right from the start of the game.
notes.+1 Greatsword: A generic +1 greatsword; the Everburn Blade and other early magical greatswords are strictly better, so it's never needed.
notes.+1 Maul: A generic +1 maul; better magical two-handed weapons are available from the very start, so there's no reason to use it.
notes.Very Heavy Greataxe: A +1 greataxe whose 'special' action is just Cleave plus +1d6 and a self-debuff (fall-prone-when-hit) — a strictly worse version of an action every greataxe already has; funny name, terrible weapon.
notes.Defender Greataxe: A +2 greataxe that can drop to +1 for +1 AC/saves; a defensive bonus is the wrong fit for a two-handed damage weapon (use a shield instead), and the save bonus isn't active at combat start when it matters most.
notes.+2 Greataxe: A generic +2 greataxe found only at the very end; by then you have far better weapons, so it's pointless.
notes.Foebreaker: A +2 maul that ignores bludgeoning resistance; resistance is better bypassed with a different damage type or damage riders, and there's no encounter where this beats your other weapons — safely ignored.
notes.The Undead Bane: A +1 greataxe with +1d6 vs fiends/undead and a once/rest 2d6 + Bane action; even in its target encounters the bonus is weaker than generically powerful weapons (e.g. the Soulbreaker's stun), so the specialist loses out.

subject: Versatile weapons (longswords, warhammers, battleaxes, tridents, katanas)
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, versatile weapons (act 2-3)

notes.Larethian's Wrath: A +1 longsword that's also FINESSE (use Dexterity), with a once/rest 13ft-cone AOE that deals weapon damage (half on a miss); being finesse lets it go on builds that couldn't otherwise use a martial weapon — one of the best one-handers in Act 1.
notes.Phalar Aluve: A +1 finesse longsword with two once/rest modes: Sing (stacks with Bless: +1d4 to your party's attacks/saves) and the broken Shriek (+1d4 thunder to enemies whenever they take ANY damage — fires several times per attack). Usable by any party; ~+40 damage/turn even unoptimized.
notes.Voss's Silver Sword (Act 3): The same +2 longsword (+1d4 psychic, +1d4 attack/damage vs gith/aberration/fiend/elemental — a passive that helps even archers) but acquired in Act 3, where you have stronger default weapons — so a solid swap-in for those common Act 3 fights rather than a mainstay.
notes.Charge-Bound Warhammer: A plain +1 warhammer, but bound by an Eldritch Knight / pact-of-the-blade warlock it becomes +2 with +1d6 lightning on every hit — best-in-slot melee damage for a bound-weapon character at the start of Act 2.
notes.Dwarven Thrower: A +2 returning (homing) warhammer that, as a dwarf (or disguised as one), deals +1d8 (and +2d8 vs large+ enemies) — the best single-target throwing damage in the game; pair it with Nyrulna (use this for big single targets, that for AOE).
notes.Nyrulna: A legendary +3 returning trident: +1d6 thunder, a 20ft thunder explosion on throw, +10ft move/jump and no fall damage, light, and two strong spells — incredible thrown OR as a one-handed weapon; a contender for best weapon in the game.
notes.Trident of the Depths: Exploit-only +3 trident with +1d6 cold and an at-will Hailstrike (+1d10 cold, applies Chilled → freeze-stun with Wet) — enormous for a character WITHOUT extra attack (e.g. a Spore Druid), since the action-cost weapon action replaces the missing attack.
notes.+1 Trident: Worth collecting because tridents are thrown weapons (full damage thrown, and a backup since returning weapons bug out when opening combat) AND it carries the unique Disarming Strike action — a powerful once/rest disarm on a stockpile-able weapon.
notes.Faithbreaker: A +1 warhammer with FOUR short-rest weapon actions (so a good option nearly every turn), including one that doubles your strength-to-damage plus +1d6 force and a 17ft knockback — the best one-handed weapon for much of the early game.
notes.Intransigent Warhammer: A no-enhancement warhammer that knocks all nearby enemies prone (spell-save DC) on a crit OR kill — superb on a crit-focused GOO warlock (prone + frightened = a one-hit stun) or a maim-build tiger barbarian; the passive lockdown outweighs the missing +1.
notes.Azer Warhammer: Exploit-only no-enhancement warhammer with an at-will bonus action doing ~2d10 fire AOE (1d10 hit + 1d10/turn burning) in a 7ft radius — great for a melee AOE-fire character who runs in and explodes; worth giving up an enhancement bonus for.
notes.Clown Hammer: A +2 warhammer where crits force a DC17 Tasha's Hideous Laughter (a 2-turn stun many late bosses like Raphael AREN'T immune to) — the catch (you can be laughed too) is nullified by the Clown Face Paint's free immunity; brutal on a crit/GOO build.
notes.Duke Ravengard's Longsword: A +1 longsword granting +2 Charisma (up to 24); +2 Charisma is often worth a weapon slot by itself — great on a Charisma caster/sword-bard archer who doesn't otherwise need their melee weapon. (Its Commander's Strike is best ignored.)
notes.Hammer of the Just: A +2 warhammer with +1d4 radiant (easy radiating-orb/reverb triggers) and +1d6 vs fiends/undead — a solid pick for radiant builds and for some of the game's hardest fiend/undead encounters.
notes.Orphic Hammer: A legendary +3 warhammer with advantage on spell saves and an at-will action to remove restrained/paralyzed/stunned from an ally — a powerful 'un-stun' safety button (story-critical too) that can avert party wipes; defensive, so a support pick.
notes.Voss's Silver Sword: Acquired in Act 1, this +2 longsword (+1d4 psychic, +1d4 attack/damage vs gith/aberration/fiend/elemental — about half the game's enemies, passive even for archers) is a standout that carries you through Acts 1-2 and into 3.
notes.Witchbreaker: A +1 battleaxe granting advantage vs concentrating enemies (passive, works with spells/bows) plus a once/rest silence; good against enemy casters, but you should be preventing enemies from casting in the first place, so it comes up less than you'd think.
notes.Blade of Oppressed Souls: A +1 longsword with +1d4 psychic and a once/rest Crown of Madness (Int save) that turns an enemy on its allies — situational and risky (the maddened foe targets the nearest creature, often you), but a fun, occasionally powerful effect.
notes.Thermodynamo Axe: A +1 battleaxe that builds Heat on damage, discharged for bonus fire damage (and Cleave to spread it); only really worth it for a dedicated Heat-convergence AOE build — otherwise a default powerful weapon is better.
notes.Blackguard's Sword: A +2 longsword with +1d4 necrotic that dazes on a Smite (DC13 con, easily passed); a fine passive bonus for a smiting paladin who wants to deny enemy reactions, but lower base damage than top Act 3 weapons and it doesn't work with Smite spells.
notes.Sword of the Emperor: A +2 longsword with +1d4 vs shapeshifters and a flat +2 to saves vs spells; the +2 saves can make a character very spell-resilient (stacks with advantage), but it's a defensive effect on a slot you'd usually want for damage.
notes.Trident of the Waves: A +1 trident that applies Wet and a water surface on hit — at-will Wet (double cold/lightning damage, freeze combos) is genuinely strong, but it doesn't trigger when thrown and lowers your damage, so it's a niche synergy weapon.
notes.Vicious Battleaxe: A +2 battleaxe adding a flat +7 on any crit (works from the unequipped slot, stacks with Dolor Amarus for +14) — decent as an off-hand crit stat stick on a dual-wielder archer, but most crit builds prefer extra damage dice or crit chance.
notes.Longsword: Non-magical d8/d10 with great action economy (Rush Attack to dash+attack, bonus-action Pummel Strike daze); the Fighter's starting weapon and fine very early, but you'll upgrade to a magical weapon ASAP.
notes.Trident: Non-magical, lower damage (d6/d8) but thrown, and crucially the only weapon with the Disarming Strike action — a very powerful once/rest disarm that makes the base trident worth using in some early fights.
notes.Warhammer: Non-magical d8/d10 with arguably the best base action set (Concussive Smash daze, Weakening Strike, Back Breaker prone); the Paladin's starting weapon and a strong early chassis until you find a magical one.
notes.+1 Warhammer: The best of the generic +1 versatile weapons and a reasonable early-merchant buy when you can't find a Morning Star or a good two-hander; you'll swap it out for Phalar Aluve or another magical weapon soon.
notes.The Sparky Points: A no-enhancement trident generating lightning charges on hit (effectively a +1 trident plus a little extra damage), but it costs you the quest choice of the much more useful Spell Sparkler — so rarely worth it.
notes.Cruel Sting: A +1 longsword with a once/rest Ensnaring Strands (web) and +1d4 poison vs restrained enemies; the spell is a weak level-1 effect by Act 2, and bonus damage against an already-locked-down enemy doesn't accomplish much — niche.
notes.Battleaxe: Non-magical; a solid chassis (Cleave, Maiming Strike for an on-demand stun combo), but by the time you'd pick one up you're upgrading to magical weapons anyway.
notes.+1 Battleaxe: A generic +1 battleaxe — one-handed users prefer a Morning Star and two-handed users want a greatweapon, so the plain +1 has no niche.
notes.+1 Longsword: A generic +1 longsword; outclassed by the Morning Star (one hand) or a greatweapon (two hands), with no special effect to justify it.
notes.Adamantine Longsword: Ignores slashing resistance and auto-crits objects, but it costs an adamantine forge far better spent on armor/shields — and the adamantine scimitar/sickle does the resistance trick as a light weapon, so this is never the pick.
notes.Ceremonial Battleaxe: A story-puzzle item that's otherwise just a generic +1 battleaxe acquired late — no special properties, so D for weapon use.
notes.Ceremonial Longsword: Like the other ceremonial weapons: a generic +1 longsword for a story puzzle, outclassed by what you already have.
notes.Ceremonial Warhammer: A generic +1 warhammer used in a story puzzle; no combat reason to use it over your existing weapons.
notes.Githyanki Longsword: Appears as a non-magical or +1 longsword identical to the generic versions, and only after you already have those — so there's never a need for it.
notes.Ketheric's Warhammer: A +1 warhammer with +1d4 psychic and no other effect; it's available in the same place as the almost-strictly-better Blade of Oppressed Souls, so there's no reason to use it.
notes.+2 Longsword: A generic +2 longsword; by the time it appears you have far better magical options, so it's skippable.
notes.Katana: A reskinned +1 longsword (an Easter-egg nod to the famous 'katana = +1 longsword' copypasta) — no special properties, D in Act 3.

subject: Amulets
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide, amulets parts 1-3

notes.Amulet (non-magical): Plain loot with no mechanical effect; sell it or feed it to Gale.
notes.Dog Collar: An Act 1 story-flavour item; emotionally memorable but no combat use.
notes.Aberration Hunters' Amulet: Bonus-action advantage on attacks against aberrations plus disadvantage on theirs back; narrow but powerful, and aberrations get more common later.
notes.Absolute's Talisman: Only casts Aid on the wearer and doesn't stack with the real Aid spell, which you can get more easily elsewhere.
notes.Amulet of Branding: Bonus-action vulnerability that can roughly double a big physical hit (often +30 to +40 damage); swap in to burst a priority target.
notes.Amulet of Misty Step: Free Misty Step once per short rest hands the best positioning tool in the game to a character who lacks it.
notes.Amulet of Selûne's Chosen: Its heal can put your own ally to sleep and is unusable in combat; out of combat, potions do the job better.
notes.Amulet of Silvanus: Short-rest Lesser Restoration for shedding paralysis or poison, and it cleanly removes Astarion's bloodless condition.
notes.Amulet of the Unworthy: Permanent slashing resistance is strong since slashing is common, but the bludgeoning vulnerability can pop you, so only wear it when no bludgeoning is expected.
notes.Beastmaster's Chain: Animal Friendship is near-useless in BG3; almost no fights are against animals and it only stops attacks on the caster.
notes.Broodmother's Revenge: Free, repeatable +1d6 poison on your attacks just by healing; strong alone and busted with the poisoner set.
notes.Champion's Chain: Once-per-day +2 intimidation, the least-used face skill, and it's mutually exclusive with the better Envoy's Amulet.
notes.Envoy's Amulet: Once-per-day +2 persuasion you can swap in to pass key checks that skip or soften hard fights.
notes.Komira's Locket: At-will Dancing Lights gives a free light source and can block hazard vents by dropping an object on them.
notes.Moondrop Pendant: Below half HP you ignore opportunity attacks; great early on warlocks and archers who need to reposition, and someone always wears it.
notes.Necklace of Elemental Augmentation: Adds your spell modifier to elemental cantrip damage, exceptional for Ray of Frost on wet enemies and stacks with other cantrip buffs.
notes.Pearl of Power Amulet: Restores a spell slot up to 3rd level each long rest, and third-level slots are your biggest power spike, so it's a caster staple.
notes.Periapt of Wound Closure: Maximises every heal you receive and auto-stabilises when downed; situational but good for heal-heavy or self-healing builds.
notes.Psychic Spark: Magic Missile once per day with an extra missile; guaranteed damage to confirm kills and the core of magic-missile builds.
notes.Sentient Amulet (Rare): Short-rest Shatter (handy for scrying orbs out of combat) plus ki restoration for monks; the laughter downside rarely matters outside combat.
notes.Silver Pendant: At-will Guidance, the most-used and most important skill-check tool in the game; rush it if you lack a Guidance caster.
notes.Tarnished Charm: Only lowers your death-save DC to 5, an effect that almost never matters; arguably the weakest positive item in the game.
notes.The Amulet of Lost Voices: Early Speak with Dead for great flavour and easier quest paths, though it rarely raises your raw power.
notes.The Blast Pendant: A fiddly lightning-charge payoff that blocks your other pre-combat buffs and only suits specific casters.
notes.The Ever-Seeing Eye: Short-rest Protection from Evil and Good: fear immunity and disadvantage on undead and fiend attacks, very relevant into Act 2.
notes.The Spectator Eyes: Two once-per-day spells that just duplicate a cantrip and a weak fear effect, easily outclassed by what your characters already do.
notes.Uncovered Mysteries: Detect Thoughts actually makes most dialogues harder, and its only combat use (free concentration) has easier sources.
notes.Amulet of Restoration: Healing Word and Mass Healing Word each once per day; superb panic buttons and excellent with the buff-on-heal set.
notes.Amulet of the Harpers: Advantage on Wisdom saves plus once-per-day Shield, a huge survivability package on a cheap slot, especially for martials.
notes.Fireheart: Builds heat only off allies hitting you with fire; too clunky and weak to bother with.
notes.Frost Prince: A level-1 Ice Knife once per day, which is nothing special by Act 2.
notes.Hammergrim Mist Amulet: At-will Fog Cloud, a no-save blindness that locks down or chases enemies; fog cloud is badly underrated.
notes.Shar's Temptation: Just short-rest Charm Person; basically only useful if your party lacks Friends.
notes.Spellcrux Amulet: Restores ANY spell slot as a bonus action once per day, up to a 6th-level slot, your most valuable resource; absurdly strong.
notes.Spineshudder Amulet: Adds reverberation to ranged spell attacks and stacks instantly on multi-hit spells; fantastic for warlocks.
notes.Strange Tendril Amulet: Free Evard's Black Tentacles once per day, brutal combined with hunger of Hadar or walls to lock enemies down.
notes.Surgeon's Subjugation Amulet: Once per day a critical hit paralyses a humanoid with no save, which often just wins the fight.
notes.Amulet of Windrider: Gust of Wind and gaseous form, both easily covered by the scrolls and potions you already carry.
notes.Absolute Confidence Amulet: A joke quest reward (+1 performance, -1 intimidation); fun flavour, no real use.
notes.Amulet of Bhaal: No-save bleed on any hit against full-HP enemies; niche, but excellent for bleed-synergy builds like the Wolverine maim combo.
notes.Amulet of Elemental Torment: Standing in a surface adds its effect (e.g. +1d4 fire, or -2 AC from acid) to your damaging spells; cool, but moved frustratingly late.
notes.Amulet of Greater Health: Sets Constitution to 23 for ~72 free HP, never-failed concentration, and freed-up attribute points; every party wants one.
notes.Amulet of the Devout: +2 to your spell save DC, one of the very best things an item can give an offensive caster.
notes.Amulet of the Drunkard: Regenerate 2d4 per turn for five turns while drunk; efficient healing that triggers heal-synergy items, with a no-action wine option in Act 3.
notes.Chancer's Carcanet: Once-per-day advantage on an attack or save, but you must commit before seeing the roll and it eats your reaction.
notes.Corvid Token: Flight, permanent feather fall, and +5 ft jump that even works while wild shaped; best in slot for shapeshifting druids and great for exploration.
notes.Fey Semblance Amulet: Advantage on all mental saves, but about 95% of those are Wisdom, where the Harpers' Shield often serves better.
notes.Khalid's Gift: +1 Wisdom can buy back a feat for multiclass clerics and druids hitting 20 Wis; S-tier for them, A-tier otherwise.
notes.Kill Collar: Lets a specific enemy instantly blow you up; listed only for completeness.
notes.Sentient Amulet (Very Rare): Long-rest Shatter without the laughter downside plus ki restoration, though Act 3 monks rarely run out of ki.
notes.Spell Savant Amulet: One extra level-2 slot, easily outclassed by Spellcrux, Psychic Spark, and angelic reprieve potions.
notes.Tressym Collar: Free Telekinesis once per day, a strong spell that's normally too pricey to cast; great for tossing enemies into hazards.
notes.Unflinching Protector Amulet: Reaction to make any attack on an ally miss, once per short rest; an excellent party-wide panic button on a high-AC defender.

notes.Adamantine Splint Armour: 18 AC. -2 all damage; crit immunity; inflicts Reeling on attackers. Story event near end of Act 1. Crit immunity + flat reduction + Reeling = stays relevant for a big chunk of the game.
notes.Dwarven Splintmail: 19 AC. +2 Constitution; +1 STR saves/checks; piercing resist. +2 CON is huge for tanks and any heavy-armor spellcaster (concentration, HP). Merchant often drops a 2nd copy if killed.
notes.Reaper's Embrace: 19 AC. -2 all damage; force-movement immunity (toggle); Numb active. Acquired in Act 2, shines early Act 3. Best-in-slot defensive when you want flat reduction over the CON bonus.
notes.Armour of Persistence: 20 AC. -2 all damage; permanent Blade Ward + Resistance (+1d4 saves). Best defensive armor in the game played normally. Flat + multiplicative reduction + save boost = nearly unkillable.
notes.Helldusk Armour: 21 AC. -3 all damage; fire resist; retaliate fire on saves; fly (bonus action). Highest base AC; anyone can wear it (counts as cloth). Broken at any point. Pairs with Bracers of Defense (23 AC).
notes.Armour of Devotion: 18 AC. Restore 1 spent Channel Oath charge once/long rest (Paladin only). Free value for paladins; effectively an extra short rest of Oath charges. Best for Oath of Vengeance.
notes.Rippling Force Mail: 17 AC. Gain Force Conduit (stacking phys damage reduction) when hit. Worn straight: solid. Built around (self-damage stacking): broken passive-kill builds. Rated for normal use.
notes.Emblazoned Plate of the Marshal: 19 AC. -2 all damage; fire resist; +2 saves; Fire Shield 1/rest. Locked behind story choices. Great package, but Armour of Persistence is usually reachable around the same time.
notes.Plate Armour: 18 AC. First heavy armor that beats your medium armor's AC. Sold same time as Splint; gets obsoleted quickly.
notes.+1 Splint Armour: 18 AC. Piercing resist. Merchants, party lvl 6-8. Piercing is the most relevant resist. Side-grade to Plate at that point.
notes.+1 Plate Armour: 19 AC. Piercing resist. Merchants from lvl 8. Highest raw AC available when you get it if you want max AC over the Adamantine Splint.
notes.+2 Plate Armour: 20 AC. -2 physical (slash/pierce/bludgeon) damage. Merchants from lvl 11. High AC + physical reduction, but better options usually exist by then.
notes.Blackguard's Plate: 19 AC. -1 all damage; advantage on WIS saves. Advantage on WIS saves dodges disables (hold person, etc.). Largely superseded by Heroes' Feast if you have it.
notes.Chain Mail: 16 AC. Only an upgrade for sub-14 DEX characters (e.g. no-respec runs). All chain mails land in C 'for symmetry.'
notes.+1 Chain Mail: 17 AC. Slashing resist. Merchants lvl 4-6. Niche AC upgrade for low-DEX parties; you usually have 17 AC half-plates by then.
notes.+2 Chain Mail: 18 AC. Slashing resist. Extremely narrow: a slashing-heavy fight where you skipped Plate/Splint and don't have the Adamantine Splint.
notes.Flawed Helldusk Armour: 18 AC. Piercing resist; 1d4 fire retaliation (DC13 Dex, melee). Retaliation is negligible. Effectively just 18 AC + piercing resist; outclassed almost immediately.
notes.Protective Plate: 18 AC. Necrotic resist; NO stealth disadvantage. Odd item (can't be spawned via tools). Swap-in for necrotic-heavy fights; no-stealth-penalty is a quirky niche.
notes.Cerebral Citadel Armour: 18 AC. Fear immunity; +1 mental saves (if tadpole-infested). Niche INT-save boost. Costs AC vs better options; fear immunity is available elsewhere (Heroes' Feast).
notes.Ring Mail: 14 AC. 14 AC with no DEX bonus = strictly worse than the Scale Mail you start with. Never use.
notes.Splint Armour: 17 AC. Sold alongside Plate Armour (18 AC), which is strictly better. No reason to buy.
notes.+1 Ring Mail: 15 AC. +1 STR saves/checks (does NOT work on Athletics). The one passive it has doesn't apply to the shoves you'd actually use it for. Downgrade in defense.
notes.+2 Ring Mail: 16 AC. +1 STR saves/checks; slashing resist. Merchants lvl 5-8. 16 AC at that point is far behind; no reason to pick it up.
notes.+2 Splint Armour: 19 AC. Piercing resist; +1 STR saves/checks. Identical to Dwarven Splintmail minus the +2 CON. Almost always strictly obsoleted by it.

tier_definitions.S+: Breaks D&D's balance range — part of the most broken builds
tier_definitions.S: Extremely powerful mainstays taken very frequently
tier_definitions.A: Strong, narrower alternatives to S-tier for specific builds
tier_definitions.B: Viable niche strategies; good leftover 3rd/4th feat picks
tier_definitions.C: Very niche; usually weaker than the alternatives
tier_definitions.D: Very weak — little real effect or easily duplicated; avoid

notes.Alert: Copied from 5e unchanged, but BG3 rolls initiative on a d4 instead of a d20, so its +5 is larger than the entire random range — a near-guaranteed win of initiative, which lets you kill enemies before they ever act. Aim to get it as your first or second feat on almost any Honour Mode character.
notes.Tavern Brawler: A BG3 house rule that adds DOUBLE your Strength modifier to both hit and damage on unarmed and thrown attacks, breaking 5e's bounded-accuracy rule — worth roughly five ASIs of hit chance and free for monks/throwers (especially with Strength elixirs).
notes.Ability Score Improvement: +2 to your main stat is a +1 to nearly every roll across the whole game, and small linear bonuses get proportionally stronger the better you already are — almost every optimal build takes two of these to hit 20.
notes.Athlete: A half-ASI (even out a 17) that also grants +50% jump distance, giving a melee character cheap bonus-action mobility to actually reach combat and set up high ground — often just a better ASI.
notes.Great Weapon Master: Toggle -5 to hit for +10 damage per two-handed attack, plus a bonus-action attack on a kill or crit; massively raises average AND maximum damage so you drop enemies before they act. Leave it on in most situations.
notes.Savage Attacker: Roll melee damage dice twice and keep the higher — a ~25-30% average damage boost that scales with how many dice you roll, so it's S-tier on smite/sneak-attack/shadowblade builds but much weaker on flat-bonus hitters.
notes.Sharpshooter: The ranged Great Weapon Master (-5/+10) plus ignoring the high-ground penalty; even better than GWM because ranged attacks land more often and the penalty is easy to offset with Archery style, high ground, and stealth advantage.
notes.Actor: +1 Charisma (uncapped, unlike most feats) plus expertise in Deception and Performance without needing proficiency — nearly free on a Charisma build that started on an odd score.
notes.Dual Wielder: Weak for actual dual-wielding, but lets casters wield two staves (or Phalar Aluve + a staff) at once for stacked passive spellcasting buffs — a core piece of some of the most broken magic-missile/fire-sorcerer builds.
notes.Heavy Armor Master: +1 Strength and -3 to all incoming bludgeoning/piercing/slashing damage in heavy armor; stacks with armor damage reduction and resistances to slash effective damage dramatically. Often a free ASI swap on heavy-armor characters.
notes.Mobile: +10 movement that stacks with everything, ignore difficult terrain while dashing, and no opportunity attacks after a melee hit — a strong fourth feat that opens late-game tactical options, especially on monoclass rogues.
notes.Moderately Armored: Grants medium armor AND shield proficiency while bumping Strength or Dexterity, letting a Dex character (e.g. monoclass rogue) wear BG3's excellent medium armors and a shield at near-zero cost.
notes.Polearm Master: A bonus-action butt-end attack and an opportunity attack when enemies enter your extended reach; the basis of the Polearm Master + Sentinel lockdown combo and great on its own because more attacks is always good.
notes.Resilient: +1 to any attribute plus proficiency in its save; mainly taken for Constitution-save proficiency (better concentration) or to restore a save lost to multiclassing — nearly free when it evens out an odd score.
notes.Sentinel: Opportunity attacks stop the target's movement, you get advantage on them, and you can punish enemies attacking your allies — pairs with Polearm Master to lock melee enemies out of range indefinitely.
notes.War Caster: Advantage on Constitution saves to hold concentration (plus cast spells as opportunity attacks); a strong safety net if your casters lose concentration often, though Resilient is usually better when you lack con-save proficiency.
notes.Defensive Duelist: Spend a reaction to add your proficiency bonus to AC when hit in melee with a finesse weapon — like half of Shield for free each turn, solid on Dex melee characters such as swashbuckler rogues.
notes.Elemental Adept: Your chosen damage type ignores resistance and can't roll 1s; realistically only worth it for fire (lots of fire-resistant enemies), and even then you can often just cast a different element instead.
notes.Lucky: Three luck points per long rest for advantage or to force an enemy to reroll an attack; good defensively and consistency-boosting if you rest often, but expensive — more of a third/fourth feat.
notes.Mage Slayer: Advantage on saves vs adjacent casters, a reaction attack when they cast, and disadvantage on their concentration; a 'win-more' lockdown tool that shines mainly in mixed melee/caster boss fights.
notes.Magic Initiate: Two cantrips and a 1st-level spell; a bad feat saved almost entirely by Patch 8's Booming Blade, which adds so much damage that some melee builds take Magic Initiate just to learn it.
notes.Spell Sniper: Learn an attack cantrip (usually Eldritch Blast, cast with YOUR stat) and reduce your crit threshold by 1 on all spells; solid on attack-spam casters like Great Old One warlocks and fire sorcerers.
notes.Durable: +1 Constitution and full HP on a short rest; out-of-combat healing is already nearly free in BG3, so it's a minor, rarely-relevant pick that's only fine when it evens out an odd con score.
notes.Heavily Armored: +1 Strength and heavy armor proficiency, but Strength characters usually already have it, BG3's medium armors are better, and a 1-level cleric/fighter dip grants it more efficiently.
notes.Martial Adept: Two maneuvers and one superiority die — a terrible return for most, but okay specifically on a Battle Master who wants one extra (and larger) superiority die per short rest.
notes.Tough: +2 HP per level (up to +24); not nothing on a squishy character, but a purely defensive feat is hard to justify over options that improve your game plan.
notes.Charger: Action + bonus action to move 30ft and attack (+5 damage) or shove; most characters get this mobility for free by jumping/dashing, and the wide charge path constantly whiffs on terrain and allies.
notes.Crossbow Expert: Negates melee disadvantage on crossbow attacks, but BG3 crossbows are weak and free weapon-set swapping lets archers just hit with melee weapons instead — the feat is duplicated by base mechanics.
notes.Dungeon Delver: Advantage to spot traps and half damage from them — so minimal it did literally nothing for six patches and nobody noticed; the lowest-impact feat in the game.
notes.Lightly Armored: +1 Strength or Dexterity and light armor proficiency with no other bonus; almost no character that lacks light armor proficiency wants to boost those stats, and robes outclass light armor anyway.
notes.Medium Armor Master: Removes medium-armor stealth disadvantage and raises the Dex cap to +3, but BG3's best medium armors are already uncapped and this feat actually OVERWRITES that, lowering AC on the high-Dex characters who'd want it.
notes.Performer: +1 Charisma and instrument proficiency that does essentially nothing mechanically; Actor is a strictly better way to get the Charisma point.
notes.Ritual Caster: Two ritual spells (Longstrider, Speak with Dead, etc.) that you can get from items or a 1-level dip; far too little value for a whole feat.
notes.Shield Master: +2 Dex saves with a shield and a Rogue-evasion-like reaction, but the half-damage half is bugged to do nothing on a fail, and Resilient gives a bigger Dex-save bonus anyway.
notes.Skilled: Three skill proficiencies — a huge opportunity cost for little benefit, since you can get skills from background/race and most BG3 skills aren't very impactful. Take only for flavor.
notes.Weapon Master: +1 Strength or Dexterity and four weapon proficiencies, but any character who wants a weapon type already has proficiency in it; almost never worth a feat even as a half-feat.

subject: Classes (single-class)
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode build guide — monoclass, level 12

tier_definitions.S: Phenomenally powerful AND versatile — slots into any party
tier_definitions.A: Either supremely versatile or extremely powerful, but not both
tier_definitions.B: More build-constrained; lacks A-tier power but still excellent
tier_definitions.C: Constrained to one/two roles and not the best at them
tier_definitions.D: Empty on purpose — there are no non-viable classes in BG3

notes.Bard: The most versatile class in the game — every skill plus easy expertise (best party face), Song of Rest sustain, and Magical Secrets to grab the best spells of any class; also among the highest ranged/melee damage and the easiest access to the broken Arcane Acuity / Band of the Mystic Scoundrel combos. Fills every role except summoning.
notes.Cleric: Misjudged as pure support — domains let it tank, deal damage, control, or heal, all backed by broken mechanics it gets out of the box (radiant-orb stacking, Spirit Guardians, heal-on-buff Blade Ward/Bless items) plus Guidance, Bless, and Command. Doesn't compete for party items; only cost is heavier long-rest reliance.
notes.Sorcerer: Quicken + Twin Spell let it cast multiple leveled spells per turn — impossible in tabletop — effectively taking two turns to everyone else's one with the game's best spell list and Charisma face access. The class most likely to become broken entirely by accident.
notes.Druid: A full spellcaster with extra health bars (wild shape / Spore shield) and the best summon access (Woodland Being, Conjure Elemental); supremely versatile and its late-game spell list is superb, but it comes online slowly and wild-shape melee falls off — slightly lower raw power than the S-tier casters.
notes.Fighter: The most versatile martial: any role via its huge feat access, plus Action Surge (a whole extra turn), three attacks at level 11, and Indomitable saves. Eldritch Knight is the single easiest honour-mode character to pilot — but all it does is hit things, and that damage caps below dedicated damage dealers.
notes.Paladin: Smite attacks trade spell slots for the highest burst damage in the game, devastating on the easily-guaranteed crits, alongside Charisma face access, party-wide defensive auras, and Lay on Hands healing. Misses S only because there's one build path and it's the most long-rest-hungry, resource-greedy class.
notes.Ranger: The archer build stacks Archery style + Sharpshooter + early extra attacks (Gloomstalker at level 3) for huge, reliable ranged damage from safety; tanky with elemental resistances, great perception/stealth, and no item conflicts. Held back only by a mid-game dead zone before its strong level-11 capstones.
notes.Warlock: Eldritch Blast + Agonizing Blast IS the damage-dealer baseline; short-rest control (Hunger of Hadar), the Darkness + Devil's Sight immunity combo, and Charisma face access round it out. Low end of A — it mostly does one thing per fight, but that thing is excellent.
notes.Wizard: The widest spell access in the game (scribe nearly any scroll) means it almost always has the perfect spell for the encounter, plus the strongest subclass variance (Abjuration's ward, Divination), Arcane Recovery, and good summons. Versatility over raw power, and squishy — you must build the party to protect it.
notes.Barbarian: Highest HP plus Rage (extra damage + physical resistance), Reckless Attack (free advantage that also baits enemy aggro onto your tank), fast movement, and Feral Instinct's ~3/5 of Alert. Very high power but essentially one role (melee bruiser) and it competes hard for the contested uncapped medium armors and strength elixirs.
notes.Monk: Moves fast, hits hard (Flurry of Blows + the broken Tavern Brawler giving ~99% accuracy), and stuns — the strongest linear strategy in the game. But it's extremely multi-attribute dependent (STR/DEX/CON/WIS), squishy, and a monoclass monk lacks the Thief bonus action, so one bad positioning turn can end an honour-mode run.
notes.Rogue: Level-1 skill expertise and sneak-attack burst are great, but no Extra Attack and the once-per-turn sneak-attack cap limit its damage, and a monoclass rogue gains almost nothing after level 3 (just a d6 every two levels). It falls steadily behind as a damage dealer while still demanding party resources and protection.

subject: Illithid Powers
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide — rated mostly assuming the Zaith'isk bonus-action upgrade

tier_definitions.S: Extremely powerful mainstays — prioritise unlocking these
tier_definitions.A: Strong abilities regularly, or very powerful occasionally
tier_definitions.B: Solidly useful throughout the entire game
tier_definitions.C: More situational — comes up, but not every encounter
tier_definitions.D: Generally weak, but no illithid power is truly useless

notes.Psionic Overload: A self-buff adding 1d4 psychic to EVERY attack you roll (multiplying across extra attacks, multi-hit spells, and crits) for a trivial 1d4 self-damage; not worth a full action, but as a bonus action via the Zaith'isk it's a huge damage spike on any attack-spam build.
notes.Favourable Beginnings: A free passive that adds your proficiency bonus AGAIN to the first attack or ability check against each creature — lands the most important attack of every fight and turns the party face into a near-guaranteed dialogue passer, at zero action cost. Unlock it first.
notes.Cull the Weak: Passive: any enemy you drop below your number of unlocked powers dies INSTANTLY (bypassing death-prevention and resistances) and explodes for AOE psychic; effectively adds ~5-20 damage to your attacks and only scales as you unlock more powers — thousands of effective damage over a run.
notes.Shield of Thralls: Pre-castable 10 temp HP that, when broken, stuns every enemy within 30ft on a failed DC15 Intelligence save — a free, pre-combat AOE stun on a save enemies routinely fail, and an AOE one-turn stun wins fights.
notes.Ability Drain: Free passive: each hit permanently shaves 1 off the stat you attacked with (Dexterity from finesse/ranged is best — lowers their AC and accuracy), with no save; marginal per stack but free, stackable across the party, and it applies a condition for reverberation/radiant-orb engines.
notes.Luck of the Far Realms: Reaction to turn any hit into a guaranteed critical, once per long rest — S-tier on crit-hungry single-big-hit builds (Paladin smites, Rogue sneak attack, lightning builds); only the once-per-day limit keeps it out of S overall.
notes.Psionic Backlash: Reaction dealing 1d4 psychic per spell level to a casting enemy (can break concentration), with no cooldown; re-rated up to A because it gives martials/archers a valuable use for an otherwise-idle reaction, useful in the hardest (caster) fights and it combos with Cull the Weak.
notes.Illithid Persuasion: Replaces certain dialogue checks with a trivial DC2 Wisdom check (auto-pass after five uses); you start with it and never choose it, but it quietly smooths over checks and can hand you allies in a couple of fights.
notes.Force Tunnel: Move 30ft in a line as a combined dash + disengage that knocks everything back 13ft with NO save, regardless of enemy size — weak as an action, but as a Zaith'isk bonus action it's a great reposition-plus-punt (off cliffs, into hazards) once per short rest.
notes.Stage Fright: Large-AOE bonus-action debuff (Wisdom save) giving enemies disadvantage on attacks and 2d6 psychic on a miss; only worth it as a bonus action, and landing it depends on your often-low illithid save DC, so it's a minor but party-wide damage reducer.
notes.Repulsor: A bigger Thunderwave (30ft around you, Strength save, 2d6 force + 20ft knockback, half on save), but it hits allies so you must be 30ft from your party, and Strength is a poor save to target for knockback — situational; Force Tunnel does no-save knockback more reliably.
notes.Concentrated Blast: End your concentration to deal 3d6 no-save psychic (and heal if the target was concentrating); re-rated up to C because cheap concentration cantrips/ritual spells let you fire it off as a free bonus-action finisher, even if you shouldn't build around it.
notes.Transfuse Health: Spend half your current HP to heal an ally that much (resistances/temp HP can cheat the cost); some cute tricks exist, but out-of-combat healing is essentially free in BG3, so it rarely matters.
notes.Charm: A defensive reaction that only triggers AFTER an enemy's first attack, lets them retarget your allies anyway, and uses a Wisdom save enemies make with advantage — far weaker than Shield/Warding Flare; only free value if your reaction is otherwise idle.
notes.Displace: Passive adding 1d8 psychic when YOUR actions cause falling damage; tiny, very situational, hard to trigger more than once per fight, and frequently bugged into doing nothing.
notes.Perilous Stakes: On Honour Mode it can only target allies, granting damage vulnerability (lethal) for 2d8 heal-on-attack; its real (broken) enemy-vulnerability use is disabled here, leaving it as decent but unnecessary out-of-combat self-healing.
notes.Illithid Expertise: Passive granting expertise in Persuasion, Deception, and Intimidation without needing the skills (like a free, no-cost Actor feat) — turns any character into a dominant Act 3 dialogue passer.
notes.Black Hole: Short-rest AOE (Intelligence save) that pulls most of an encounter to one point and Slows them without using concentration — then recasts five more times as a bonus action; groups enemies for AOE nukes and traps them on hazard surfaces. The reason to dive the outer ring.
notes.Fly: Free passive (no tadpole) granting flight that effectively doubles movement and ignores terrain at no action cost — the best mobility tool in the game, and you can give it to the whole party. Positioning is king.
notes.Psionic Dominance: Effectively a free 4th-level Counterspell once per long rest against spells targeting you (auto-counters spells up to your proficiency bonus, ability check above that); A rather than S only because dedicated Counterspell is more flexible.
notes.Mind Blast: Once-per-long-rest 47ft cone (Intelligence save) for 4d8+mod psychic and a full-turn STUN; the damage is modest by Act 3 but an AOE stun wins encounters outright — A only because it fires once a day and competes with Black Hole.
notes.Freecast: Long-rest toggle that removes ALL costs of your next spell/ability (slot, sorcery points, dice) — roughly an extra 6th-level slot per day, best on sorcerers (also refunds metamagic points); pure adventuring-day longevity, near S-tier if you rest rarely.
notes.Absorb Intellect: Cast on a creature for escalating Intelligence drain and 15d8 of healing over five turns (Intelligence save once); a solid heal and a safe way to assassinate low-Int enemies like Steel Watchers, but usually your bonus action is better spent on Black Hole/Mind Blast.
notes.Displacer Beast Shape: Once-per-long-rest wild-shape into an 85 HP / 16 AC displacer beast with a guaranteed-teleport and triple attack; a competent but not top-tier combat form — best as a big bonus-action shield to tank one hit or fight when out of resources.
notes.Survival Instinct: Pre-cast buff that heals an ally 3d4 instead of dropping to 0 (for three turns); preventing a death is nice, but it must be cast in advance on a target you predict will be hit, so it's rarely the best use of a bonus action.
notes.Fracture Psyche: Short-rest single-target debuff (Intelligence save) lowering AC and saves by 1 (2 on a chain after a kill) for five turns — basically a weaker single-target Bane; occasionally used to set up an important spell, but low priority for your bonus action.
notes.Mind Sanctuary: On Honour Mode it's simply AOE Haste (no stacking), which is fine but redundant by Act 3 — and it's badly bugged into sometimes eating a character's entire turn, which can lose an Honour run, so it's best avoided until fixed.

subject: Subclasses (single-class)
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode build guide — monoclass, level 12 (includes the Patch 8 subclasses)

tier_definitions.S+: Out of scale — breaks the game's intended balance
tier_definitions.S: Both extremely powerful and extremely versatile
tier_definitions.A: Either extremely powerful or extremely versatile, not both
tier_definitions.B: A bit less powerful/versatile than A, but still great
tier_definitions.C: Restricts your strategy via a drawback you must play around

notes.Berserker (Barbarian): Frenzy's bonus-action throw (no frenzied-strain penalty) makes it a top-tier Tavern Brawler thrower, plus Mindless Rage immunity to fear/charm — but a monoclass Eldritch Knight throws more often, so it lands at A rather than S.
notes.Wild Heart (Barbarian): Five excellent rage aspects (Bear's resistance, Tiger's GWM-friendly AOE, Elk's party move-speed aura) make it the most reliable bruiser in the game — the author's benchmark for good game balance.
notes.Wild Magic (Barbarian): No negative surges (unlike the sorcerer) and it can restore allies' spell slots, but the random rage effects are minor and you must rage 20ft from allies, which hampers party positioning.
notes.Path of the Giant (Barbarian): Giant's Rage and Elemental Cleaver stack huge bonus damage onto every (any-weapon) throw, becoming the best thrown-weapon barbarian by the late game — pure damage and nothing else.
notes.College of Lore (Bard): Cutting Words (a castable-on-anyone Shield), the earliest Magical Secrets for the best spells in the game, and extra skill proficiencies — the default, no-downside caster bard.
notes.College of Swords (Bard): Ranged Slashing Flourish gives four attacks per round on top of full bard casting and medium armour, with the easiest access to the broken Arcane Acuity / Band of the Mystic Scoundrel combo — out of scale with the game's balance.
notes.College of Valour (Bard): Combat Inspiration and extra attack are fine, but it does everything Swords Bard does, worse — outclassed within the class, though still a strong bard.
notes.College of Glamour (Bard): A misunderstood support bard — Mantle of Inspiration's no-save charm-on-hit disrupts enemy turns for free and Mantle of Majesty wins a fight per day with bonus-action Command; brings everything but damage.
notes.Knowledge Domain (Cleric): Knowledge of the Ages grants full skill proficiency in an ability on demand, fixing cleric's poor skill access (party face, lockpicker, perception), plus a few control spells.
notes.Life Domain (Cleric): The best solo applier of buff-on-heal items via an AOE Channel Divinity and self+ally healing, but a one-dimensional healer that gains no new spells — a great dip, merely A as monoclass.
notes.Light Domain (Cleric): Warding Flare (a party-wide Shield-like reaction), the big Radiance of the Dawn AOE nuke (and best radiant-orb access), plus a full fire/damage spell list that patches cleric's lack of direct damage.
notes.Nature Domain (Cleric): Shillelagh makes the early game playable in melee, plus an elite added spell list (Spike Growth, Sleet Storm, Plant Growth) and Dampen Elements — superb control access.
notes.Tempest Domain (Cleric): The best added spell list (Call Lightning, Sleet/Ice Storm, Fog Cloud) plus Destructive Wrath to maximise lightning damage and reaction knockback that ping-pongs enemies across electrified water.
notes.Trickery Domain (Cleric): Really only adds the spell Fear; Invoke Duplicity is a famously underwhelming concentration action, so it's outclassed by domains with better AOE control — still a fine cleric.
notes.War Domain (Cleric): Heavy armour, martial weapons, and a reaction to add +10 to an ally's attack (turning a miss into a hit); the bonus-action attacks don't keep up on a monoclass caster, but a solid domain.
notes.Death Domain (Cleric): Reaper double-targets necrotic cantrips (Toll the Dead/Bone Chill) for strong early damage and Touch of Death is a Smite-sized burst — patches cleric's only weakness (early damage) while keeping every cleric strength.
notes.Circle of the Land (Druid): The default caster druid — Circle spells add the best low-level picks (Misty Step, Haste, Hypnotic Pattern) plus difficult-terrain and poison immunity; falls off slightly late but a great class.
notes.Circle of the Moon (Druid): Bonus-action wild shape (cast-then-shift in one turn) plus strong forms (Owlbear, Myrmidons) that abuse Tavern Brawler for big sustained melee damage on top of full druid casting.
notes.Circle of Spores (Druid): Symbiotic Entity adds temp HP and +1d6 to every attack plus a proactive reaction, Bone Chill smooths the cantrip gap, and free Animate Dead enables a summon army — but it's MAD with no extra attack.
notes.Circle of the Stars (Druid): Star forms give spammable free damage (Archer/Dragon) or healing (Chalice) plus Dragon-form reliable concentration, free Guiding Bolts, and Cosmic Omen (a mini Cutting Words) — 'Druid but more', obsoleting Land Druid.
notes.Battle Master (Fighter): Maneuvers add control while dealing full damage (Precision/Trip/Pushing), but burn through only five superiority dice fast — the author's benchmark for good balance, and three attacks make it great regardless.
notes.Champion (Fighter): Improved Critical works out to under +1 damage per attack — far less than Battle Master's maneuvers and dice, with no other meaningful options, so it's outclassed within the class.
notes.Eldritch Knight (Fighter): Best played as a tanky Tavern Brawler thrower with Shield/Misty Step utility — the most forgiving character and the easiest way to win Honour Mode, near-unmissable and hard to kill.
notes.Arcane Archer (Fighter): Arcane Shots add can't-miss attacks (Seeking/Piercing) and removal-from-combat (Banishing Arrow vs the often-weak Charisma save) on top of a normal strong fighter — lower damage, higher control than Battle Master.
notes.Way of the Four Elements (Monk): Mostly spends key points on worse copies of other classes' spells; the unique ranged punches and ice block add a little dimension, but it gives little the base monk doesn't already do.
notes.Way of the Open Hand (Monk): Free crowd control on Flurry of Blows (effective extra stuns), +1d4+Wis (radiant for orb-stacking) on every attack, and Wholeness of Body key recovery — the best Tavern Brawler abuser and highest sustained monk damage.
notes.Way of Shadow (Monk): Thematic and fun, but Shadow Step's bonus-action teleport is something monks already do by jumping, and its tricks (Darkness/Silence) are cheaper from other classes — little unique value monoclass.
notes.Drunken Master (Monk): A playtest subclass whose key abilities are situational or literally blank-text and self-defeating (you spend key points for marginal stacks that vanish the moment you act); outclassed by every other monk and badly in need of a rework.
notes.Oath of the Ancients (Paladin): Healing Radiance double-applies buff-on-heal items, and its aura grants spell-damage resistance — combined with Paladin saves, that's party-wide quarter damage from spells, letting healing actually outpace incoming damage; great spells (Misty Step, Plant Growth) too.
notes.Oath of Devotion (Paladin): Sacred Weapon adds Charisma to attack rolls, but Vengeance's Vow of Enmity does the accuracy job better and the rest of the kit is weak — outclassed, though still a fine smiting paladin.
notes.Oath of Vengeance (Paladin): The best paladin spell list (Misty Step, Haste, Hold Person) plus Vow of Enmity advantage (currently bugged to apply to all your attacks) and Inquisitor's Might — excels at dropping priority targets fast.
notes.Oathbreaker (Paladin): Saves the oath-restoration tax and adds an AOE fear and Animate Dead, but its signature Aura of Hate doesn't work on any summonable undead, so it's just a worse Vengeance — outclassed.
notes.Oath of the Crown (Paladin): Righteous Clarity buffs the whole party's accuracy, Champion Challenge controls aggro, and Charisma-based Spirit Guardians at level 9 makes it the best radiant-orb melee paladin — damage plus cleric-like support.
notes.Beast Master (Ranger): A continuously-scaling companion (the Raven's free AOE Darkness/blind is unmatched late utility) adds extra actions and Hunter's Mark damage on top of a Sharpshooter archer; the beast's fragility keeps it at A.
notes.Gloomstalker (Ranger): Dread Ambusher's free opening attack (~25 burst with Sharpshooter), out-of-combat invisibility for perfect positioning, Wisdom-save proficiency, and Misty Step — an incredibly safe, strong archer.
notes.Hunter (Ranger): Heavily back-loaded — Colossus Slayer is weak and the level-7 picks minor, but the level-11 Volley capstone is S-tier; rated B across a full 1-12 playthrough (S if you respec into it at 11).
notes.Swarm Keeper (Ranger): A strong level-3 spike (2d6 plus a per-turn blind, later blind+slow at 11) but roughly eight dead levels in between — a happy-to-have B-tier with a soft mid-game.
notes.Arcane Trickster (Rogue): Wizard utility spells (Shield, Misty Step, disguise/charm for a party face) plus Magical Ambush disadvantage to reliably land Hold Person; nearly unkillable but adds little damage — the best monoclass rogue.
notes.Assassin (Rogue): Huge opening burst (restored actions + auto-crits on the surprised) that falls off hard after level 3 and forces you to always initiate with the rogue, often costing more party damage than it gains.
notes.Thief (Rogue): The extra bonus action gives superb positioning and reliable sneak-attack landing, but a monoclass thief still lacks Extra Attack, so its damage lags every dedicated damage dealer — great in multiclass, weak solo.
notes.Swashbuckler (Rogue): Sneak attacks without allies, a scaling initiative bonus, and bonus-action blind/disarm (Flick of the Wrist is a near-second attack) make a safe hit-and-run rogue — but still lower damage than other damage subclasses.
notes.Draconic (Sorcerer): Elemental Affinity adds Charisma to every damage spell of your type (broken with leveled Scorching Ray or wet cold/lightning), plus extra HP and free Mage Armour — a powerful sorcerer with a great damage feature.
notes.Storm (Sorcerer): Tempestuous Magic gives bonus-action flight with no opportunity attacks from level one (huge early-game safety), plus Heart of the Storm AOE rider damage and a flexible added spell list.
notes.Wild Magic (Sorcerer): Tides of Chaos and Bend Luck are powerful, but the random surges (including a hostile cambion) force restrictive positioning and can snowball a bad fight — rated C for the constraint, though one of the most fun playthroughs.
notes.Shadow Magic (Sorcerer): A sorcerer that gets Darkness plus see-in-magical-darkness without a warlock dip (one of the game's best strategies), plus the Hound of Ill Omen summon and a later teleport — high-end S.
notes.The Archfey (Warlock): Underrated — Fey Presence is an AOE fear/charm disable from level one, Misty Escape gives an invisibility-plus-teleport escape, and Plant Growth pairs with Hunger of Hadar.
notes.The Fiend (Warlock): The best added spell list (auto-scaling Command, resource-free Wall of Fire), kill-based temp HP for sustain, Dark One's Own Luck (+d10 to a check), and changeable damage resistance — very hard to kill.
notes.The Great Old One (Warlock): Mortal Reminder spreads free fear on your frequent Eldritch Blast crits, plus a spell list that lets a warlock cheaply cast situational spells (Slow, Telekinesis) and Entropic Ward — the generically strongest warlock.
notes.Hexblade (Warlock): Ties weapon and spell attacks to Charisma with medium armour/shields at level one, Hex's Curse adds proficiency to every damage instance (huge with Eldritch Blast), and Cone of Cold/Banishing Smite later remove its only weakness — too much in one character.
notes.Abjuration (Wizard): Arcane Ward is flat damage reduction (up to 2x level) refreshed by cheap Glyph of Warding and stacked with resistances/healing — effectively thousands of hit points; literally unkillable and game-breaking.
notes.Conjuration (Wizard): Almost nothing until Focus Conjuration at level 10 (concentration unbreakable by damage), which Abjuration does better — outclassed, but Wizard's base chassis keeps it strong.
notes.Divination (Wizard): Portent dice let you replace any attack or save you see with a pre-rolled value (force enemy fails, guarantee crits), regaining more via prophecies — removing randomness at key moments is nonsensically strong.
notes.Enchantment (Wizard): Little until level 10, but Split Enchantment double-casts single-target enchantments (double Hold Person / Otto's Irresistible Dance) — the best wizard at late-game disables.
notes.Evocation (Wizard): Sculpt Spells makes allies immune to your AOE (drop Fireball in a melee, stand in your own Wall of Fire), and late Int-to-damage makes Magic Missile/Acid Splash reliable finishers — the easiest, most forgiving wizard.
notes.Illusion (Wizard): Improved Minor Illusion barely matters and Illusory Self (turn a hit into a miss at level 10) is duplicated better by Divination's portent dice — outclassed, though still a strong wizard chassis.
notes.Necromancy (Wizard): The summoner wizard — free Animate Dead plus an extra corpse per cast and big HP/damage buffs to your minions; little else, but summon armies are very strong.
notes.Transmutation (Wizard): Usually a camp follower, but as an active member the Transmuter's Stone grants Constitution-save (concentration) proficiency so it rarely loses spells, plus extra potions — enough for an A on the strong wizard base.
notes.Bladesinging (Wizard): Bladesong adds AC, Con saves, and move speed for survivability plus a bonus-action burst of damage/healing (Bladesong Climax); the melee is secondary, but a wizard needs little from its subclass to be A.

subject: Spells — Level 1
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide (Patch 8 updated spell tier list)

tier_definitions.S: Critical — take it whenever available and cast it constantly
tier_definitions.A: Great workhorse spells; mainstays of your strategy
tier_definitions.B: Very useful — high impact in the right situations
tier_definitions.C: Marginal — for specific builds or narrow encounters

notes.Bless: +1d4 to attacks AND saves for up to 3 allies (10 turns, pre-castable, upcasts) — a massive game-long accuracy boost that offsets Great Weapon Master/Sharpshooter penalties; the spell still beats the Whispering Promise ring because it lasts the whole fight.
notes.Chromatic Orb: Non-concentration damage plus a chosen surface — ice for prone-stun control, acid for -2 AC, fire/lightning to finish low-HP enemies; 3d8 thunder or 2d8 elemental, doubled vs wet. Huge damage and versatility.
notes.Command: A wisdom-save 'Grovel' drops an enemy prone and skips its turn (or 'Drop' to disarm), no concentration, upcasts to hit more — pure action-economy theft and the core of many builds (Band of the Mystic Scoundrel, Extend, fiend warlock).
notes.Create or Destroy Water: Applies the broken Wet condition (double cold/lightning damage) and makes water for ice/electrified surfaces — a level-1 way to halve fights, and it saves your scarce honour-mode water bottles.
notes.Dissonant Whispers: 3d6 psychic (wisdom save for half) that also frightens for 2 turns with no concentration — effectively stuns a melee enemy AND gives bards/GOO warlocks real damage; lands reliably on low-wisdom melee.
notes.Enhanced Leap: Ritual (free) that triples jump distance for 10 turns — a free bonus-action 'teleport' and big effective move-speed boost for the whole party. Mobility is king.
notes.Healing Word: Bonus-action ranged heal — the best panic button to revive a downed ally, the way to re-apply buff-on-heal items (Whispering Promise / Hellrider's Pride) mid-fight, and a turn-saver. Belongs in every party.
notes.Hex: Bonus action, +1d6 necrotic on every attack roll vs a target all day (re-applies free on kill) — absurd cumulative damage, especially with multi-hit Eldritch Blast; a level-1 spell worth hundreds of damage.
notes.Hunter's Mark: The ranger Hex — +1d6 weapon-damage-type on every weapon attack all day, re-applies on kill; mechanically the same enormous value, so every ranger takes it.
notes.Longstrider: Ritual (free), all-day +10 move speed castable on the whole party and summons — mobility for free; basically an S+ pick.
notes.Magic Missile: Three auto-hit force missiles (no save, ignores line of sight) that split across low-HP targets and stack per-missile riders (Callous Glow, lightning charges) — guaranteed damage is the least-random thing in the game.
notes.Sanctuary: Bonus-action, no-concentration — enemies can't directly target the warded ally, so your healer / haste-bot / concentration-holder becomes untouchable; one of the most versatile and powerful spells in the game.
notes.Shield: Reaction +5 AC (prompted only when it turns a hit into a miss) that also nullifies Magic Missile — negates an enemy attack (often a whole turn) for free; worth dipping a class just to get it.
notes.Armor of Agathys: Temp HP plus cold retaliation that upcasts well (and doubles vs wet) and lasts until depleted — solid on warlocks and the centrepiece of the broken Abjuration-wizard retaliation build.
notes.Disguise Self: Ritual (free) for crime cover, racial dialogue/merchant bonuses, AND using race-locked items (Githyanki swords, Dwarven Thrower) or shapeshift-keyed gear at full power; gets stronger as you learn the game.
notes.Ensnaring Strike: Cast on a weapon hit (bonus action) to root a melee enemy for 10 turns at no damage cost; very reliable with the ranger's Bounty Hunter giving disadvantage on the save.
notes.Find Familiar: Ritual summon (Raven blinds) for scouting, pressure plates, soaking a hit, and triggering sneak attack — value limited mostly by your patience; near-S if you love micromanaging.
notes.Fog Cloud: No-save AOE blind/obscure — wins fights when you keep enemies inside it (great on immobile bosses), and otherwise just hands your party advantage; also a guaranteed hiding spot for stealing.
notes.Grease: Non-concentration AOE prone-stun on YOUR save DC (better than grease bottles), great for repeated stuns and breaking caster concentration — only docked from S because it's flammable.
notes.Ice Knife: Like a cold Chromatic Orb — 1d10 + 2d6 cold AOE leaving an ice surface; redundant for wizards/sorcerers but a mainstay damage-and-control spell for druids, who lack Chromatic Orb.
notes.Mage Armor: All-day base AC 13 (+ Dex) for unarmored casters — skip it early, but near-mandatory late once you want to wear powerful robes.
notes.Tasha's Hideous Laughter: Wisdom-save full disable (10 turns) that, unusually, many incapacitation-immune bosses can still suffer — unreliable early but a fight-winner once your save DC is high.
notes.Thunderous Smite: A smite that knocks prone + 10ft back (breaks concentration, cliffs) for damage comparable to Divine Smite — and you can stack both on one attack for huge burst; most paladins take it.
notes.Bane: A tiny -1d4 to enemy attacks/saves, but uniquely useful late: it targets the often-weak Charisma save, few bosses are immune, and it softens a target up for a bigger spell — only worth it in long late-game fights.
notes.Entangle: A big 10-turn AOE root (strength save) granting advantage on attacks — strong control, but flammable and partly superseded by the druid's free spider webs.
notes.Feather Fall: Ritual (free) AOE no-fall-damage — mostly traversal/trap-skipping, but pairs with Enhanced Leap to use height safely in combat arenas; the quintessential handy B-tier spell.
notes.Guiding Bolt: 4d6 radiant plus advantage on the next attack; a solid cleric filler (and radiant for orb-stacking), but cantrips like Toll the Dead now cover ranged damage, so it's rarely a go-to.
notes.Inflict Wounds: 3d10 melee necrotic — the highest single-target level-1 damage (a cleric 'smite'), upcasts well and extends with sorcerer, but it puts the cleric in melee, so it's situational.
notes.Shield of Faith: All-day +2 AC (any armour), but costs concentration that clerics/paladins usually want elsewhere — best on one-level cleric dips that have nothing else to concentrate on.
notes.Sleep: A no-save full disable based on enemy HP totals — an all-star for the first ~3 levels, then it falls off a cliff as HP outscales it; you'll swap it out.
notes.Thunderwave: 2d8 thunder in a square that flings enemies ~27ft (con save half) — a decent panic/repositioning button into cliffs/hazards, but the con save lets the melee enemies you most want to push resist.
notes.Wrathful Smite: A smite with a good rider — frightens for 2 turns (disadvantage + can't move), pinning a fleeing enemy next to your paladin/hexblade; lower damage than Thunderous Smite.
notes.Burning Hands: 3d6 fire cone on a bad (dexterity) save at close range — weak, but light clerics get it free and use it to finish low-HP enemies.
notes.Cure Wounds: A touch heal whose numbers can't keep up with incoming damage (potions / Healing Word are better) — only notable now as a way to trigger a Star Druid's Chalice healing with your action.
notes.Divine Favor: +1d4 radiant on attacks for 3 turns, but a paladin would rather smite; only worth it on a war-cleric-dip weapon build doing radiant-orb / reverberation.
notes.Expeditious Retreat: All-day bonus-action dash, but it costs concentration and is mostly superseded by Misty Step / Enhanced Leap — niche for dash-trigger and concentration-trigger item builds.
notes.Faerie Fire: A big-radius advantage-granting / invisibility-revealing debuff (dex save), but Fog Cloud / Command / Entangle grant advantage too while doing more, so it's usually outclassed.
notes.Hellish Rebuke: 2d10 fire reaction — poor damage ratio, but useful for retaliation builds (Armor of Agathys), it works while raging, and a bug can delete multi-hit enemy attacks.
notes.Heroism: Fear immunity + 5 temp HP/turn (upcasts to the whole party) — a pet pick that shines in a narrow set of long fights or as pre-Heroes'-Feast fear immunity, but needs many conditions to be good.
notes.Protection from Evil and Good: A concentration buff giving disadvantage to aberrations/undead/fiends/etc (common in BG3) plus fear immunity — decent in those fights, but you usually want to concentrate on something proactive.
notes.Speak with Animals: A ritual that bypasses some skill checks (and is fun), but the game floods you with animal-speaking potions, making the spell mostly redundant.
notes.Witch Bolt: An embarrassing 1d12/turn normally — useless except as the single best spell for the one-shot Lightning Lord build (upcast + maximised + wet + auto-crit).
notes.Animal Friendship: The weakest crowd control — charm-only, on a wisdom save, and it only works in the game's few easy animal fights; just use Command instead.
notes.Arms of Hadar: 2d6 necrotic (strength save half) around a squishy caster that scales terribly on upcast — far too little damage to be worth a slot.
notes.Charm Person: A marginal charm on a wisdom save (enemies have advantage) that's superseded by Friends in dialogue and useless in combat.
notes.Color Spray: A no-save blind capped by enemy HP — it fails on the dangerous high-HP enemies, and Fog Cloud blinds with no HP cap while doing far more.
notes.Compelled Duel: In BG3 it only gives disadvantage on attacks vs others (the 'must attack me' effect isn't implemented) — a bad trade for a paladin's bonus action, slot, and concentration.
notes.False Life: 7 temp HP that doesn't progress your game plan and blocks better temp-HP sources — a waste of a slot.
notes.Goodberry: Summons four weak (1d4) healing berries in a game drowning in better potions; only relevant for infinite-long-rest camp-supply abuse.
notes.Hail of Thorns: A bonus-action 1d10 AOE on a hit — negligible damage, a terrible ratio.
notes.Ray of Sickness: 2d8 of the game's worst damage type (poison) on a bad (con) save with a weak debuff — skip it.
notes.Searing Smite: The worst smite — less up-front damage than Divine Smite and delayed; only ever used free by Zariel tieflings.

subject: Spells — Level 2
context: Baldur's Gate 3 honour mode guide (Patch 8 updated spell tier list)

tier_definitions.S: Critical — take it whenever available and cast it constantly
tier_definitions.A: Great workhorse spells; mainstays of your strategy
tier_definitions.B: Very useful — high impact in the right situations
tier_definitions.C: Marginal — for specific builds or narrow encounters

notes.Aid: All-day, no-concentration +5 (and more on upcast) to MAX HP for the whole party AND every summon within range — multiplies the value of fragile summons and gives huge party-wide survivability; cast it from a camp follower for free.
notes.Cloud of Daggers: No-save, no-roll 4d4 slashing both on cast and at the start of each enemy turn (~8d4 before they act) in a decent AOE — guaranteed damage that just wins fights, great single-target or AOE; pairs with anything that traps enemies in it.
notes.Darkness: An AOE blind that also blocks ranged attacks across it; the core of the broken Darkness + Devil's Sight strategy (your party advantage/ranged-immunity, enemies disadvantage), and it solves any fight with ranged attackers on its own.
notes.Hold Person: Wisdom-save paralysis — the most debilitating condition, granting AUTO-CRITS to every attack within 10ft; locks down humanoids and is THE burst-damage setup, near-unbeatable with high save DC (Arcane Acuity / Band of the Mystic Scoundrel).
notes.Misty Step: A bonus-action 60ft teleport with no opportunity attacks — top-five spell in the game; escape, reposition, reach squishy enemies, dodge AOE. Every honour-mode character should have access (spell or item).
notes.Shadow Blade: Bonus action, an all-day finesse weapon doing 2d8-4d8 psychic with auto-advantage in dim light — more damage than any early weapon, free 4d8 upcast on warlocks, and absolutely broken with the psychic-vulnerability Resonant Stone.
notes.Spike Growth: A huge (40ft across) non-flammable difficult-terrain zone dealing 2d4 per 5ft moved — single-handedly wins most melee Act 1 encounters (put Cloud of Daggers on top), and casters with Land's Stride walk through it freely.
notes.Spiritual Weapon: Bonus-action, no-concentration summon — best understood as free crowd control: it baits attacks (resists all damage), blocks doorways, and chips in damage every turn at almost no opportunity cost. Great early and late.
notes.Flaming Sphere: A durable (resists physical) summon that deals guaranteed fire each turn, soaks several attacks, blocks movement, and rams enemies as a bonus action — tons of accrued value over a fight; excellent until enemies can one-shot it.
notes.Heat Metal: No-save 2d8 fire plus disadvantage-on-attacks or disarm vs any metal-armed/armoured enemy (most dangerous foes), repeatable each turn — very strong despite being buggy (and you can even drop concentration and keep the debuff).
notes.Invisibility: Concentration buff for scouting/positioning, a panic button to save an ally (or the whole party on upcast), and free traversal of hostile areas — doesn't win fights alone but is useful in countless situations; conserves invisibility potions.
notes.Magic Weapon: All-day +1 to +3 (on upcast) attack and damage on a weapon — small but always-relevant, and excellent specifically for characters with nothing else to concentrate on (Eldritch Knight, paladin, arcane trickster, a wizard-dip sword bard).
notes.Moonbeam: The highest average level-2 damage — 2d10 radiant (con save) on cast and each enemy turn, and you can move it to chase enemies; efficient, though radiant-orb/Cull-the-Weak riders don't trigger (it counts as a summon).
notes.Scorching Ray: Three fire beams (2d6 each, attack rolls) splittable across targets — strong non-concentration damage that doubles under Hold Person and is THE engine for fire Arcane Acuity stacking and upcast Scorching Ray builds.
notes.Silence: Ritual (free) 20ft no-cast zone — start a fight by dropping it on enemy casters (and it blocks some honour-mode boss legendary actions); fight-winning when you can keep them inside it, just combine with something that stops movement.
notes.Blindness: A rare non-concentration, 10-turn, ranged debuff (con save) — solid and easy to apply, and it targets Constitution so it works on high-wisdom or incapacitation-immune enemies; only docked because Fog Cloud/Darkness blind with no save.
notes.Blur: Self-only concentration disadvantage-on-attacks-against-you — wizards prefer Mirror Image, but it makes melee Hexblades and (cheap-concentration) Eldritch Knights extremely hard to kill.
notes.Calm Emotions: A big-AOE charm/fear/rage immunity that 'solves' specific puzzle fights (harpies, charmers) and, despite the tooltip, shuts off ANY enemy rage — including some honour-mode boss legendary actions; situational but great where relevant.
